metadata.description: Business rules enriched with entity and relationship definitions

FANNIE_MAE.definition: The government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) that publishes the Selling Guide and purchases or securitizes eligible mortgage loans delivered by approved lenders.
examples: Fannie Mae Selling Guide (Published March 4, 2026)
Fannie Mae QC process for purchased or securitized loans
Fannie Mae remedies including compensatory fees or loan repurchase
business_rules: Only mortgage loans delivered by approved Seller/Servicers may be purchased or securitized under the Guide.
Fannie Mae may review delivered loans and enforce remedies for breaches of requirements or representations and warranties.
Fannie Mae technology and dataset requirements (e.g., UCDP/UAD) can be mandatory for appraisal delivery and validation.

SELLER_SERVICER.definition: A lender approved by Fannie Mae to sell and service residential mortgage loans, subject to ongoing eligibility, contractual obligations, and quality control requirements.
examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations
business_rules: Seller/Servicers must satisfy approval qualification requirements and maintain eligibility over time.
Seller/Servicers must implement and follow a lender quality control program aligned to Fannie Mae requirements.
Seller/Servicers make enforceable representations and warranties when delivering mortgage loans to Fannie Mae.

LENDER_CONTRACT.definition: The contractual agreement governing the obligations, representations, warranties, and remedies applicable to an approved Seller/Servicer’s transactions with Fannie Mae.
examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments
business_rules: Breach of contractual obligations can trigger remedies including repurchase, substitution, compensatory fees, or sanctions.
Contract may define limited waivers of certain representations and warranties for specified underwriting channels (e.g., DU).
Contract can require establishment, maintenance, and examination of mortgage files and related records.

MORTGAGE_LOAN.definition: A residential mortgage loan intended for delivery to, purchase by, or securitization through Fannie Mae, evaluated against eligibility, underwriting, and documentation standards.
examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks
business_rules: Loan eligibility depends on product attributes, borrower eligibility, property eligibility, and documentation requirements.
Certain loan attributes (e.g., ARMs, temporary buydowns) require additional policy compliance and disclosures.
Loans delivered to Fannie Mae are subject to post-delivery QC reviews and enforcement of representations and warranties.

HIGH_BALANCE_MORTGAGE_LOAN.definition: A mortgage loan with an original loan amount exceeding the baseline conforming loan limit but within the permitted high-balance limit for the property’s location and program.
examples: High-balance loan in a designated high-cost county within permitted limits
High-balance loan requiring mortgage insurance due to LTV thresholds
High-balance loan with special delivery limitations and pricing requirements
business_rules: High-balance classification depends on location-based limits and original loan amount.
High-balance loans may have specific pricing, mortgage insurance, special feature codes, and delivery limitations.
Eligibility and underwriting requirements can be more restrictive for high-balance loans than baseline conforming loans.

LOAN_APPLICATION.definition: The structured borrower request for a mortgage loan, including declarations, income/asset information, liabilities, and property/transaction details used for underwriting and eligibility.
examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)
business_rules: Application data must be sufficient to support underwriting, eligibility determination, and documentation requirements.
Underwriting method (manual vs DU) impacts required documentation and validation steps.
Borrower eligibility (e.g., citizenship/residency status, multiple financed properties) must be evaluated based on application data.

BORROWER.definition: The primary applicant who seeks credit and is obligated on the mortgage loan, whose eligibility, credit risk, income, assets, and liabilities are underwritten.
examples: U.S. citizen borrower purchasing an owner-occupied home
Permanent resident borrower applying for a conforming loan
Borrower with multiple financed properties seeking an additional mortgage
business_rules: Borrower eligibility requirements vary based on citizenship/residency status and program rules.
Multiple financed properties can trigger additional eligibility, reserve, or underwriting considerations.
Income and asset sources must be documented and meet acceptability requirements for underwriting.

CO_BORROWER.definition: An additional applicant jointly obligated on the mortgage loan, contributing income/assets and subject to eligibility and underwriting requirements.
examples: Spouse co-borrower contributing qualifying income
Non-occupant co-borrower supporting the application
Co-borrower with separate asset accounts used for down payment
business_rules: Co-borrowers must meet the same core eligibility and documentation standards applicable to obligated parties.
Occupancy intent and relationship can affect underwriting treatment and required documentation.
Combined borrower/co-borrower income and debts drive DTI and other underwriting metrics.

SUBJECT_PROPERTY.definition: The residential real estate collateral securing the mortgage loan, evaluated for eligibility, valuation, and acceptable property/project characteristics.
examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review
business_rules: Property eligibility depends on type (e.g., manufactured housing, PUD, co-op) and specific program requirements.
Disaster impact can trigger additional documentation, inspection, or appraisal requirements.
Collateral valuation must be supported by an acceptable appraisal method and documentation standards.

PUD_UNIT.definition: A property unit located within a Planned Unit Development (PUD) project, subject to PUD-specific eligibility requirements for delivery to Fannie Mae.
examples: Detached PUD unit with HOA dues and common area maintenance
Attached PUD unit in a master-planned community
PUD unit requiring verification of project/unit eligibility
business_rules: Units in PUD projects must satisfy eligibility requirements applicable to the unit and project characteristics.
HOA/assessment obligations may affect underwriting and escrow considerations.
Project classification must be consistent with guide-defined PUD requirements.

COOP_UNIT.definition: A cooperative (co-op) unit where the borrower obtains shares and a proprietary lease, subject to co-op project and share-loan eligibility requirements.
examples: Co-op share loan for an owner-occupied unit
Co-op project requiring legal compliance review
Co-op unit with maintenance fee and underlying mortgage considerations
business_rules: Co-op project eligibility and legal requirements must be satisfied before a co-op share loan is eligible.
Share-loan eligibility rules can differ from standard fee-simple collateral requirements.
Maintenance fees and underlying financing can affect borrower qualification and loan eligibility.

MANUFACTURED_HOME.definition: A manufactured housing collateral type subject to specialized eligibility, underwriting, and documentation requirements under the Guide.
examples: Doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation
Manufactured home with HUD label verification
Manufactured housing loan requiring specific eligibility checks
business_rules: Manufactured housing must meet Guide eligibility requirements related to construction, foundation, and land ownership.
Documentation must evidence compliance with manufactured housing standards (e.g., labeling, foundation).
Appraisal requirements may include additional comparability or marketability considerations for manufactured homes.

APPRAISER.definition: A credentialed valuation professional selected under lender responsibilities and selection criteria to develop an appraisal compliant with Guide requirements.
examples: State-certified residential appraiser engaged for a purchase transaction
Appraiser meeting lender selection criteria and independence requirements
Appraiser rejected due to unacceptable appraisal practice indicators
business_rules: Lender must select appraisers based on Guide selection criteria and ensure appraiser independence.
Unacceptable appraisal practices must be avoided; violations can invalidate the appraisal or trigger remedies.
Information disclosed to appraisers must comply with policy (e.g., avoid undue influence while allowing necessary data).

APPRAISAL_REPORT.definition: The valuation deliverable supporting collateral market value and property condition, produced under documentation standards and subject to appraisal age and use requirements.
examples: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (URAR) with UAD-compliant fields
Appraisal report reused within permitted age and use requirements
Appraisal report flagged for unacceptable practices or missing exhibits
business_rules: Appraisal report must be on an acceptable form and include required exhibits per documentation standards.
Appraisal age and use requirements determine whether an appraisal is acceptable for the transaction date and purpose.
Appraisal data may need to conform to UAD and be successfully submitted through UCDP for delivery.

DESKTOP_APPRAISAL.definition: A valuation product completed without an interior physical inspection, permitted only when specific Guide requirements for desktop appraisals are met.
examples: Desktop appraisal used for a low-risk refinance where permitted
Desktop appraisal completed with required data sources and exhibits
Desktop appraisal rejected because transaction/property type is ineligible
business_rules: Desktop appraisals are only allowed for specific transactions and property scenarios defined by policy.
Required scope, data sources, and documentation must be met to support credibility of value conclusion.
Lender must ensure submission/validation requirements are satisfied when a desktop appraisal is used.

HYBRID_APPRAISAL.definition: A valuation product combining third-party property data collection with an appraiser’s analysis and reconciliation, subject to Guide hybrid appraisal requirements.
examples: Hybrid appraisal with separate property data collector and appraiser analysis
Hybrid appraisal delivered with required data collection documentation
Hybrid appraisal declined due to insufficient data collection scope
business_rules: Hybrid appraisals must meet defined roles, responsibilities, and documentation requirements for data collection and appraiser analysis.
Eligibility for hybrid appraisals can vary by transaction type and collateral characteristics.
Submission standards (e.g., UAD/UCDP where applicable) must be met for delivery and downstream validation.

MARKET_VALUE.definition: The defined concept of market value used for collateral valuation in the appraisal process, reflecting an objective value opinion under stated assumptions and conditions.
examples: Market value concluded as of appraisal effective date for a purchase transaction
Market value estimate based on sales comparison and reconciliation
Market value definition applied per Guide section B4-1.1-01
business_rules: Market value must be determined using the Guide’s definition and consistent assumptions.
Value conclusion must be supported by credible analysis and adequate market data.
Discrepancies between market value and contract price may trigger additional review requirements.

UAD_DATASET.definition: The Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) standardized data fields and formats required for appraisal reporting and electronic submission for certain loans.
examples: UAD-compliant appraisal field coding for condition and quality
UAD standard formatting for comparable sale adjustments
UAD validation errors requiring correction before submission
business_rules: Appraisal data must adhere to UAD formatting and enumerations when required for submission and delivery.
UAD validation failures can require correction or resubmission and may delay loan delivery.
UAD requirements vary by appraisal form/type and delivery channel policies.

UCDP_SUBMISSION.definition: The electronic submission of appraisal data and files to the Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP) for validation and delivery requirements.
examples: UCDP submission with hard-stop errors requiring correction
Successful UCDP submission and acceptance prior to loan delivery
Resubmission after updating UAD field formatting
business_rules: Certain loans require successful UCDP submission/acceptance as a prerequisite for delivery.
Hard-stop messages must be resolved; warnings may require documented lender review.
Submission must match the correct appraisal, property, and loan identifiers to avoid delivery defects.

DESKTOP_UNDERWRITER.definition: Fannie Mae’s automated underwriting system (DU) that evaluates risk factors and produces underwriting recommendations and documentation/validation requirements.
examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation
business_rules: DU recommendations drive required documentation levels and underwriting conditions.
DU evaluates defined risk factors; missing or inconsistent data can change recommendations.
Limited waivers of representations and warranties may depend on DU usage and compliance with DU requirements.

UNDERWRITING_DECISION.definition: The underwriting outcome for a loan application (automated or manual), including approval status, conditions, and documentation requirements.
examples: Approve/Eligible with standard documentation conditions
Approve/Ineligible requiring changes to become eligible
Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting and additional conditions
business_rules: Decision type and conditions must align to underwriting method (DU vs manual) and Guide requirements.
Conditions must be cleared with acceptable documentation before closing/delivery.
Out-of-scope or ineligible decisions require corrective action or loan ineligibility designation.

BORROWER_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE.definition: The structured set of borrower eligibility determinations used to assess compliance with Guide borrower requirements (e.g., citizenship, residency, financed property count).
examples: Non-U.S. citizen borrower profile meeting documented eligibility requirements
Borrower profile flagged due to multiple financed properties
Borrower profile marked ineligible due to unmet documentation or status criteria
business_rules: Borrower eligibility requirements differ for non-U.S. citizens and must be supported by acceptable documentation.
Multiple financed properties can require additional constraints, reserves, or eligibility checks.
Eligibility results must be consistent with underwriting method outputs and Guide rules.

LOAN_ELIGIBILITY_PROFILE.definition: The structured set of loan-level eligibility attributes and determinations (loan limits, legal requirements, attributes like ARMs and escrow policies) for delivery to Fannie Mae.
examples: Loan eligibility profile meeting baseline conforming loan limit requirements
Loan eligibility profile flagged due to ARM feature noncompliance
Loan eligibility profile requiring escrow account per policy
business_rules: Loan amount must not exceed applicable loan limits for the property location and program.
Loan attributes (ARMs, buydowns, curtailments, escrow) must comply with related policies and legal requirements.
Delivery eligibility status must integrate borrower, property, appraisal, and documentation outcomes.

ESCROW_ACCOUNT.definition: A lender-managed account used to collect and disburse funds for property-related obligations (e.g., taxes, insurance) per escrow policies.
examples: Escrow account established for property taxes and homeowners insurance
Escrow waiver granted where permitted and documented
Escrow analysis identifying shortage and updated monthly payment
business_rules: Escrow establishment and waiver must follow product, legal, and investor-specific requirements.
Escrowed items and payment calculations must be consistent with allowable cushion and analysis rules.
Escrow account status and servicing controls can be reviewed in QC and servicing oversight.

ARM_TERMS.definition: The adjustable-rate mortgage (ARM) feature set defining interest rate adjustment mechanics, caps, index, margin, and conversion options where applicable.
examples: 5/6 ARM with periodic and lifetime caps defined
Convertible ARM with conversion feature per product terms
ARM with temporary interest rate buydown applied at origination
business_rules: ARM parameters must comply with product eligibility rules and any special policy requirements.
Temporary interest rate buydowns must be structured and documented per policy.
Convertible features must meet defined requirements and be accurately reflected in disclosures and loan data.

TRUST_ACCOUNT.definition: A trust account used as an asset source in underwriting, subject to verification of ownership, access, and acceptable use for closing/ reserves.
examples: Revocable trust account with documented access for closing funds
Irrevocable trust with restricted distributions affecting eligibility
Trust account counted for reserves based on permitted distribution terms
business_rules: Trust assets must be verified and evaluated for borrower access and permitted use.
Restrictions or lack of control can limit whether funds may be used for closing or reserves.
Documentation must support ownership/beneficiary status and distribution terms.

RETIREMENT_ACCOUNT.definition: A retirement account (e.g., 401(k), IRA) used as an asset source, subject to documentation, vesting, and withdrawal/penalty considerations for allowable use.
examples: 401(k) with vested balance used to meet reserve requirements
IRA funds withdrawn for down payment with documented terms
Retirement account with outstanding loan affecting available funds
business_rules: Only vested and accessible retirement funds may be considered, subject to documentation and withdrawal terms.
Penalties/terms can affect usable amounts for closing and reserves calculations.
Any loans against retirement accounts may reduce available assets and must be considered in underwriting.

PERSONAL_GIFT.definition: Funds provided to the borrower as a personal gift for down payment, closing costs, or reserves, subject to acceptable donor, documentation, and transfer requirements.
examples: Gift from parent used toward down payment on a primary residence
Gift funds transferred via wire with documentation
Gift letter and proof of transfer retained in mortgage file
business_rules: Gift acceptability depends on donor eligibility, relationship, and transaction requirements.
Documentation must support gift letter and evidence of transfer/source as required.
Certain transactions may restrict gift use (e.g., investment properties or minimum borrower contribution rules).

GIFT_OF_EQUITY.definition: A gift where the seller provides equity to the borrower (e.g., below-market sale price), subject to documentation and acceptability criteria.
examples: Seller-parent provides equity gift via below-market sales price
Gift of equity used to satisfy down payment requirement
Equity gift documented in purchase contract and appraisal
business_rules: Gift of equity must be properly documented and reflected in sales contract and valuation support.
Relationship and transaction structure can affect whether equity is eligible and how it is treated in LTV calculations.
Equity gift may be subject to limitations depending on property type and occupancy.

GRANT.definition: A grant provided by a government entity, nonprofit, or other eligible source used for down payment or closing costs, subject to program terms and documentation.
examples: State housing finance agency grant applied to closing costs
Nonprofit down payment assistance grant with recorded terms
Grant structured as a forgivable subordinate lien
business_rules: Grants must be from acceptable providers and comply with program and Guide requirements.
Repayment/forgiveness terms and lien structure must be documented and may affect loan eligibility and CLTV.
Use of grant funds must be consistent with allowable purposes and properly evidenced in the mortgage file.

LENDER_CONTRIBUTION.definition: Funds contributed by the lender (or through an approved mechanism) toward borrower closing costs or other allowable amounts, subject to limits and documentation.
examples: Lender credit applied to borrower closing costs
Lender contribution structured as pricing credit (rate/points tradeoff)
Contribution limited by transaction caps and documented on closing disclosure
business_rules: Lender contributions may be capped based on transaction characteristics and must be properly disclosed.
Pricing credits and contributions must be consistent with product and investor rules and not circumvent eligibility constraints.
Documentation must clearly evidence the contribution source and application at closing.

DISASTER_RELIEF_GRANT_OR_LOAN.definition: Disaster-related assistance provided to the borrower (grant or loan) used as funds for closing or recovery, subject to documentation and acceptability rules.
examples: Disaster relief grant used to replenish savings for reserves
Disaster relief loan with repayment terms impacting DTI
Assistance tied to a declared disaster affecting the subject property area
business_rules: Disaster relief assistance must be documented and evaluated for repayment obligations and impact on qualifying ratios.
Assistance acceptability may depend on provider type and program terms.
Disaster-related property impacts may trigger additional collateral and underwriting requirements beyond the assistance itself.

EARNEST_MONEY_DEPOSIT.definition: Buyer funds deposited to demonstrate good faith under a purchase contract, which may be credited toward closing, subject to verification and source requirements.
examples: Earnest money deposit paid by check and verified as cleared
EMD held by title company and credited toward closing costs
EMD sourced from borrower bank account with verification
business_rules: Earnest money deposits must be verified for amount, source, and clearing as required.
Unverified or unacceptable sources can trigger additional documentation or disallowance toward required funds to close.
Holder and crediting mechanics must align with purchase contract and closing documentation.

BRIDGE_SWING_LOAN.definition: A short-term loan used to finance a borrower’s current transaction pending sale of another property, subject to underwriting and documentation requirements.
examples: Bridge loan used to fund down payment before sale of existing home
Swing loan with monthly payment included in DTI until payoff
Bridge loan secured by departing residence with documented terms
business_rules: Bridge/swing loans must be documented and treated appropriately as a liability in underwriting where applicable.
Expected payoff source (e.g., sales proceeds) may require evidence and timing alignment.
Collateral and repayment terms can affect eligibility, reserve requirements, and DTI calculations.

QUALITY_CONTROL_PROGRAM.definition: The Seller/Servicer’s quality control (QC) program consisting of policies, sampling, audits, and corrective actions designed to ensure loans meet eligibility and underwriting requirements.
examples: Lender QC program performing pre-funding and post-closing audits
QC program with documented defect categories and corrective action tracking
QC program updated to reflect new appraisal or DU policy changes
business_rules: Seller/Servicers must maintain a QC program that meets Fannie Mae requirements and is consistently executed.
QC findings must drive corrective actions and may be reviewed by Fannie Mae.
QC scope must cover underwriting, appraisal, documentation, and delivery data integrity.

QC_REVIEW.definition: A quality control review performed by the Seller/Servicer and/or Fannie Mae to confirm that delivered loans meet eligibility, underwriting, and documentation requirements.
examples: Fannie Mae post-purchase QC review identifying an appraisal documentation defect
Lender post-closing QC audit sampling loans and documenting findings
QC review resulting in remediation and updated mortgage file records
business_rules: QC reviews may be required both by the lender (internal QC) and by Fannie Mae (investor QC).
Identified defects must be categorized, remediated where possible, and tracked to resolution.
Material defects can lead to remedies including repurchase, make-whole payments, fees, or sanctions.

MORTGAGE_FILE.definition: The complete loan file containing the required records, disclosures, underwriting documentation, appraisal, and evidence of compliance for a mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae.
examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings
business_rules: Mortgage files must be established, maintained, retained, and made available for examination per contractual requirements.
File completeness and audit trail support defensibility of eligibility and underwriting determinations.
Missing or inconsistent documents can create delivery defects and trigger remedies.

REPRESENTATION_AND_WARRANTY.definition: A binding assurance made by the Seller/Servicer about loan quality, data accuracy, underwriting compliance, and documentation at the time of delivery to Fannie Mae.
examples: Representation that underwriting complied with eligibility and documentation requirements
Warranty that loan data delivered matches the mortgage file
Limited waiver applied for DU-underwritten loans under specified conditions
business_rules: Representations and warranties attach to delivered loans and may be enforced when defects are found.
Limited waivers may apply only if underwriting channel and conditions are met (e.g., DU with required compliance).
Breach of representations and warranties can lead to repurchase, make-whole payments, fees, or sanctions.

LOAN_REPURCHASE_REQUEST.definition: A formal demand by Fannie Mae for the Seller/Servicer to repurchase a mortgage loan or otherwise remedy a material breach or defect under contractual remedies.
examples: Repurchase request issued due to ineligible borrower status documentation
Repurchase request based on unacceptable appraisal practice findings
Make-whole payment request issued as an alternative remedy
business_rules: Repurchase/make-whole remedies are driven by defect severity and contractual remedy provisions.
Seller/Servicer may have defined processes/timelines for cure, appeal, or alternative remedy outcomes.
Repurchase triggers can include eligibility failures, documentation gaps, data misrepresentation, or appraisal defects.

APPROVES_SELLER_SERVICER.definition: Fannie Mae grants approval status to a lender to act as an eligible Seller/Servicer, enabling delivery and servicing activities under the Guide and contract.
examples: Fannie Mae approves a lender after completing the approval qualification process.
Fannie Mae changes a Seller/Servicer’s status to terminated following a breach.
business_rules: Approval requires satisfaction of eligibility and operational qualification criteria.
Approval status must be maintained; loss of eligibility can restrict or end delivery/servicing authority.
Approval decisions can be subject to ongoing monitoring and periodic review.

GOVERNS_RELATIONSHIP_WITH.definition: The lender contract establishes the obligations and enforceable terms that apply to the Seller/Servicer in its transactions with Fannie Mae.
examples: Contract requires record retention and examination of mortgage files for all delivered loans.
Contract defines remedies available to Fannie Mae for material defects.
business_rules: Contract provisions define representations and warranties and the consequences of breach.
Contract can specify termination rights and sanctions for noncompliance.
Contract may set conditions for limited waivers tied to underwriting channels.

ORIGINATES_MORTGAGE_LOAN.definition: The Seller/Servicer originates (or delivers as the responsible party) a mortgage loan that will be evaluated for eligibility and delivery to Fannie Mae.
examples: Approved Seller/Servicer originates a fixed-rate purchase loan intended for delivery.
Seller/Servicer originates an ARM subject to ARM policy requirements.
business_rules: Originated loans must meet eligibility, underwriting, appraisal, and documentation standards prior to delivery.
Loan attributes determine which additional policies apply (e.g., ARM, escrow).
Originator remains responsible for loan quality and data integrity under representations and warranties.

SUBMITS_LOAN_APPLICATION.definition: A borrower submits a loan application containing the data used to evaluate eligibility and underwriting requirements.
examples: Borrower submits a purchase application listing income, assets, and liabilities.
Borrower submits a refinance application requiring an updated appraisal.
business_rules: Application must contain sufficient information to support underwriting and compliance determinations.
Changes to application data may require resubmission to DU or re-underwriting.
Borrower declarations (occupancy, financed properties) must be evaluated for eligibility impact.

JOINS_APPLICATION_AS.definition: A co-borrower is included on a loan application as an additional obligated party contributing income/assets and subject to underwriting.
examples: Co-borrower is added to support qualifying income for DTI.
Non-occupant co-borrower joins the application to strengthen credit profile.
business_rules: All obligated parties must meet applicable eligibility and documentation requirements.
Co-borrower occupancy intent and relationship can affect underwriting treatment.
Combined liabilities and income determine key ratios and eligibility outcomes.

REQUESTS_MORTGAGE_LOAN.definition: A loan application requests a specific mortgage loan product and terms that will be underwritten and evaluated for eligibility.
examples: Application requests a 30-year fixed-rate loan amount of $450,000.
Application requests an ARM with a specified initial fixed period.
business_rules: Requested terms must satisfy loan limits, product rules, and legal requirements.
If requested terms change (loan amount, product type), eligibility and underwriting may need to be recalculated.
Product selection can drive required appraisal type and documentation level.

IS_SECURED_BY.definition: A mortgage loan is collateralized by the subject property whose eligibility and market value support the credit decision.
examples: Purchase loan secured by a one-unit primary residence.
Refinance loan secured by a property in a disaster-affected area requiring additional review.
business_rules: Property type and project characteristics can change eligibility requirements and permissible appraisal methods.
Collateral valuation must be supported by an acceptable appraisal compliant with documentation standards.
Disaster impact can trigger extra inspection/appraisal or documentation requirements.

CLASSIFIES_AS_PUD_UNIT.definition: The subject property is classified as a PUD unit, making PUD-specific eligibility requirements applicable.
examples: Subject property identified as a detached PUD unit with HOA dues.
Subject property identified as an attached PUD unit in a planned development.
business_rules: Correct classification is required to apply the right eligibility and documentation rules.
PUD-related obligations (e.g., HOA dues) may affect underwriting and escrow decisions.
Project/unit eligibility must be validated when PUD classification applies.

CLASSIFIES_AS_COOP_UNIT.definition: The subject property is classified as a co-op unit, making co-op project and share-loan eligibility requirements applicable.
examples: Subject property identified as a cooperative unit requiring project legal review.
Loan structured as a co-op share loan with proprietary lease documentation.
business_rules: Co-op legal and project eligibility must be met for delivery eligibility.
Share-loan requirements differ from fee-simple collateral and must be applied correctly.
Maintenance fees and underlying financing must be considered in qualification and eligibility.

CLASSIFIES_AS_MANUFACTURED_HOME.definition: The subject property is identified as manufactured housing, triggering manufactured housing-specific eligibility and documentation rules.
examples: Subject property identified as a doublewide manufactured home on a permanent foundation.
Subject property identified as manufactured housing requiring HUD label verification.
business_rules: Manufactured housing eligibility depends on construction, foundation, and land ownership criteria.
Additional documentation may be required to evidence compliance with manufactured housing standards.
Valuation may require careful comparable selection and marketability analysis.

ENGAGES_APPRAISER.definition: The Seller/Servicer selects and engages an appraiser under defined selection criteria and independence requirements.
examples: Lender selects an appraiser from an approved panel meeting independence standards.
Lender reassigns an appraiser due to credential or independence concerns.
business_rules: Appraiser selection must meet eligibility criteria and independence requirements.
Unacceptable practices or conflicts of interest can disqualify an appraiser or appraisal.
Lender is responsible for ensuring proper engagement and permitted information disclosure.

PRODUCES_APPRAISAL_REPORT.definition: An appraiser completes and signs an appraisal report documenting the analysis supporting the value conclusion and required exhibits.
examples: Appraiser produces a UAD-compliant URAR with required exhibits.
Appraiser produces an appraisal report with comparable sales and reconciliation.
business_rules: Appraisal report must comply with acceptable forms, exhibits, and documentation standards.
Reports can be rejected if unacceptable appraisal practices are identified.
Appraisal report dates must support appraisal age and use requirements.

ESTIMATES_MARKET_VALUE.definition: The appraisal report provides an estimate of market value as of the effective date using the applicable market value definition and assumptions.
examples: Appraisal report concludes market value of $525,000 as of the effective date.
Appraisal report applies the Guide’s market value definition and assumptions.
business_rules: Market value must use the Guide-defined framework and be supported by credible analysis.
Value conclusion must be consistent with documented comparables and reconciliation logic.
Significant variances from contract price or prior values may require additional review.

IS_DESKTOP_APPRAISAL_TYPE.definition: An appraisal report is categorized as a desktop appraisal, indicating the inspection scope and desktop-specific policy requirements apply.
examples: Appraisal report labeled as desktop appraisal for an eligible transaction.
Desktop appraisal identified and reviewed for required data sources and scope.
business_rules: Desktop appraisal usage is restricted to policy-permitted scenarios.
Desktop appraisal scope and documentation must meet specific requirements.
If ineligible for desktop use, an alternative appraisal type may be required.

IS_HYBRID_APPRAISAL_TYPE.definition: An appraisal report is categorized as a hybrid appraisal, indicating data collection and appraiser analysis roles are split and hybrid requirements apply.
examples: Hybrid appraisal includes third-party data collection with appraiser reconciliation.
Hybrid appraisal reviewed for compliance with hybrid documentation requirements.
business_rules: Hybrid appraisals must meet role separation, documentation, and quality requirements.
Hybrid eligibility depends on transaction and property characteristics.
Submission/validation requirements must still be satisfied for delivery when applicable.

SUBMITS_TO_UCDP.definition: The Seller/Servicer submits appraisal data/files to UCDP to obtain validation results required for delivery and compliance.
examples: Lender submits appraisal file to UCDP and receives hard-stop messages.
Lender resubmits corrected appraisal after resolving UAD formatting errors.
business_rules: Successful UCDP submission may be required for loan delivery depending on policy and loan characteristics.
Hard-stop messages must be resolved; warnings may require documented review and acceptance.
Submission identifiers must align with loan/appraisal records to prevent delivery defects.

INCLUDES_UAD_DATASET.definition: A UCDP submission includes standardized UAD data elements that must pass formatting and content validation rules.
examples: UCDP submission includes UAD-coded condition and quality fields.
UCDP submission includes UAD-formatted comparable sale entries.
business_rules: UAD formatting and enumerations must be correct to pass validation.
UAD compliance requirements vary based on appraisal form and policy requirements.
Validation errors must be corrected to avoid delivery delays or defects.

EVALUATES_IN_DU.definition: Desktop Underwriter evaluates the loan application’s data to assess risk factors and determine underwriting recommendations and documentation requirements.
examples: DU evaluates an application and generates an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
DU evaluates an application and returns Refer with Caution requiring manual underwriting.
business_rules: DU outcomes depend on accuracy and completeness of submitted loan/application data.
DU documentation requirements must be satisfied to support delivery eligibility.
DU validation services can change documentation needs and may affect representation and warranty exposure.

GENERATES_UNDERWRITING_DECISION.definition: Desktop Underwriter produces an underwriting decision/recommendation record, including conditions and documentation requirements.
examples: DU generates Approve/Eligible decision with standard documentation conditions.
DU generates Approve/Ineligible decision requiring changes to achieve eligibility.
business_rules: Decision types map to defined categories (Approve/Eligible, Approve/Ineligible, Refer with Caution, Out of Scope).
Conditions must be tracked and cleared prior to closing/delivery.
Decision validity can expire or require refresh if material loan data changes.

DETERMINES_BORROWER_ELIGIBILITY.definition: Loan application data is used to create a borrower eligibility profile capturing citizenship/residency and financed property considerations affecting eligibility.
examples: Application indicates non-U.S. citizen status, triggering specific eligibility documentation requirements.
Application indicates multiple financed properties, triggering additional eligibility and reserve considerations.
business_rules: Borrower eligibility must be explicitly assessed and supported by documentation in the mortgage file.
Non-U.S. citizen requirements vary by status category and must be correctly applied.
Multiple financed property rules can restrict eligibility or add underwriting constraints.

DETERMINES_LOAN_ELIGIBILITY.definition: Loan attributes and policy checks produce a loan eligibility profile capturing loan limits, product attributes (e.g., ARM), escrow, and legal compliance status for delivery.
examples: Loan eligibility profile shows high-balance indicator true based on county limits.
Loan eligibility profile flags escrow required per policy and loan characteristics.
business_rules: Loan limits must be evaluated using location-based thresholds and program definitions (including high-balance rules).
Loan attribute policies (ARM, buydown, curtailments) must be checked and documented.
Legal compliance status can be a prerequisite to delivery eligibility.

REQUIRES_ESCROW_ACCOUNT.definition: A mortgage loan requires an escrow account when escrow policies and loan characteristics dictate collection and disbursement of property-related obligations.
examples: Loan requires escrow for property taxes and homeowners insurance.
Loan includes an escrow waiver only where permitted and properly documented.
business_rules: Escrow requirement/waiver must comply with applicable policy and legal requirements.
Escrow items and calculations must be supported by acceptable documentation and servicing controls.
Escrow setup and maintenance may be evaluated in QC reviews.

HAS_ARM_TERMS.definition: A mortgage loan includes ARM terms that define how interest rates adjust over time, including index, margin, caps, and frequency.
examples: ARM loan references index and margin and defines periodic and lifetime caps.
Convertible ARM includes conversion indicator and related parameters.
business_rules: ARM term parameters must comply with product rules and be consistent across loan data and disclosures.
Temporary buydowns must be structured in accordance with policy and not conflict with ARM mechanics.
ARM-specific eligibility checks may affect delivery acceptance.

USES_DOWN_PAYMENT_SOURCE.definition: A mortgage loan transaction uses personal gift funds as part of the funds to close, down payment, or reserves, subject to gift documentation requirements.
examples: Loan uses a $15,000 gift from a parent for down payment.
Loan uses documented gift funds transferred prior to closing.
business_rules: Gift eligibility and documentation rules must be satisfied before counting funds toward required borrower contribution.
Source/transfer evidence must be retained in the mortgage file per documentation standards.
Some transactions may limit gift usage depending on occupancy or property type.

USES_GIFT_OF_EQUITY.definition: A mortgage loan transaction uses a gift of equity from the seller, affecting sales price, LTV calculation, and required documentation.
examples: Loan uses gift of equity documented in purchase contract and supported by appraisal.
Loan uses equity gift from a related-party seller with required documentation.
business_rules: Equity gift must be documented and accurately reflected in the contract and valuation to support LTV calculations.
Related-party transactions can require heightened scrutiny and documentation.
Equity gift acceptability can vary by occupancy and property type.

USES_GRANT_ASSISTANCE.definition: A mortgage loan uses grant assistance for down payment or closing costs, subject to provider eligibility and program terms affecting liens and CLTV.
examples: Loan uses a state agency grant for closing costs with documented terms.
Loan uses nonprofit down payment assistance structured as a forgivable lien.
business_rules: Grant provider and terms must be acceptable and properly documented.
Subordinate lien or repayment/forgiveness terms can affect eligibility and CLTV calculation.
Grant disbursement and application must be evidenced in closing documentation.

USES_LENDER_CONTRIBUTION.definition: A mortgage loan uses lender contributions (credits) applied to closing costs or other allowable items, subject to caps and disclosure requirements.
examples: Loan includes a lender credit due to pricing choice to reduce borrower cash to close.
Loan includes lender contribution limited by applicable caps and shown in closing documentation.
business_rules: Lender contributions must not exceed permitted amounts and must be accurately disclosed.
Credits must not be used to circumvent eligibility or minimum contribution requirements.
Contribution structure must align with pricing and loan terms.

USES_EARNEST_MONEY_DEPOSIT.definition: A mortgage loan purchase transaction applies an earnest money deposit toward funds to close, requiring verification of amount, source, and clearing.
examples: Loan credits EMD held by title company toward borrower funds to close.
Loan uses EMD paid from borrower account with documented clearing.
business_rules: EMD must be verified and sourced per requirements before it can be counted toward funds to close.
Unverified EMD may require additional borrower funds or documentation.
EMD treatment must be consistent between the purchase contract and closing records.

USES_BRIDGE_SWING_LOAN.definition: A mortgage loan relies on a bridge/swing loan to facilitate closing, requiring evaluation of repayment terms and impact on liabilities/DTI.
examples: Loan uses a bridge loan secured by departing residence until that property sells.
Loan includes swing loan monthly payment in DTI until payoff.
business_rules: Bridge loan terms and repayment obligations must be documented and included in underwriting where applicable.
Expected payoff via sale proceeds may require evidence and timing consistency.
Bridge loan can affect reserves and DTI calculations and may be constrained by eligibility rules.

MAINTAINS_QC_PROGRAM.definition: The Seller/Servicer maintains a quality control program to monitor and ensure loan compliance with Fannie Mae eligibility and underwriting requirements.
examples: Seller/Servicer maintains a QC program with post-closing sampling and defect tracking.
Seller/Servicer updates QC procedures following policy changes in appraisal requirements.
business_rules: QC program must meet defined requirements for scope, frequency, and corrective actions.
QC program execution must be documented and auditable.
Program deficiencies can be considered a compliance risk and trigger further oversight.

CONDUCTS_QC_REVIEW.definition: Fannie Mae conducts quality control reviews of purchased or securitized loans to confirm compliance and determine remedies when defects are found.
examples: Fannie Mae performs a post-purchase QC review and identifies an underwriting documentation defect.
Fannie Mae performs a review focused on appraisal compliance and UCDP submission results.
business_rules: Fannie Mae QC reviews can select loans based on risk, sampling, or targeted concerns.
Material defects identified can lead to remedies under contract and representations/warranties.
QC findings require traceability to mortgage file evidence and delivery data.

EXAMINES_MORTGAGE_FILE.definition: A QC review examines the mortgage file for required documentation, consistency with delivered data, and compliance with underwriting and appraisal standards.
examples: QC review examines file for required appraisal exhibits and UCDP acceptance.
QC review checks file for borrower eligibility documentation and DU findings.
business_rules: Mortgage file must contain required documents to support eligibility and underwriting decisions.
Inconsistencies between file documents and delivered data can be treated as defects.
File retention and availability must meet contractual requirements for examination.

ATTACHES_REPRESENTATION_AND_WARRANTY.definition: The Seller/Servicer makes representations and warranties that attach to delivered loans, covering compliance, data accuracy, and documentation.
examples: Seller/Servicer makes underwriting compliance representations for each delivered loan.
Seller/Servicer relies on limited waiver conditions for certain warranties when DU is used properly.
business_rules: Representations and warranties apply per delivered loan and are enforceable upon breach.
Limited waivers depend on satisfying required conditions and documentation standards.
Breach outcomes and cure/appeal processes are governed by the lender contract and review findings.

APPLIES_TO_LOAN.definition: A representation and warranty applies to a specific mortgage loan delivered to Fannie Mae, defining the assurance scope for that loan.
examples: Data accuracy warranty applies to delivered loan data fields for a specific mortgage loan.
Underwriting compliance representation applies to the loan’s eligibility and documentation.
business_rules: Different categories of representations and warranties may apply based on delivery channel and underwriting method.
Waiver indicators must be evidenced by satisfying prerequisites (e.g., DU conditions).
Defects tied to applicable representations/warranties can drive remedy selection.

ISSUES_REPURCHASE_REQUEST.definition: Fannie Mae issues a repurchase (or make-whole) request as a remedy after identifying material defects or breaches related to a loan.
examples: Fannie Mae issues repurchase request due to ineligible loan attribute discovered in QC.
Fannie Mae issues make-whole request based on appraisal-related defect findings.
business_rules: Repurchase/make-whole requests must be supported by documented defect basis and contractual remedy authority.
Requests may include cure deadlines and defined appeal processes.
Severity and nature of defect influence whether repurchase, substitution, fees, or sanctions are pursued.

REFERENCES_LOAN.definition: A repurchase request references the specific mortgage loan subject to the requested remedy action.
examples: Repurchase request references loan number and details of the defect basis.
Make-whole request references loan delivered date and QC findings.
business_rules: Repurchase request must unambiguously identify the affected loan and defect basis.
Status tracking and resolution outcomes must be auditable and linkable to QC findings and mortgage file evidence.
Resolution can include cure, repurchase, substitution, make-whole, or other contract-defined remedy.

business_rules.rule_name: Trading margin call threshold $3,000,000 and minimum $50,000
business_rules.description: For MBS trading account margin calls based on price differential, both Fannie Mae’s and the lender’s designated threshold amount are $3,000,000 and both parties’ minimum transfer amount is $50,000, unless otherwise agreed in writing or modified due to a triggering event.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating price differential margin calls for MBS trading account
business_rules.consequences: If below threshold/min transfer, no margin call; if met, margin call allowed
business_rules.exceptions: May differ by written agreement or triggering event modification

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-01 (10/06/2021), Designated Threshold Amount and Minimum Transfer Amount
source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae’s designated threshold amount and a lender’s designated threshold amount shall each be $3,000,000, unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing and/or subject to the occurrence of a triggering event as discussed below. ... Fannie Mae’s minimum transfer amount and a lender’s minimum transfer amount shall each be $50,000, unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in writing and/or subject to the occurrence of a triggering event as discussed below.

examples: Price differential = $3,100,000 in lender’s favor; lender may call for $100,000 ($3.1M-$3.0M).
Price differential = $3,040,000; amount above threshold is $40,000 (<$50,000), so margin call not allowed.
Parties execute written amendment setting threshold to $1,000,000; margin call eligibility follows amended terms.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Trading margin call / collateral demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Price differential due same day if notice by 10:00 a.m.
business_rules.description: When Fannie Mae provides notice to satisfy the price differential on the lender’s trades with the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk, the deadline depends on when notice is sent: if notice is sent by 10:00 a.m. on a business day, the lender must satisfy the price differential by close of business that same business day; if notice is sent after 10:00 a.m. on a business day, the lender must satisfy by 12:00 noon on the next business day.
business_rules.conditions: When Fannie Mae sends notice to satisfy a trading account price differential
business_rules.consequences: Failure to meet deadline constitutes nonperformance and may trigger default remedies
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-01 (10/06/2021), Default of Lender Under the Terms of Trading Account
source_reference.source_text: Instances of nonperformance that constitute a default include the failure to: ... if notice is sent by Fannie Mae by 10:00 a.m. on a business day, satisfy the price diﬀerential on lender’s trades with the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk by the close of business on that same business day; or if notice is sent by Fannie Mae after 10:00 a.m. on a business day, satisfy the price diﬀerential on lender’s trades with the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk by 12:00 noon on the next business day.

examples: Notice sent 9:45 a.m. Tuesday; lender must wire/settle by COB Tuesday.
Notice sent 10:30 a.m. Wednesday; lender must satisfy by 12:00 noon Thursday.
Notice sent 9:30 a.m. Friday before a Monday holiday; lender must satisfy by COB Friday (business day).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Default declaration; accelerate settlement; UCC secured party remedies

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Pass-through rate ≥8% limits pools to 3, 4, or 5 per $1M
business_rules.description: For MBS trades with pass-through rate ≥ 8%, the maximum number of pools permitted depends on original trade amount: ≤ $500,000 allows 3 pools; > $500,000 and < $1,000,000 allows 4 pools; and ≥ $1,000,000 allows 5 pools per each million dollars of trade amount.
business_rules.conditions: When structuring MBS pools for trades with pass-through rate ≥ 8%
business_rules.consequences: If pool count exceeds limit, delivery may be rejected or require restructuring
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-04 (05/05/2021), MBS Pass-Through Rate / Original Trade Amount
source_reference.source_text:
MBS Pass-Through Rate Original Trade Amount Maximum Number of Pools 
≥ 8%≤ $500,000 3
> $500,000 and < $1,000,000 4
≥ $1,000,0005 pools per each million dollars of
the trade amount

examples: Trade amount $450,000 at 8.125%: maximum 3 pools.
Trade amount $750,000 at 8.000%: maximum 4 pools.
Trade amount $2,400,000 at 8.50%: maximum 10 pools (5 per $1M; apply to each million).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection / required correction

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Fannie Majors must be certified by 6:00 p.m. ET 4th-to-last day
business_rules.description: All Fannie Major deliveries must be error-free and certified by 6:00 p.m. Eastern time on the fourth to last business day before the end of the month.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering into Fannie Majors MBS pools for a given month-end cycle
business_rules.consequences: If not certified by deadline, delivery may miss issue month/settlement eligibility
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: A lender may submit data for mortgages in the same Fannie Majors at diﬀerent times and on diﬀerent days of the month, as long as all of the mortgages submitted are the same product type and have the same remittance cycle and servicer. All Fannie Major deliveries must be error-free and certiﬁed by 6:00 p.m. Eastern time on the fourth to last business day before the end of the month.

examples: Fourth-to-last business day is Fri 26th; certification must be completed by 6:00 p.m. ET on the 26th.
Submission on the 26th at 6:05 p.m. ET is late; lender may need to roll delivery to next issue month.
If errors remain at 5:55 p.m. ET, lender must correct and certify before 6:00 p.m. ET to meet requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery delay / missed securitization window

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Weighted-average guaranty fee factor rounded 0.01 bp
business_rules.description: To determine the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for an MBS pool (in basis points), the lender multiplies each loan’s issue-date principal balance (or scheduled balance in later months) by the applicable guaranty fee rate (in basis points, adjusted for remittance cycle adjustments and any buyup/buydown), sums the products, divides by the aggregate pool balance (issue-date principal balance or scheduled security balance), and rounds the result to the nearest hundredth of a basis point.
business_rules.conditions: When calculating annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for an MBS pool
business_rules.consequences: Correct factor supports accurate remittance; miscalc may cause remittance shortfall/adjustment
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-3-01, Determining the Annualized Weighted-Average Guaranty Fee Factor for a Pool
source_reference.source_text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for a pool (expressed in basis points) is determined by multiplying the issue date principal balance of each mortgage loan in the pool (or, in subsequent months, the “scheduled” balance of each mortgage loan) by the applicable guaranty fee rate for the mortgage loan expressed in basis points (adjusted for any remittance cycle adjustments, buyup, or buydown, if applicable), then: summing the products of all the mortgage loans in the pool, dividing that sum by the aggregate issue date principal balance for the pool (or, in subsequent months, by the “scheduled” security balance for the pool), and rounding the result to the nearest hundredth of a basis point.

examples: Pool has two loans: $200,000 at 35 bps and $300,000 at 40 bps; factor = ((200,000*35)+(300,000*40))/500,000 = 38 bps (then round to 0.01 bp).
If a loan’s guaranty fee is adjusted due to a buydown, use the adjusted fee rate (in bps) in the multiplication step.
In a subsequent month, use each loan’s scheduled balance and the pool’s scheduled security balance instead of issue-date balances.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee draft/adjustment

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Monthly guaranty fee remittance divide by 120,000
business_rules.description: To determine the monthly guaranty fee remittance amount for an MBS pool, the lender multiplies the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor by the pool balance (aggregate issue-date principal balance or scheduled security balance in later months), divides by 120,000, and rounds the result to two decimal places.
business_rules.conditions: When computing the monthly guaranty fee remittance amount for an MBS pool
business_rules.consequences: Correct calculation ensures proper monthly remittance; errors can create drafting discrepancies
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C3-3-01, Determining the Monthly Guaranty Fee Remittance for the Pool
source_reference.source_text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor is then multiplied by the aggregate issue date principal balance of the pool (or, in subsequent months, the “scheduled” security balance for the pool), divided by 120,000, and rounded to two decimal places. The result is monthly guaranty fee remittance for the pool.

examples: Annualized factor 38.25 bps and pool UPB $50,000,000: remittance = 38.25*50,000,000/120,000 = $15,937.50 (rounded to 2 decimals).
If using scheduled security balance $49,800,000 in month 2, use that balance in the same formula.
If the calculation yields $15,937.505, round to $15,937.51.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee draft/adjustment

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Guaranty fee drafted on 7th day or prior business day
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae drafts monthly guaranty fee remittances on the 7th day of each month; if the 7th is a holiday or weekend, the draft occurs on the preceding business day. The lender must manage its designated custodial bank draft account so the draft can occur as scheduled.
business_rules.conditions: When Fannie Mae drafts guaranty fee remittances from lender’s designated draft account
business_rules.consequences: Sufficient funds enables successful drafting; insufficient funds can trigger collection/escalation
business_rules.exceptions: If 7th is holiday/weekend, draft on preceding business day

source_reference.section_id: C3-3-01, Remitting Guaranty Fees to Fannie Mae
source_reference.source_text: Remitting Guaranty Fees to Fannie Mae On the 7th day of each month (or, if the 7th is a holiday or weekend, on the preceding business day), Fannie Mae will draft guaranty fee remittances from the custodial bank account the lender designates as its draft account.

examples: If the 7th falls on a Sunday, Fannie Mae drafts on Friday the 5th; lender must fund the draft account by that Friday.
If the lender changes its designated draft account, it must ensure the new account is able to be drafted on the next 7th-day cycle.
If the pool has no collection activity, the guaranty fee still drafts on the scheduled date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee draft/collection action

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: CPM Eligibility Impact Notice Due Within 5 Business Days
business_rules.description: If the lender becomes aware of information that could impact the project eligibility status reflected in CPM (e.g., significant deferred maintenance or major litigation), it must notify the CPM Management team as soon as practicable but no later than five business days after becoming aware. Before selling a loan secured by a unit in such a project, the lender must confirm the project retains its approved status as of the note date unless the loan received and retains the CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender learns new project info affecting CPM eligibility status for an approved project.
business_rules.consequences: If met: CPM can be re-evaluated; sale may proceed if status confirmed. If violated: delivery risk and remedies.
business_rules.exceptions: Confirm-as-of-note-date step not required if DU retains CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message.

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.2-02, Additional Obligations of the Lender for Projects Approved by Fannie Mae
source_reference.source_text: If a lender becomes aware of any information that could impact the eligibility status reflected in CPM (such as, significant deferred maintenance or major litigation), the lender must notify the CPM Management team (see E-1-02, List of Contacts) with the relevant data and information. Fannie Mae will evaluate the new information and its impact on eligibility. Before selling a loan secured by a unit in such a project, the lender must confirm that the project retains its approved status as of the note date, unless a loan has received and retains a CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU. Notification must occur as soon as practicable but no later than five business days after becoming aware of such information.

examples: On Monday, lender learns of newly filed major HOA litigation; lender must notify CPM Management by the following Monday (5 business days).
Lender discovers significant deferred maintenance on an approved project; before selling a loan with note date next week, lender confirms project still approved as of note date.

Loan retains DU message 'project has an Approved by Fannie Mae status in CPM'; lender is not required to re-confirm status as of note date, but still must follow delivery restrictions and other obligations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility / potential repurchase or remedies if defect later identified

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Self-report Potential Breaches Within 60 Days and 500 Loans or 1% Threshold
business_rules.description: For repeated potential breaches related to compliance with laws, notification is required within 60 days of the later of (i) the end of the calendar quarter in which the loans were delivered, or (ii) discovery of the potential breach, and reporting must be done using Loan Quality Connect self-report functionality. If the calculation results in fewer than five potentially affected loans, notification is not required. For Reporting Category 2, reporting requirements apply when the breach could warrant repurchase, or when the number of loans affected by the same type of potential breach delivered in the same calendar quarter does not exceed the lesser of 500 loans or 1% of prior-year deliveries; if such breach could warrant repurchase and is not remedied (or will not be remedied) within 60 days, the seller/servicer must notify Fannie Mae within 60 days; if remedied (or will be remedied) within 60 days, notification is not required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when seller/servicer identifies potential legal compliance breach affecting delivered loans.
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met: must self-report timely. If violated: increased repurchase/remedy and governance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: No notice required if fewer than 5 affected loans; or if repurchase-warranting breach is remedied within 60 days.

source_reference.section_id: A3-2-01, Reporting Category 2 (partial) and timing rule
source_reference.source_text: The notification is required within 60 days of the later of: the end of the calendar quarter in which such loans were delivered, or discovery of the potential breach. The seller/servicer must report these loans using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect. Note: If the calculation described above results in fewer than five potentially affected loans, then the seller/servicer does not need to notify Fannie Mae. Reporting Category 2 The following reporting requirements apply when: the potential breach could warrant a repurchase demand, or the number of loans affected by the same type of potential breach delivered in the same calendar quarter does not exceed the lesser of 500 loans or 1% of the total number of loans delivered to Fannie Mae in the prior year.

examples: Lender discovers an ECOA valuation notice issue on Aug 15 affecting 200 loans delivered in Q1; because <500 and <1% (if 70,000 prior-year deliveries), reporting may not be required under Category 2 thresholds, but lender must still evaluate whether repurchase could be warranted.
A repurchase-warranting legal breach is identified on May 1 and cannot be remedied by June 30; lender must notify via Loan Quality Connect within 60 days of May 1 (by June 30).
A potential breach impacts 3 loans; because fewer than 5 potentially affected loans, lender does not need to notify Fannie Mae per the note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand / indemnification risk (if breach is material)

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: OFAC Match Notice Due Within 24 Hours and No Delivery to Fannie Mae
business_rules.description: Seller/servicer must establish and maintain an effective OFAC compliance program, may not deliver a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on an OFAC sanctions list, and must periodically screen serviced loans against OFAC sanctions lists. If a valid match is identified, the servicer must notify Fannie Mae Ethics via email within 24 hours of identifying the match, block and segregate funds (including escrow), and ensure all servicing activities cease (including remittances and disbursements).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering loans to Fannie Mae or servicing Fannie Mae loans with potential OFAC list matches.
business_rules.consequences: If met: complies with OFAC obligations. If violated: loan cannot be delivered; escalated enforcement and sanctions risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Department of Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) Regulations• must establish and maintain an effective OFAC compliance program; • must report all instances of penalties (civil or criminal) or enforcement actions for compliance failures or violations related to OFAC requirements to Fannie Mae's Ethics division; • may not deliver a loan to Fannie Mae if any borrower is on one of the sanctions lists maintained by OFAC; and • must periodically screen the loans that it services for Fannie Mae against OFAC’s sanctions lists. If the servicer identifies a valid match, the servicer must notify Fannie Mae Ethics via email within 24 hours of identifying a match

examples: Before delivery, lender screens borrower and finds an OFAC SDN list match; loan must not be delivered to Fannie Mae.
Servicer runs periodic OFAC screening and identifies a valid match at 2:00 PM Tuesday; it must notify Fannie Mae Ethics by 2:00 PM Wednesday (within 24 hours).
After a valid match, servicer blocks/segregates escrow funds and stops remitting P&I and paying taxes/insurance until directed by Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination risk; refusal to purchase; regulatory enforcement exposure

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Seller must deliver records within Fannie Mae deadline
business_rules.description: When Fannie Mae issues a written request to examine mortgage records, the seller/servicer must deliver all requested records to Fannie Mae (or its designee) within the timeframe specified by Fannie Mae. If records are maintained in a non-paper format, the seller/servicer must reproduce them at its own expense. If unable to respond timely, the seller/servicer must provide a reasonable explanation and, if appropriate, evidence that the relevant requirement was satisfied. The seller/servicer is responsible for Fannie Mae losses incurred to enforce record access unless Fannie Mae had no legal right of access.
business_rules.conditions: Applies upon Fannie Mae written request to examine mortgage records
business_rules.consequences: Timely delivery satisfies audit; failure may trigger loss liability/enforcement
business_rules.exceptions: Loss liability exception if Fannie Mae had no legal right of access

source_reference.section_id: A2-4.1-02, General Requirements for Audits of Records
source_reference.source_text: When Fannie Mae sends a written request to a seller/servicer to examine mortgage records, the seller/servicer must deliver all records to Fannie Mae or to whomever Fannie Mae designates within the time frame specified by Fannie Mae. Fannie Mae will not execute any trust receipts for documents it requests and will not pay for their delivery. If the seller/servicer is retaining any of the records in a format other than paper, the seller/servicer must reproduce them at its own expense.

examples: Fannie Mae requests a post-purchase file; lender ships imaged documents by the stated due date and satisfies the request.
Lender stores records electronically; when requested, lender prints/exports and provides them at its own expense within the deadline.
Lender cannot meet the deadline due to a system outage; lender provides a written explanation and supporting evidence of compliance for the items under review.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification for Fannie Mae losses enforcing record access

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: QC Review Requires Loan Quality Connect Registration and Complete E-Files
business_rules.description: Seller/Servicers must register for Loan Quality Connect (QC selection notifications are sent via email from Loan Quality Connect) and must maintain a complete loan file including all documents used to support the underwriting decision. Upon request, the lender must provide electronic copies of the complete loan file as described in the request. If unable to submit in supported format, the lender must obtain Loan Quality Center approval before using an alternate submission method.
business_rules.conditions: Fannie Mae QC selects a loan and requests the lender’s complete loan file
business_rules.consequences: If met, QC submission is compliant; if violated, QC response may be non-compliant
business_rules.exceptions: Alternate format allowed only with Loan Quality Center approval

source_reference.section_id: D2-1-02, Notification of a QC Review + Document Submission Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Lenders are notiﬁed which loans Fannie Mae has selected for QC review via an email sent by Fannie Mae’s Loan Quality Connect. Therefore, lenders are required to register for Loan Quality Connect to obtain access to it. Lenders must maintain a complete loan ﬁle, including all documents used to support the underwriting decision. Upon Fannie Mae’s request, lenders must provide electronic copies of the complete loan ﬁle, as described in the request.

examples: Compliant: Lender receives a Loan Quality Connect email, logs in, and uploads the complete underwriting file as requested.
Non-compliant: Lender is not registered for Loan Quality Connect and misses the QC request email, delaying file delivery.
Conditional: Lender cannot produce supported upload format and uses an alternate method only after Loan Quality Center approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Public MBS Disclosures Limited to Prospectus and Supplement Only
business_rules.description: When selling Fannie Mae securities to third parties, the lender may publicly disclose only the information contained in the MBS Prospectus and the applicable Prospectus Supplement for that security. Any additional pool or loan information disclosure to the public outside those documents is prohibited.
business_rules.conditions: Lender sells or offers Fannie Mae MBS securities to third parties
business_rules.consequences: If met, public disclosure is permitted; if violated, disclosure is non-compliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The information in the MBS Prospectus and the Prospectus Supplement for that security is the only information about a pool (or the mortgage loans included in a pool) that a lender may disclose to the public.

examples: Compliant: Lender posts only the PoolTalk link and references the MBS Prospectus and Prospectus Supplement content.
Non-compliant: Lender publishes a public spreadsheet listing loan-level borrower details beyond what the prospectus materials provide.
Non-compliant: Lender issues a press release describing additional pool loan characteristics not contained in the prospectus/supplement.
business_rules.extraction_notes: No numeric threshold present; explicit prohibition language is clear.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Schedule K-1 income allowed only if distributed or liquid
business_rules.description: Income reported on Schedule K-1 may be considered for qualifying only if the lender obtains documentation verifying either (i) the income was actually distributed to the borrower and is consistent with the level of business income used to qualify, or (ii) the business has adequate liquidity to support the withdrawal of earnings. The lender may use discretion in the method used to confirm adequate liquidity.
business_rules.conditions: When qualifying income includes borrower Schedule K-1 income from business
business_rules.consequences: If documentation exists, K-1 income may be used; if not, exclude K-1 income
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Income reported on Schedule K-1 can only be considered if the lender obtains documentation verifying: the income was actually distributed to the borrower and is consistent with the level of business income being used to qualify, or the business has adequate liquidity to support the withdrawal of earnings. The lender may use discretion in the method used to conﬁrm the business has adequate liquidity.

examples: Borrower receives K-1 income but distributions are not evidenced: lender must not use K-1 income for qualifying.
Borrower’s K-1 shows earnings and business bank statements support sufficient liquidity: lender may use K-1 income even if not fully distributed.
K-1 income is distributed but distributions are materially lower than earnings used to qualify: lender must reconcile or reduce qualifying income to supported level.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Self-employed income generally requires 2-year earnings history
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae generally requires the lender to obtain a two-year history of the borrower’s prior earnings to demonstrate the likelihood that self-employment income will continue to be received.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting self-employment income as qualifying income
business_rules.consequences: If 2-year history obtained, proceed; if not, must meet <2-year exception rules
business_rules.exceptions: Income may be considered with <2-year history if requirements are met

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae generally requires lenders to obtain a two-year history of the borrower’s prior earnings as a means of demonstrating the likelihood that the income will continue to be received.

examples: Borrower has filed 2 years of returns for the current business: satisfies the general 2-year history expectation.
