Borrower has only 1 year self-employment history: lender must evaluate under the <2-year history provisions before using income.
Borrower has 3+ years of self-employment: still analyze stability, but 2-year history requirement is met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Less than 2 years self-employment allowed with 12 months returns
business_rules.description: A borrower with less than a two-year history of self-employment may be considered if the borrower’s most recent signed personal and business federal income tax returns reflect a full year (12 months) of self-employment income from the current business, and the loan file contains documentation supporting prior income receipt at the same or greater level in a same-products/services field or similar-responsibilities occupation.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower has <2 years self-employment and income is used to qualify
business_rules.consequences: If 12 months returns + prior income support exist, income may be used; else exclude
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated conditional allowance

source_reference.source_text: However, the income of a person who has less than a two-year history of self-employment may be considered, as long as the borrower’s most recent signed personal and business federal income tax returns reﬂect a full year (12 months) of self-employment income from the current business. The loan ﬁle must also contain documentation to support the history of receipt of prior income at the same (or greater) level

examples: Borrower self-employed 18 months; most recent signed returns show 12 months, and prior W-2 income in same trade at equal/higher level: lender may consider income.
Borrower self-employed 14 months; returns cover only 9 months: lender may not use self-employment income under this exception.
Borrower self-employed 20 months; returns cover 12 months but prior employment was in unrelated field with different duties: exception not met without additional support.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Self-employed income verified with 2 years returns or transcripts
business_rules.description: The lender may verify a self-employed borrower’s employment and income by obtaining signed federal income tax returns filed with the IRS for the past two years (individual and, when applicable, business returns) with all schedules attached. Alternatively, the lender may use IRS-issued transcripts for the most recent two years if the transcripts are complete and legible and include information from all applicable schedules.
business_rules.conditions: When verifying self-employed income used for qualifying in underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If acceptable returns/transcripts obtained, proceed; if not, income not properly verified
business_rules.exceptions: One-year returns may be permitted if additional requirements are met

source_reference.source_text: The lender may verify a self-employed borrower’s employment and income by obtaining from the borrower copies of their signed federal income tax returns (both individual returns and in some cases, business returns) that were ﬁled with the IRS for the past two years (with all applicable schedules attached). Alternatively, the lender may use IRS-issued transcripts of the borrower’s individual and business federal income tax returns that were ﬁled with the IRS for the most recent two years—as long as the information provided is complete and legible and the transcripts include the information from all of the applicable schedules.

examples: Lender obtains signed 2024 and 2025 individual returns with Schedule C and all schedules: satisfies 2-year return option.
Lender uses IRS transcripts for 2024 and 2025 but the transcript omits a required schedule: does not meet transcript completeness requirement.
Lender collects only one year of returns without meeting the five-year business/ownership exception: verification requirement not met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: One year tax returns allowed only with 5-year business and 25% ownership
business_rules.description: The lender may provide one year of personal and business tax returns for self-employed income if: (1) the business has been in existence for five years as reflected on the Form 1003, and (2) the borrower has had an ownership share of 25% or more for the past five years consecutively. For partnerships/S-corps/corporations, the business return must support the Form 1003; if the business existed before the borrower reached 25% ownership, the lender must demonstrate 25%+ ownership for at least five years consecutively. All businesses must be assessed separately for the five-year benchmark when multiple self-employment income sources exist.
business_rules.conditions: When lender seeks to document self-employed income with 1 year of tax returns
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions met, 1-year returns permitted; if not, provide 2 years returns/transcripts
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated eligibility to use 1-year returns

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.5-01, Verification of Income (one-year returns option)
source_reference.source_text: The lender may provide one year of personal and business tax returns if the following requirements are met: the business from which the borrower is using self-employed income must have been in existence for ﬁve years as reﬂected on the Form 1003, and the borrower has had an ownership share of 25% or more for the past ﬁve years consecutively, and for partnerships, S corporations and corporations, the federal income tax return for the business must support the information reﬂected on the Form 1003.

examples: Borrower has owned 30% of an S-corp for 6 consecutive years and business exists 6 years: lender may use 1 year of returns (if other requirements met).
Borrower owns 25% now but only 3 years at that level (business exists 10 years): lender may not use 1 year returns; must obtain 2 years.
Borrower has two self-employment sources: one business meets 5-year benchmark, the other does not; lender may apply different return-year requirements per business.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Verbal VOE within 10 business days or 120 days before note date
business_rules.description: A verbal verification of employment (verbal VOE) must be obtained for each borrower using employment or self-employment income to qualify. The verbal VOE must be obtained within 10 business days prior to the note date for employment income, and within 120 calendar days prior to the note date for self-employment income. Alternatively, the lender may obtain the verbal VOE after closing up to the time of loan delivery; if the verbal VOE (or allowable alternative) cannot be obtained prior to delivery, the loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower uses employment or self-employment income to qualify for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If obtained timely, proceed; if not obtained by delivery, loan is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Allowed to obtain after closing up to loan delivery

source_reference.source_text: A verbal veriﬁcation of employment (verbal VOE) must be obtained for each borrower using employment or self-employment income to qualify. The verbal VOE must be obtained within 10 business days prior to the note date for employment income, and within 120 calendar days prior to the note date for self-employment income. Alternatively, the lender may obtain the verbal VOE after closing, up to the time of loan delivery. If the verbal VOE (or allowable alternative) cannot be obtained prior to delivery, the loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.

examples: W-2 borrower closing on June 20: verbal VOE must be dated within 10 business days before June 20 (or use an allowed alternative within required window).
Self-employed borrower note date June 20: verbal VOE must be dated within 120 calendar days before June 20, unless obtained after closing but before delivery.
Lender cannot obtain VOE or alternative before delivering the loan: loan is ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Seller must self-report selling warranty breach within 30 days
business_rules.description: If, after conducting due diligence, the lender determines that a breach of a selling warranty has likely occurred, the lender must notify Fannie Mae within 30 days using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect.
business_rules.conditions: After due diligence, lender determines a selling warranty breach likely occurred
business_rules.consequences: Failure to timely report may increase remedy/sanction risk under the contract
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.section_id: A2-2-01, Lender Reporting Requirements for Breach of Selling Warranty
source_reference.source_text: The lender must notify Fannie Mae within 30 days if, after conducting due diligence, it determines that a breach of a selling warranty has likely occurred, by using the self-report functionality in Loan Quality Connect .

examples: QC review identifies misstatement of occupancy; lender completes due diligence and self-reports via Loan Quality Connect within 30 days: meets reporting requirement.
Lender discovers missing title exception documentation but does not report for 75 days: violates the 30-day reporting requirement.
Third-party fraud is discovered; lender determines a selling warranty breach likely occurred and self-reports within 30 days: meets requirement even though lender did not commit fraud.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Indemnification or loan repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Value acceptance may be exercised only if offer ≤ 4 months old
business_rules.description: A lender may only exercise value acceptance if the final DU submission resulted in a value acceptance offer, no appraisal is obtained for the transaction, and the offer is not more than four months old on the date of the note and mortgage. If exercised, the lender must include Special Feature Code (SFC) 801 at delivery.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer intends to deliver a loan using DU value acceptance
business_rules.consequences: If conditions met, deliver without appraisal; if violated, must order appraisal
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the exercise criteria excerpt

source_reference.source_text: A lender may only exercise value acceptance if the ﬁnal submission of the loan caseﬁle to DU resulted in a value acceptance oﬀer, an appraisal is not obtained for the transaction, and the value acceptance oﬀer is not more than four months old on the date of the note and the mortgage. Lenders that elect to exercise value acceptance must include SFC 801 at delivery.

examples: DU value acceptance offer dated 3 months before note date and no appraisal ordered → lender may exercise value acceptance and deliver with SFC 801.
DU value acceptance offer is 5 months old at note date → lender may not exercise; must obtain an appraisal.
Lender orders an appraisal after receiving value acceptance → lender may not exercise value acceptance for that transaction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand / remedies for breach of requirements

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Value acceptance plus data requires API submit before note and 12-month data
business_rules.description: A lender may only exercise value acceptance + property data when the final DU submission resulted in an eligibility message for the option, the property data collection is submitted to the Property Data API prior to the note date, no appraisal is obtained, and the offer is not more than four months old on the date of the note and mortgage. The lender must include SFC 774 at delivery. Property data collection is valid for 12 months from the collection date and must be performed prior to the note date.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer uses value acceptance + property data instead of an appraisal
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied, appraisal not required; if not, must obtain hybrid/desktop/traditional
business_rules.exceptions: If offer lost, property data may support a hybrid appraisal per Guide

source_reference.source_text: A lender may only exercise value acceptance + property data when the ﬁnal submission of the loan caseﬁle to DU resulted in an eligibility message for value acceptance + property data, property data collection is submitted to the Property Data API prior to the note date, an appraisal is not obtained for the transaction, and the oﬀer is not more than four months old on the date of the note and mortgage. Lenders that elect to exercise value acceptance + property data must include Special Feature Code 774 at loan delivery. The property data collection is only valid for 12 months from date of collection and must be performed prior to the note date.

examples: Property data collected 2 weeks before note date and submitted to API before note date; offer is 2 months old → lender may deliver without appraisal using SFC 774.
Property data collected after the note date → lender cannot exercise this option; must obtain appraisal solution required by DU.
Property data collected 13 months ago (even if resubmitted) → invalid; lender must obtain new property data collection or appraisal.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand / delivery rejection risk

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: eNote Registration Must Occur Within 1 Business Day After Signing
business_rules.description: Lenders must have process controls to ensure eNotes are registered in the MERS eRegistry as soon as possible after the tamper-evident seal is applied, but no later than one business day after signing. The MERS eRegistry record must show the originating lender (the lender whose name is on the eNote) as the first Controller. If trade/assumed names are used on eNotes, the legal entity name must also be included on the eNote, and the initial MERS eRegistry registration must reflect the legal entity name as the initial Controller. Failure to comply could render the loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when originating and closing an eMortgage with an eNote.
business_rules.consequences: If registered timely with correct Controller, compliant; if not, may be ineligible for sale.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Lenders must have process controls in place to ensure eNotes are registered in the MERS eRegistry as soon as possible after the tamper-evident seal has been applied, but no later than one business day after signing. The record in the MERS eRegistry must reflect the originating lender - the lender whose name is on the eNote - as the first Controller. The following applies to lenders using tradenames or assumed names on eNotes: the legal entity name must also be included on the eNote... and the initial MERS eRegistry registration must reflect the legal entity name as the initial Controller... Failure to comply with these requirements could render the loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.

examples: Borrower signs Tuesday; lender registers eNote in MERS eRegistry by Wednesday (next business day) with lender as first Controller → compliant.
Borrower signs Friday; lender registers the following Tuesday (2 business days later) → violates timing requirement; loan may be ineligible.
eNote shows 'XYZ Mortgage' only; legal entity 'ABC Financial, Inc.' not included and Controller set to XYZ → violates tradename/legal-entity requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for sale to Fannie Mae.

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Employees on GSA HUD or FHFA Lists Cannot Work Fannie Loans
business_rules.description: The seller/servicer must follow a written procedure for checking all employees (including management) involved in origination or servicing of mortgage loans against the GSA Excluded Parties List (EPL), HUD Limited Denial of Participation (LDP) List, and FHFA Suspended Counterparty Program (SCP) list. If at time of hire or any time later an individual is determined to be on any of these lists, the seller/servicer may not permit that employee to manage or perform origination or servicing functions on loans sold to Fannie Mae, regardless of the reason the individual is on the list.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at hire and ongoing for staff supporting Fannie Mae loan lifecycle.
business_rules.consequences: If listed, employee must be removed from covered functions; failure is noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; applies regardless of reason for listing.

source_reference.section_id: A3-4-03, Fraud Prevention Requirements - Hiring Practices
source_reference.source_text: The seller/servicer must follow a written procedure for checking all employees, including management, involved in the origination or servicing of mortgage loans (including application through closing and servicing) against the U.S. General Services Administration (GSA) Excluded Parties List (EPL), the HUD Limited Denial of Participation List (LDP List), and the Federal Housing Finance Agency’s (FHFA) Suspended Counterparty Program (SCP) list. If, at the time of hire or any time later, the seller/servicer has determined an individual is on the GSA, LDP, or SCP list, the seller/servicer may not permit that employee to manage or perform origination or servicing functions on loans sold to Fannie Mae, irrespective of the reason the individual is on such list.

examples: New processor is screened at hire and not on any list → may work Fannie Mae loans.
Underwriter later appears on FHFA SCP list; lender immediately removes underwriter from Fannie Mae loan underwriting → compliant response.
Lender learns a closer is on HUD LDP list but continues assigning closings on Fannie Mae loans → violation and heightened repurchase/termination risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination risk; loan repurchase/indemnification exposure.

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Seller Must Return Servicing Docs Within 5 Days
business_rules.description: When the Servicer requires Seller execution of servicing-related documentation, Seller must execute and return the documentation as promptly as reasonably feasible, but in no event more than five (5) days after delivery.
business_rules.conditions: Execution by Seller is required for servicing documentation after delivery
business_rules.consequences: If met, servicing transfer proceeds; if violated, transfer delays and disputes
business_rules.exceptions: Alternative: Seller may provide POA allowing Servicer execution

source_reference.source_text: alternative when execution by Seller is required, Seller shall, as promptly as reasonably feasible, but in no event exceeding ﬁve (5) days after delivery, execute and

examples: Servicer sends a satisfaction document to Seller on Monday; Seller must return executed document by Saturday (5 days).
Servicer requests Seller-signed assignment correction delivered on June 1; Seller returns signed version by June 5 (compliant).
Instead of signing each document, Seller provides a power of attorney enabling Servicer to execute required documents (alternative compliance).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Operational remedy; potential contractual dispute

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Escrowed Taxes Must Be Paid Within 30 Days After Transfer
business_rules.description: If the mortgage loan requires the mortgagor to escrow funds, Seller must pay all real estate taxes that are due and payable on or before the Purchase Date and within thirty (30) days after the Purchase Date. Seller must not pay any bill for which Seller has not specifically retained escrow funds. If the loan does not require escrow, Seller must cause the mortgagors to pay the described real estate taxes.
business_rules.conditions: Servicing rights transferred; taxes due and payable and escrow requirement applies
business_rules.consequences: If met, tax obligations stay current; if violated, tax delinquency and transfer issues
business_rules.exceptions: If no escrow required, Seller must cause mortgagors to pay taxes

source_reference.source_text: Real Estate Tax Payment . In the event the Mortgage Loan requires the mortgagor to escrow funds, Seller shall pay all real estate taxes that are due and payable (i) on or before the Purchase Date, and (ii) within thirty (30) days after the Purchase Date. Seller agrees to follow transfer procedures with respect to real estate tax service contracts in a manner described in the Servicing Transfer Instructions. In addition, Seller shall not pay any bill for which Seller has not specifically retained escrow funds pursuant to Section 6.2 herein. In the event the Mortgage Loan does not require the mortgagor to escrow funds, Seller shall cause the mortgagors to pay the above-described real estate taxes.

examples: Escrowed loan has property taxes due 10 days after Purchase Date; Seller must pay within 30 days after Purchase Date.
Tax bill arrives but Seller did not retain escrow funds under Section 6.2; Seller must not pay that bill (non-payment required).
Non-escrow loan has taxes due after transfer; Seller must ensure mortgagor pays the taxes as described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Operational remedy; potential contractual dispute

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Loan File Due in 3 Business Days or $500 Late Fee
business_rules.description: Within three (3) Business Days after the Purchase Date, Seller must furnish Servicer (or designated document custodian) a complete loan file as described in the Fannie Mae Selling Guide (unless otherwise directed by Fannie Mae), must retain a copy of the complete loan file per the Fannie Mae Servicing Guide, and may be charged a late fee up to $500.00 per mortgage loan if Seller fails to comply.
business_rules.conditions: After Purchase Date when servicing rights are transferred and file delivery required
business_rules.consequences: If met, Servicer receives compliant file; if violated, late fee up to $500/loan
business_rules.exceptions: Complete file delivery may differ if directed otherwise by Fannie Mae

source_reference.source_text: Within three (3) Business Days after the Purchase Date, Seller at its own expense shall furnish Servicer or the designated document custodian, as applicable, with the following: ... (b) File. A complete loan file as described in the Fannie Mae Selling Guide to Servicer unless otherwise directed by Fannie Mae. Seller must also retain a copy of the complete loan file and maintain that copy in accordance with the requirements of the Fannie Mae Servicing Guide and any other applicable requirements set forth therein. ... (d) Late Fee . If Seller fails to comply with this Section, Servicer may, in its sole discretion, charge Seller a late fee of up to $500.00 per mortgage loan.

examples: Purchase Date is Tuesday; Seller must deliver the complete file by Friday (3 business days) to avoid late fee exposure.
Seller delivers file on day 6; Servicer may charge $500 late fee for that mortgage loan.
Fannie Mae directs an alternative file routing; Seller follows that instruction instead of standard delivery destination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Late fee up to $500 per mortgage loan

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Annual audited financial statement due within 90 days
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must submit its annual Audited Financial Statement (AFS) within 90 days after the end of its fiscal year. Failure to timely provide required financial statements and information may result in Fannie Mae declaring a breach of the Lender Contract.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to every Seller/Servicer fiscal year-end AFS submission to Fannie Mae
business_rules.consequences: If late/not provided, Fannie Mae may declare a Lender Contract breach
business_rules.exceptions: See separate exceptions for supervised depository institutions and HUD audits

source_reference.source_text: The seller/servicer must demonstrate its ﬁnancial adequacy to Fannie Mae. To accomplish this, Fannie Mae requires the seller/servicer to submit ﬁnancial statements and other information described in this topic. A seller/servicer’s failure to timely provide the required information may result in Fannie Mae declaring a breach of the Lender Contract. A seller/servicer must submit their annual Audited Financial Statement (AFS) within 90 days after the end of the seller/servicer’s ﬁscal year.

examples: Seller/Servicer fiscal year ends 12/31; AFS must be submitted by 03/31; submission on 04/15 is a late submission exposing a breach finding.
Seller/Servicer fiscal year ends 06/30; AFS due by 09/28; failure to submit can trigger Lender Contract breach remedies.
Seller/Servicer submits AFS by day 75 after fiscal year end; requirement satisfied and no breach risk on timeliness.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Breach of Lender Contract

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1002 quarterly due 30 days; Dec 31 due 60
business_rules.description: If the Seller/Servicer is a mortgage banker, subsidiary of a federally supervised depository institution, housing finance agency, or REIT, it must submit Form 1002 at the end of each calendar quarter, certified by the CEO, CFO, or equivalent. The Seller/Servicer must electronically submit within 30 days for the March 31, June 30, and September 30 reports, and within 60 days for the December 31 report. Each report must include only the financial data related to the quarterly reporting period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Seller/Servicer type requires Form 1002 quarterly reporting
business_rules.consequences: Late/inaccurate quarterly reporting may support eligibility actions or breach
business_rules.exceptions: Non-calendar accounting cycles may be used if report includes only required quarter

source_reference.source_text: In addition to the AFS, the seller/servicer must submit a Mortgage Bankers’ Financial Reporting Form (Form 1002) that has been certiﬁed by the chief executive oﬃcer, the chief ﬁnancial oﬃcer, or equivalent at the end of each calendar quarter. Each report must include only the ﬁnancial data related to the quarterly reporting period for which the report is being submitted. The seller/servicer must electronically submit this information within 30 days for the March 31, June 30, and September 30 reports and within 60 days for the December 31 report.

examples: For Q1 ending 03/31, Form 1002 must be submitted by 04/30; submission on 05/10 violates the deadline.
For Q4 ending 12/31, Form 1002 must be submitted by 03/01 (60 days); submission on 02/15 meets the rule.
If a Seller/Servicer uses a non-standard quarterly accounting cycle, it may keep its methodology but must ensure each report contains data for only the required quarter.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Breach of Lender Contract

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Form 1002A monthly due 30 days; skip quarter 3rd month
business_rules.description: If the Seller/Servicer is a large non-depository Seller/Servicer, it must electronically submit Form 1002A within 30 days of the end of each month, certified by the CEO, CFO, or equivalent. Each report must include only the financial data related to the month preceding the month of submission. The Seller/Servicer is not required to provide a monthly report for the third month of any fiscal quarter because the data will be captured in the quarterly report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to large non-depository Seller/Servicers subject to monthly Form 1002A
business_rules.consequences: Missing/late reports may drive enhanced oversight or eligibility actions
business_rules.exceptions: No monthly report required for each third month of any fiscal quarter

source_reference.source_text: In addition to the required quarterly reporting, a large non-depository seller/servicer must electronically submit a Mortgage Bankers' Financial Reporting Short Form (Form 1002A) within 30 days of the end of each month. The report must be certiﬁed by the chief executive oﬀer, the chief ﬁnancial oﬃcer, or equivalent. Each report must include only the ﬁnancial data related to the month preceding the month in which the report is being submitted. The seller/servicer is not required to provide a monthly report for each third month of any ﬁscal quarter, as the data will be captured in the quarterly report.

examples: A large non-depository Seller/Servicer’s January month-end is 01/31; Form 1002A must be submitted by 03/02 (30 days after 01/31).
For a fiscal quarter April–June, the Seller/Servicer submits monthly reports for April and May but does not submit a June monthly report in July because June is the third month of the quarter.
If a monthly report includes two months of data (e.g., April+May), it violates the requirement to include only the prior month’s data.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Breach of Lender Contract

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Form 582 update within 90 days; notify changes in 5 days
business_rules.description: The Seller/Servicer must update Form 582 electronically no later than 90 days after the end of its fiscal year. If lender record information changes, the Seller/Servicer must update Form 582 and provide written notification by email to the designated mailbox within five business days of the occurrence of specified events (e.g., actions pending/threatened that could reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to annual eligibility maintenance and upon any lender record information change
business_rules.consequences: Failure/late notice increases eligibility risk and may lead to Fannie Mae action
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: The Lender Record Information ( Form 582 ) provides information needed to verify that the seller/servicer continues to meet Fannie Mae's basic eligibility requirements as well as certiﬁcations regarding compliance with our requirements. The seller/servicer must update its Form 582 electronically no later than 90 days after the end of the seller/servicer’s ﬁscal year. If there are changes to the lender's record information, the seller/servicer must update Form 582 and provide Fannie Mae with written notiﬁcation in the form of an email to the Changes in Lender Organization mailbox ... within ﬁve business days of the occurrence of any of the following: any actions pending, starting, or to the seller/servicer's knowledge, threatened against or involving the seller/servicer that could reasonably be expected to have a material adverse eﬀect

examples: Fiscal year ends 09/30; Form 582 must be updated by 12/29 (90 days).
A regulator initiates an enforcement action on 02/01 that could materially affect operations; the Seller/Servicer must email notice and update Form 582 by 02/08 (five business days).
A corporate reorganization occurs on 03/10; Seller/Servicer must provide written notification within five business days and update Form 582 accordingly.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Breach of Lender Contract

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: PERS Required for Florida Attached Condos and 4+ Unit Conversions
business_rules.description: Lenders must submit certain projects to Fannie Mae’s Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS) as a required review method. Required Standard PERS includes, among other cases: newly converted non-gut rehabilitation condo and co-op projects with attached units that contain more than four units, and new/newly converted condo projects consisting of attached units located in Florida. Other projects are optional or may be required under the streamlined PERS process as specified.
business_rules.conditions: When lender seeks project eligibility for project types designated as PERS-required
business_rules.consequences: Meets: project reviewed/eligible path supported; Violates: missing required PERS; project/unit ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Projects not listed may be optional per table; manufactured-home references include single/multi-width unless noted

source_reference.section_id: B4-2.2-06, Overview (PERS required vs optional table)
source_reference.source_text: Required for: • newly converted non-gut rehabilitation condo and co-op projects with attached units that contain more than four units; • new and newly converted condo projects consisting of attached units located in Florida; and • limited or shared equity co-op projects, provided the limited or shared equity provisions are designed to preserve or promote access to aﬀordable housing. Optional for: • all other new or newly converted condo projects not listed above.

examples: Newly converted non-gut rehab co-op with 12 attached units: PERS submission is required before delivering a unit loan to Fannie Mae.
New attached condo project in Florida: lender must use Standard PERS (cannot treat as optional).
Newly converted attached condo project outside Florida and not otherwise listed: PERS may be optional per the table.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Seller must sell at least 12 loans in prior calendar year
business_rules.description: To maintain Seller eligibility, the Seller must have sold at least 12 loans to Fannie Mae during the prior calendar year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Fannie Mae-approved sellers during annual eligibility review.
business_rules.consequences: If met, seller remains eligible; if not met, seller approval termination applies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Minimum Requirements Sellers/servicers must comply with the minimum requirements described in the following table. Category Description Loan Sales A seller must sell at least 12 loans to Fannie Mae during the prior calendar year.

examples: Seller delivered 15 loans to Fannie Mae in the prior calendar year: meets the 12-loan minimum.
Seller delivered 11 loans to Fannie Mae in the prior calendar year: fails the minimum and is subject to termination.
Seller delivered 12 loans exactly in the prior calendar year: meets the minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Termination of seller approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Servicer must service at least 1 Fannie Mae loan as of Dec 31
business_rules.description: To maintain Servicer eligibility, the Servicer must service at least one loan for Fannie Mae as of December 31 of the prior calendar year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Fannie Mae-approved servicers at year-end eligibility assessment.
business_rules.consequences: If met, servicer remains eligible; if not met, servicer approval termination applies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Minimum Requirements Sellers/servicers must comply with the minimum requirements described in the following table. Category Description Loan ServicingA servicer must service at least one loan for Fannie Mae as of December 31 of the prior calendar year.

examples: Servicer has 250 Fannie Mae loans boarded as of Dec 31: meets the minimum.
Servicer has 0 Fannie Mae loans boarded as of Dec 31: fails and is subject to termination.
Servicer has exactly 1 Fannie Mae loan boarded as of Dec 31: meets the minimum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Termination of servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Termination and tech access ended within 60 days if minimums not met
business_rules.description: If the seller fails the minimum loan sale requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval and terminate access to technology licensed only to approved sellers within 60 days. If the servicer fails the minimum servicing requirement, Fannie Mae will terminate approval and terminate access to technology licensed only to approved servicers.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when prior-year minimum loan sale or servicing requirements are not met.
business_rules.consequences: Approval is terminated; technology access is terminated within 60 days; reapproval requires new application.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: If a seller does not meet the minimum loan sale requirement below, the seller's approval to sell loans to Fannie Mae will be terminated and access to all technology that is licensed only to approved sellers will be terminate within 60 days. If a servicer does not meet the minimum loan servicing requirement below, the servicer's approval to service loans for Fannie Mae will be terminated

examples: Seller sold 9 loans last year: Fannie Mae terminates seller approval and ends seller-only tech access within 60 days.
Servicer has no Fannie Mae loans as of Dec 31: Fannie Mae terminates servicer approval and ends servicer-only tech access.
Entity terminated as seller re-applies via Seller/Servicer application process to regain eligibility.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Termination and technology access termination

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Adjusted net worth minimum $2.5 million plus UPB factors
business_rules.description: All depository and non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain Adjusted Net Worth at all times of at least $2,500,000 plus additional amounts based on servicing unpaid principal balance (UPB): (1) 0.25% of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, plus (2) 0.35% of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus (3) 0.25% of Other Servicing UPB.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times to all approved depository and non-depository Seller/Servicers
business_rules.consequences: If met, Seller/Servicer remains financially eligible; if not met, approval is at risk and Fannie Mae may impose remedies
business_rules.exceptions: Nonprofits may use equivalent financial data per Fannie Mae determination

source_reference.source_text: All depository and non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times an Adjusted Net Worth of at least $2.5 million, plus an amount equal to or greater than the sum of the following: • 0.25% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, plus • 0.35% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus • 0.25% of the Other Servicing UPB

examples: Seller/Servicer has $2.5M base + ($10B FNMA/FHLMC UPB×0.25%=$25M) + ($1B GNMA×0.35%=$3.5M) + ($500M other×0.25%=$1.25M) → minimum ANW $32.25M.
Depository Seller/Servicer with $8M ANW and no servicing UPB beyond $0 must still meet the $2.5M base; $8M passes.
Nonprofit Seller/Servicer cannot calculate Adjusted Net Worth under its reporting; Fannie Mae uses equivalent financial data to test whether it meets the same minimum requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Non-depository capital ratio minimum 6% adjusted net worth
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain at all times a minimum Adjusted Net Worth to total assets ratio of 6% (or equivalent as determined by Fannie Mae). This is a standing capital adequacy requirement separate from absolute net worth and liquidity requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times when Seller/Servicer is non-depository
business_rules.consequences: If met, Seller/Servicer meets minimum capital requirement; if violated, Fannie Mae may require corrective action or restrict eligibility
business_rules.exceptions: Equivalent ratio may be used if determined by Fannie Mae

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times a minimum Adjusted New Worth/total assets ratio of 6%, or equivalent, as determined by Fannie Mae.

examples: Non-depository has Adjusted Net Worth $12M and total assets $150M → ratio 8.0% (passes).
Non-depository has Adjusted Net Worth $9M and total assets $180M → ratio 5.0% (fails; must remediate or risk restrictions).
Seller/Servicer uses an alternative but equivalent ratio metric accepted by Fannie Mae due to reporting conventions; must still demonstrate at least 6% equivalency.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Non-depository minimum liquidity based on UPB and remittance type
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers must maintain Allowable Liquidity at all times at or above a calculated minimum equal to: (1) 0.07% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with scheduled/scheduled or scheduled/actual remittance types, plus (2) 0.035% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with actual/actual remittance types, plus (3) 0.10% of the portion of Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus (4) 0.035% of the Other Servicing UPB.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at all times when Seller/Servicer is non-depository and has servicing UPB
business_rules.consequences: If met, liquidity requirement satisfied; if violated, Seller/Servicer may face eligibility actions and required liquidity remediation
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository sellers/servicers must maintain at all times an Allowable Liquidity at a level equal to or greater than the sum of the following: • 0.07% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with scheduled/scheduled or scheduled/actual remittance types, plus • 0.035% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac with actual/actual remittance types, plus; • 0.10% of the portion of the seller/servicer's Residential First Lien Mortgage Servicing UPB serviced for Ginnie Mae, plus • 0.035% of the Other Servicing UPB.

examples: Non-depository has $5B scheduled/scheduled FNMA/FHLMC UPB and $2B actual/actual FNMA/FHLMC UPB → min liquidity = $5B×0.07% ($3.5M) + $2B×0.035% ($0.7M) plus other components.
Non-depository services $1B GNMA UPB → adds $1B×0.10% = $1.0M to minimum liquidity requirement.
If Seller/Servicer’s computed minimum liquidity is $6.2M but allowable liquidity is $5.8M, the Seller/Servicer fails the “at all times” threshold and must remediate to avoid eligibility actions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Origination liquidity 0.5% when 4-quarter originations exceed $1B
business_rules.description: Non-depository Seller/Servicers that originate more than $1,000,000,000 in one- to four-unit residential first lien mortgage loans in the most recent four-quarter period (excluding reverse mortgages, construction-to-permanent loans to home buyers, and lot loans) must maintain origination liquidity equal to 0.5% of the sum of: (1) eligible one- to four-unit first lien loans held for sale at lower of cost or market, plus (2) eligible one- to four-unit first lien loans held for sale at fair value, plus (3) UPB of interest rate lock commitments after fallout adjustments.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when non-depository originations > $1B in most recent 4-quarter period (with stated exclusions)
business_rules.consequences: If met, origination liquidity requirement satisfied; if violated, Seller/Servicer may be required to increase liquidity or face eligibility restrictions
business_rules.exceptions: Reverse, construction-to-permanent to home buyers, and consumer lot loans excluded from threshold/base

source_reference.source_text: Non-depository seller/servicers who originate greater than $1 billion in one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans (excluding reverse mortgages, one- to four-unit residential construction-to- permanent loans to home buyers, and lot loans to consumers) in the most recent four-quarter period must maintain an origination liquidity of 0.5% of the sum of the following: • one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans ... held for sale, at lower of cost or market; plus • one- to four-unit residential ﬁrst lien mortgage loans ... held for sale, at fair value; plus • UPB of interest rate lock commitments after fallout adjustments.

examples: Non-depository originated $1.2B eligible first liens in last 4 quarters and has $200M HFS at LOCOM + $150M HFS at FV + $300M IRLC UPB → base $650M; min origination liquidity $650M×0.5% = $3.25M.
Non-depository originated $950M in eligible first liens last 4 quarters → origination liquidity rule does not apply (but other liquidity rules still apply).
If originations exceed $1B only after including excluded reverse mortgages, the threshold test must still exclude those loans; do not trigger origination liquidity solely due to excluded products.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Large non-depository plan due 90 days with 5-day and 1-day notices
business_rules.description: Within 90 days after the end of each calendar year, Large Non-depository Seller/Servicers must submit a capital and liquidity plan to Fannie Mae describing how they will manage capital and liquidity consistent with Fannie Mae requirements. The plan must require written notice to Fannie Mae (a) within five business days following any material change to or material deviation from the plan, and (b) within one business day of any material changes during times of stress.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Large Non-depository Seller/Servicers; measured annually with event-driven notice triggers
business_rules.consequences: If completed timely, meets reporting obligation; if late/missed notices, Fannie Mae may require remediation or impose eligibility actions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (stress test may be in-house or third-party)

source_reference.source_text: Within 90 days after the end of each calendar year, Large Non-depository sellers/servicers must submit a plan to Fannie Mae that describes how the seller/servicer intends to manage its capital and liquidity in a manner consistent with Fannie Mae requirements. ... and • a requirement to provide written notice to Fannie Mae ⚬ within ﬁve business days following any material change to or material deviation from the plan; and ⚬ within one business day of any material changes during times of stress.

examples: Calendar year ends 12/31; Large non-depository submits its plan by 3/31 (within 90 days) and satisfies the timing requirement.
Seller/Servicer updates contingency funding assumptions on 6/15 (material change) → must notify Fannie Mae in writing by 6/22 (within 5 business days).
During a stress event, Seller/Servicer makes a material change on Monday → must provide written notice by Tuesday (within 1 business day).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension/termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Daily mandatory commitment cap is $200 million
business_rules.description: A Seller/Servicer may enter into multiple mandatory whole loan commitments in a day, but the total aggregate commitment volume per day must not exceed $200,000,000 unless Fannie Mae grants permission. If the Seller/Servicer exceeds the limit without Fannie Mae approval, Fannie Mae may require the lender to pair off the commitment(s) at the lender’s expense.
business_rules.conditions: When entering mandatory whole loan commitments in a single day
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded w/o approval, Fannie Mae may require pair-off at lender expense
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed only with prior permission from Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk

source_reference.source_text: Commitment Amounts Lenders may enter into multiple commitments but may not exceed $200 million in aggregate commitment volume per day. Lenders seeking permission to exceed this amount must contact the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk (see E-1-02, List of Contacts ). Lenders exceeding the limit without Fannie Mae approval may be required to pair oﬀ their commitment(s) at the lender's expense.

examples: Lender enters $150M + $40M commitments same day ($190M): compliant; no action needed.
Lender enters $120M + $90M commitments same day ($210M) without approval: may be required to pair off at lender expense.
Lender expects $250M volume; contacts Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk and receives permission: compliant to exceed cap.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pair-off at lender expense

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Fidelity and E&O policies require 30-day and 10-day notices
business_rules.description: A Seller/Servicer must have a blanket fidelity bond and an errors and omissions (E&O) insurance policy in effect at all times. Each fidelity bond and E&O policy must include: (1) notice to Fannie Mae at least 30 days before insurer cancels, reduces, declines to renew, or imposes a restrictive modification (other than partial/full exhaustion of liability limits); and (2) a provision that the insurer will notify Fannie Mae within 10 days after receiving the Seller/Servicer’s request to cancel or reduce coverage. The Seller/Servicer must provide Fannie Mae a copy of the fidelity bond or E&O insurance certificate within 30 days of Fannie Mae’s request.
business_rules.conditions: Seller/Servicer maintains fidelity bond and E&O coverage while approved
business_rules.consequences: If provisions/certificates not provided, approval risk and contract noncompliance
business_rules.exceptions: 30-day notice exception when change due to exhaustion of liability limits

source_reference.section_id: A3-5-01, Fidelity Bond and Errors and Omissions Coverage Provisions
source_reference.source_text: A seller/servicer must have a blanket fidelity bond and an errors and omissions insurance policy in effect at all times in an amount sufficient to meet Fannie Mae’s minimum coverage requirements described in A3-5-02, Fidelity Bond Policy Requirements and A3-5-03, Errors and Omissions Policy Requirements. Each fidelity bond and errors and omissions insurance policy must include the following provisions: Fannie Mae will be notified at least 30 days before the insurer cancels, reduces, declines to renew, or imposes a restrictive modification to the seller/servicer’s coverage for any reason other than a partial or full exhaustion of the insurer’s limit of liability under the policy; and a provision that the insurer will notify Fannie Mae within ten days after the insurer receives a seller/servicer’s request to cancel or reduce any coverage. The seller/servicer must provide Fannie Mae a copy of its fidelity bond or errors and omissions insurance certificate within 30 days of Fannie Mae’s request.

examples: Insurer plans nonrenewal effective July 31; insurer must notify Fannie Mae by at least July 1 (30 days prior).
Seller/Servicer emails insurer requesting reduced limits; insurer must notify Fannie Mae within 10 days of receiving the request.
Fannie Mae requests insurance certificate on May 1; Seller/Servicer must provide it by May 31.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit timing requirements (30 days, 10 days, 30 days) and always-in-effect requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension or termination of Seller/Servicer approval

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: C1-2-03 UCD XML Successful Status Before Delivery
business_rules.description: Before delivering a conventional loan to Fannie Mae, the lender must electronically submit a Uniform Closing Dataset (UCD) XML file and receive a “Successful” status from the UCD collection solution. The UCD XML must contain borrower data and the PDF of the borrower Closing Disclosure. This submission is required for all conventional loans, even when Regulation Z does not require a Closing Disclosure, including non-owner-occupied property loans.
business_rules.conditions: Before delivery of any conventional loan to Fannie Mae (all occupancies)
business_rules.consequences: If met: loan may proceed to delivery; if violated: delivery is blocked until successful
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; applies regardless of Regulation Z CD requirement

source_reference.source_text: Lenders are required to electronically submit a UCD XML ﬁle of the loan to Fannie Mae and receive a “Successful” status from the UCD collection solution prior to the delivery of the loan. The UCD XML ﬁle must contain the borrower data and the PDF of the borrower Closing Disclosure. Lenders must submit the UCD XML ﬁle for all conventional loans, regardless of whether the TILA/Regulation Z Rule requires the Closing Disclosure, including non-owner-occupied property loans.

examples: Primary residence conventional loan: lender uploads UCD XML plus CD PDF; status returns Successful; delivery proceeds.
Investment property conventional loan with CD not required by Reg Z: lender still submits UCD XML and gets Successful.
UCD submission returns non-Successful status; lender must correct/resubmit before delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection (cannot deliver until Successful status)

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Whole loan overdelivery capped at 25% plus $50 tolerance
business_rules.description: For whole loan commitments, the maximum delivery amount generally cannot exceed the original commitment amount by more than the greater of $10,000 or 2.5% of the original commitment amount, unless the lender requests and receives permission to overdeliver. If an overdelivery is requested/approved, the maximum overdelivery amount is 25% of the original commitment amount and the total delivered against the commitment cannot exceed (original commitment + maximum overdelivery + $50). After an overdelivery occurs, the maximum delivery tolerance level decreases to $50 above the new commitment amount. If the 25% overdelivery calculation is less than $10,000, the 'high tolerance level' becomes the maximum amount the lender can deliver.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering whole loans against a Fannie Mae whole loan commitment
business_rules.consequences: If exceeded, delivery may be rejected and/or overdelivery price adjustment may apply
business_rules.exceptions: Overdelivery requires permission; otherwise use greater of $10,000 or 2.5% cap

source_reference.source_text: The maximum delivery amount is an amount that will not exceed the original commitment amount by more than the greater of $10,000 or 2.5% of the original commitment amount, unless a lender requests an overdelivery. If a lender requests an overdelivery, the maximum required delivery amount will be $50 above the revised commitment amount... The maximum overdelivery amount is 25% of the original commitment amount. For example, on an original commitment amount of $150,000, the maximum overdelivery amount is an additional $37,500 (25% of $150,000). In this example, the total delivered against the commitment cannot exceed $187,550 ($150,000 + $37,500 + $50). After an overdelivery occurs, the maximum delivery tolerance level decreases to $50 above the new commitment amount.

examples: Original commitment $400,000: standard max delivery = $400,000 + max($10,000, $10,000) = $410,000 unless overdelivery approved.
Original commitment $150,000 with approved overdelivery: max extra = 25% = $37,500; total cap = $187,550 including $50 tolerance.
Original commitment $30,000: 25% = $7,500 (<$10,000); the high tolerance level becomes the maximum deliverable amount (per text).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Overdelivery price adjustment draft or delivery rejection

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Whole loan docs overnight same day for next-day certification
business_rules.description: Mortgage document submission packages for whole loans (and ASAP Plus/ASAP Sale) must be submitted to the assigned document custodian via overnight delivery with instructions for first morning delivery. To ensure timely delivery, the seller should submit the document package to its overnight carrier the same day the seller submits loan data to Fannie Mae for purchase. Loan data delivered will be certified the next business day provided documents were delivered via first morning delivery and there are no discrepancies and no unresolved lien issues.
business_rules.conditions: When seller submits whole loan data to Fannie Mae for purchase and custodian delivery
business_rules.consequences: If timely and error-free, certification occurs next business day; delays if late/discrepancies
business_rules.exceptions: Certification timing conditioned on no discrepancies and no unresolved lien issues

source_reference.source_text: Mortgage document submission packages must be submitted to the document custodian via overnight delivery (with instructions for ﬁrst morning delivery) for the following: whole loans, As Soon As Pooled Plus (ASAP Plus), and As Soon As Pooled (ASAP Sale)... the seller should ensure the loan document submission package is submitted to its overnight carrier (with instructions for ﬁrst morning delivery) the same day that the seller submits the loan data to Fannie Mae for purchase. Loan data delivered by the seller to Fannie Mae will be certiﬁed the next business day provided the documents have been delivered to the document custodian via ﬁrst morning delivery, and there are no discrepancies and no unresolved lien issues.

examples: Seller submits loan data Monday and tenders custodian package to overnight carrier Monday for first-morning Tuesday delivery → eligible for Tuesday (next business day) certification if no discrepancies.
Seller submits loan data Monday but ships package Tuesday → certification delayed beyond next business day.
Package arrives first morning but custodian identifies missing note endorsement → certification delayed until discrepancy resolved.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Purchase/certification delay; potential delivery failure remedies per agreements

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Servicing rights pledge approval requested 30 days prior
business_rules.description: Before entering into a pledge of servicing or a transfer of an interest in servicing income, the lender must obtain Fannie Mae’s prior written consent and must request Fannie Mae’s prior approval at least 30 days before the proposed effective date of the transaction.
business_rules.conditions: When pledging servicing rights or transferring servicing-income interest
business_rules.consequences: If requested ≥30 days prior, can proceed subject to consent; if not, noncompliant transaction risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; applies only to permitted-purpose transactions

source_reference.section_id: A3-3-03, Pledge of Servicing Rights and Transfer of Interest in Servicing Income
source_reference.source_text: A lender may enter into one of the following transactions, provided that the purpose of the transaction is a purpose permitted by Fannie Mae and the lender obtains Fannie Mae’s prior written consent: a pledge, or grant of a security interest in, the servicing rights to all or part of its Fannie Mae servicing portfolio... The lender must request Fannie Mae’s prior approval of a speciﬁc pledging transaction or transfer of an interest in servicing income at least 30 days prior to the proposed eﬀective date.

examples: On 2026-08-01 a Seller/Servicer plans a servicing-rights pledge effective 2026-09-15; it must request prior approval no later than 2026-08-16 (30 days prior).

A lender requests consent 10 days before the effective date of a transfer of excess servicing compensation; the request timing violates the 30-day requirement and must be rescheduled or otherwise corrected.
A lender obtains written consent but submits the approval request 20 days before the effective date; the lender is still noncompliant with the ≥30 day lead-time requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension or other contractual remedy

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: Payee-code security breach must be reported within 24 hours
business_rules.description: If a breach in the lender’s security arrangements (or failure to give timely notice of an error/omission/irregularity in payee arrangements) results in transfer instructions/transactions being initiated, the lender is solely liable for the transfers. The lender must notify Fannie Mae Asset Acquisitions by telephone and submit written confirmation of the call to Fannie Mae within 24 hours of the breach.
business_rules.conditions: When security breach or payee arrangement irregularity affects wire transfer instructions
business_rules.consequences: Lender remains liable for transfers; late notice may prevent suspension/correction actions
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: C2-2-03, Procedures Related to the Secure Transfer of Funds
source_reference.source_text: A lender is solely liable for transfers that are initiated (either directly or indirectly) as the result of a breach in its security arrangements or as the result of its failure to give Fannie Mae timely notice of an error, omission, or irregularity in establishing payee arrangements. The lender is fully responsible for notifying the Fannie Mae Asset Acquisitions by telephone and submitting written conﬁrmation of the call to Fannie Mae within 24 hours of the breach.

examples: Fraudster compromises lender email and submits altered wire instructions; lender must call Asset Acquisitions and provide written confirmation within 24 hours.
Lender discovers incorrect payee code set-up that could route funds improperly; lender must provide timely notice and confirm in writing within 24 hours after detection of breach/irregularity.
Vendor system breach changes payee code file; lender remains solely liable for resulting transfers and must complete the 24-hour notification steps.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Lender liability for transfers; operational suspension/removal of payee codes

examples: Fannie Mae-approved Seller/Servicer with active approval
Seller/Servicer under probationary status
Seller/Servicer terminated for breach of obligations

business_rules.rule_name: No cash wiring on positive differential after trigger events or net worth drops 20%/30%
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae will not wire cash to a lender even when there is a positive price differential if any specified triggering event has occurred, including (i) event of default under the Lender Contract or other contract, (ii) failure to meet lender eligibility requirements, (iii) adjusted net worth decline >20% over a quarterly period or >30% over two consecutive quarterly periods, (iv) total UPB of outstanding Fannie Mae repurchase requests exceeding 15% of adjusted net worth, (v) adverse information affecting counterparty relationship/financial condition, or (vi) outstanding contractual fees owed to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: When positive price differential would otherwise require Fannie Mae cash wire
business_rules.consequences: Fannie Mae withholds cash wire; may modify/eliminate thresholds without notice
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond stated trigger event list; thresholds may be changed at discretion

source_reference.section_id: C3-7-01 (10/06/2021), Triggering events / cash wire restriction
source_reference.source_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Fannie Mae will not wire cash to a lender in the event of a positive price diﬀerential if any of the following “triggering events” has occurred with respect to a lender: ... a decline in a lender’s adjusted net worth by more than 20% over a quarterly reporting period or by more than 30% over two consecutive quarterly periods, the total unpaid principal balance of all outstanding Fannie Mae repurchase requests to a lender exceeds 15% of the lender’s adjusted net worth, ... outstanding contractual fees owed by a lender to Fannie Mae.

examples: Lender is in the money by $200,000 but had a 25% adjusted net worth decline last quarter; Fannie Mae does not wire cash.
Outstanding repurchase requests UPB equals 18% of adjusted net worth; positive price differential exists; no cash wire is made.
Lender owes contractual fees to Fannie Mae; even with positive price differential, Fannie Mae withholds wire.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Withhold margin cash; modify thresholds; counterparty action

examples: Fannie Mae Selling Guide (Published March 4, 2026)
Fannie Mae QC process for purchased or securitized loans
Fannie Mae remedies including compensatory fees or loan repurchase

business_rules.rule_name: No purchase if private transfer fee covenant created on or after Feb 8, 2011
business_rules.description: Except as permitted by the FHFA Private Transfer Fee Regulation (12 CFR Part 1228), Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants created on or after February 8, 2011. Lenders must maintain policies/procedures to ensure delivered loans are not secured by unacceptable private transfer fees under the regulation.
business_rules.conditions: When subject property is encumbered by a private transfer fee covenant
business_rules.consequences: If unacceptable covenant exists, loan is ineligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: Excepted transfer fee covenants (as defined) are eligible

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize loans on properties encumbered by private transfer fee covenants if those covenants were created on or after February 8, 2011, unless permitted by the Private Transfer Fee Regulation. The lender must establish policies and/or procedures to ensure that the loans it delivers to Fannie Mae, whether or not the loans were originated by the lender, are not secured by properties encumbered with a private transfer fee that is unacceptable under the Private Transfer Fee Regulation.

examples: HOA covenant recorded March 1, 2012 requires 1% fee on each resale; unless it qualifies as an excepted covenant, loan is ineligible.
Property has a shared equity transaction fee covenant that meets “excepted transfer fee covenant” definition; loan may be eligible.
Lender QC finds a private transfer fee created in 2015; lender must block delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility; possible repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: SFHA property in non-NFIP community is ineligible
business_rules.description: If a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA and the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan secured by the property is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If the property is in a non-participating community and also in a CBRS/OPA, it is only eligible if it is not located in an SFHA.
business_rules.conditions: Property has principal/residential structure in SFHA; community is non-NFIP
business_rules.consequences: Meets: eligible only if not in SFHA (CBRS/OPA case); Violated: ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: CBRS/OPA non-participating: eligible only when not in SFHA

source_reference.source_text: Community Eligibility If the lender or servicer determines that a principal or residential detached structure is located in an SFHA, but the community does not participate in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), the loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae. If the property is in a non-participating community and a CBRS or an OPA, it is only eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae if it is not located in an SFHA.

examples: SFR in Zone AE; town does not participate in NFIP → loan ineligible for delivery.
Property in CBRS and community is non-NFIP; FEMA zone is X (not SFHA) → eligible if other requirements met.
Property in CBRS and Zone VE (SFHA) in non-NFIP community → ineligible.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Direct ineligibility statement tied to NFIP participation and SFHA status.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Condo SFHA master flood coverage minimum 80% RCV
business_rules.description: For an attached condo building located in an SFHA, the lender/servicer must verify the HOA maintains an RCBAP (or equivalent private flood insurance). The HOA master policy must cover the entire building and common elements. The building coverage amount must be at least the lesser of 80% of the replacement cost value or the maximum NFIP coverage available per unit. If the master policy meets the 80% replacement-cost requirement but the per-unit amount is insufficient versus one- to four-unit requirements, the unit owner must maintain a supplemental policy for the difference.
business_rules.conditions: Loan secured by unit in attached condo project in SFHA where flood required
business_rules.consequences: Meets: acceptable project flood coverage; Violated: loan ineligible/delivery defect
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental unit policy required when per-unit amount is short

source_reference.source_text: The master ﬂood insurance policy maintained by the HOA must cover the subject unit's • entire building; and • all of the common elements and property, including machinery and equipment that are part of the building. The coverage amount for the building must be at least equal to the lesser of • 80% of the replacement cost value(1), or • the maximum coverage amount available from NFIP per unit(2). (1)If the master ﬂood insurance policy meets the minimum coverage requirement of 80% of the replacement cost value, but the per unit coverage amount does not meet the requirement for loans secured by one- to four-unit properties, as described in Requirements for One- to Four-Unit Properties , the unit owner must maintain a supplemental policy for the diﬀerence.

examples: RCV $1,000,000; 80%=$800,000; NFIP per-unit max totals $900,000 → HOA must carry ≥$800,000 building coverage.
Master policy meets 80% RCV but per-unit allocation is short vs one- to four-unit minimum → borrower must add supplemental flood policy.
HOA policy excludes machinery/equipment common elements → fails coverage requirement; loan has delivery defect risk.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit numeric threshold (80% RCV) and lesser-of test; includes supplemental-policy condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: ADU limited to one and requires stove hookup minimum
business_rules.description: To classify a property as a one-unit dwelling with an accessory dwelling unit (ADU), only one ADU is permitted on the parcel and ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling. The ADU kitchen must, at minimum, have cabinets, a countertop, a sink with running water, and a stove or stove hookup; hotplates, microwaves, or toaster ovens are not acceptable stove substitutes.
business_rules.conditions: Property evaluated as one-unit with ADU; ADU kitchen and unit-count reviewed
business_rules.consequences: Meets: ADU classification allowed; Violated: property not eligible as 1-unit+ADU
business_rules.exceptions: None stated (classification requirements are mandatory)

source_reference.source_text: The following table describes the requirements for classifying an ADU. ✓ Requirements • Only one ADU is permitted on the parcel of the primary one-unit dwelling. • ADUs are not permitted with a two- to four-unit dwelling. • The kitchen must, at a minimum, contain the following: ⚬ cabinets; ⚬ a countertop; ⚬ a sink with running water; and ⚬ a stove or stove hookup (hotplates, microwaves, or toaster ovens are not acceptable stove substitutes).

examples: 1-unit SFR with garage apartment having cabinets/sink/stove hookup → eligible as 1-unit with ADU if otherwise compliant.
Duplex with detached studio → ADU not permitted with 2-unit dwelling; cannot classify as ADU under this rule.
Basement unit has microwave only (no stove/hookup) → fails ADU kitchen minimum; cannot be treated as ADU.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear 'only one' and explicit minimum kitchen components with prohibited substitutes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase if delivered

examples: One-unit single-family detached primary residence
Condominium unit in an eligible project
Property affected by a declared disaster requiring additional review

business_rules.rule_name: Hawaii lava zones eligible only in zones 3 through 9
business_rules.description: Mortgage loans secured by properties on the island of Hawaii are only eligible for purchase/securitization if the property is located in lava zones 3 through 9. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to increased risk of destruction from lava flows.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property is located on the island of Hawaii; lava zone identified
business_rules.consequences: Meets: property eligible; Violated: property in lava zone 1-2 is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Hawaiian Lava Zones Fannie Mae will only purchase or securitize mortgage loans secured by properties that are located within lava zones 3 through 9 on the island of Hawaii. Properties in lava zones 1 and 2 are not eligible due to the increased risk of property destruction from lava ﬂows within these areas.

examples: Hilo property in lava zone 3 → eligible (subject to all other underwriting requirements).
Puna district property in lava zone 2 → ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
Kona property in lava zone 9 → eligible under lava zone rule (other eligibility still applies).
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit zone range (3-9) and explicit ineligibility for zones 1-2.
