The mortgage transaction may be a purchase or a reﬁnance transaction. HUD Section 184 mortgages that are subject to an interest rate buydown plan are not permitted.

examples: Section 184 purchase loan includes a temporary interest rate buydown agreement → not permitted; do not deliver to Fannie Mae as Section 184.
Section 184 refinance has no buydown and meets HUD requirements → passes this rule, continues through other eligibility checks.
Lender attempts to offset a buydown by qualifying at note rate → still prohibited for Section 184; loan remains ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Section 184 delivery must report SFC 202 and Assumption Indicator True
business_rules.description: When selling HUD Section 184 (and 184A) loans to Fannie Mae, the lender must report Special Feature Code (SFC) 202 and must set the Assumption Indicator in the Loan Delivery application to "True" to reflect that Section 184 loans are assumable as of the note date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering HUD-guaranteed Section 184/184A loans to Fannie Mae via Loan Delivery
business_rules.consequences: If correctly reported, delivery data meets program delivery requirements; if misreported, delivery defects may require correction and can trigger purchase/delivery issues
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B6-1-04, Delivery of HUD-Guaranteed Section 184 Mortgages
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must report SFC 202 when selling HUD Section 184 (and 184A) loans to Fannie Mae. Section 184 loans are assumable as of the note date. When selling such loans to Fannie Mae, the Assumption Indicator in the Loan Delivery application must be "True" (which means assumable).

examples: Lender delivers a Section 184 loan and reports SFC 202; Assumption Indicator set to True → compliant delivery coding.
Lender forgets SFC 202 even though loan is Section 184 → delivery data defect; must correct prior to/after delivery per Fannie Mae processes.
Assumption Indicator is set to False for a Section 184 loan → misrepresents assumability; must be corrected to True.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery suspense/curative action; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady counseling credit needs 12-month completion, DU data, SFC 184
business_rules.description: For HomeReady loans, borrowers who complete housing counseling within 12 months prior to closing may be eligible for a loan-level price adjustment (LLPA) credit. To apply the LLPA credit, the Housing Counseling data must be submitted to DU and the loan must be delivered with Special Feature Code (SFC) 184.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering a HomeReady loan seeking the housing counseling LLPA credit
business_rules.consequences: If DU data or SFC 184 missing, LLPA credit cannot be applied as described
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (this is an optional credit; base eligibility unaffected if not taken)

source_reference.source_text: HomeReady loans where borrowers who complete housing counseling within 12 months prior to closing may be eligible for a loan-level price adjustment credit. For the LLPA credit to be applied to the loan the Housing Counseling data must be submitted to DU, and the loan must be delivered with SFC 184.

examples: HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 6 months before closing; counseling data submitted to DU; loan delivered with SFC 184: LLPA credit eligible.
HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 10 months before closing; data not submitted to DU: LLPA credit not applied.
HomeReady purchase: counseling completed 14 months before closing: outside 12-month window; LLPA credit not eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pricing adjustment not granted / delivery data correction

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary buydown limit is 3% down and 1% per year up for 3 years
business_rules.description: Temporary interest rate buydowns are permitted only if the buydown terms stay within Fannie Mae limits: the rate reduction must not exceed 3%, the rate increase must not exceed 1% per year, and the buydown period must not be greater than 3 years. More frequent changes are permitted only if the total annual increase does not exceed 1%.
business_rules.conditions: When a mortgage loan includes a temporary interest rate buydown plan
business_rules.consequences: If limits exceeded, loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization as described
business_rules.exceptions: More frequent changes allowed if total annual increase ≤ 1%

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.4-04 (General Requirements; Terms of the Buydown)
source_reference.source_text: Temporary interest rate buydowns are allowed on ﬁxed-rate mortgages and certain ARM plans for principal residences or second homes provided the rate reduction does not exceed 3%, and the rate increase will not exceed 1% per year. ... An interest rate buydown plan must provide for: a buydown period not greater than 3 years, and increases of not more than 1% in the portion of the interest rate paid by the borrower in each 1-year interval. More frequent changes are permitted as long as the total annual increase does not exceed 1%.

examples: 2-1 buydown: note rate 6.00%, year 1 at 4.00% (2% reduction), year 2 at 5.00% (1% increase), year 3 at 6.00%: compliant.
3-2-1 buydown from 7.00% to 4.00% in year 1 (3% reduction) then 5.00% then 6.00%: compliant if total period ≤ 3 years.
Buydown drops rate 4% below note rate or has a 4-year buydown period: noncompliant/ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase for eligibility breach

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Buydown account must be fully funded before delivery and cannot pay past due
business_rules.description: For loans with temporary interest rate buydowns, the lender must establish and fully fund the buydown account by the time the lender submits the mortgage to Fannie Mae for purchase or securitization. Buydown funds must be deposited into custodial bank accounts and cannot be included in accounts with the lender’s other corporate funds. Buydown funds cannot be used to pay past-due payments and cannot be used to reduce the mortgage amount for purposes of determining the LTV ratio.
business_rules.conditions: When submitting a temporary buydown loan to Fannie Mae for purchase/securitization
business_rules.consequences: If not funded/custodial or misused, delivery/eligibility defect and remedy risk
business_rules.exceptions: Refundability addressed only if paid off early (not covered in this rule)

source_reference.source_text: Buydown Funds ... Buydown accounts must be established and fully funded by the time the lender submits the mortgage to Fannie Mae for purchase or securitization. Funds for buydown accounts must be deposited into custodial bank accounts. Note: Buydown funds cannot be included in accounts with the lender’s other corporate funds. ... Buydown funds cannot be used to pay past-due payments. Buydown funds cannot be used to reduce the mortgage amount for purposes of determining the LTV ratio.

examples: Lender funds the buydown escrow account at closing and maintains it as a separate custodial account before delivery: compliant.
Lender delivers the loan while buydown account is partially funded: noncompliant; subject to delivery defect/remedy.
Borrower becomes 30 days past due; servicer attempts to use remaining buydown funds to cure delinquency: prohibited.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection / repurchase or make-whole remedy

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Green MBS requires 10% efficiency and 5-year certification
business_rules.description: To qualify as a Green MBS loan, the mortgage must be secured by a property that received an approved green building certification within the last five years, and the certification must require the home to be at least 10% more efficient than a home built to the applicable state's energy code.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender intends to deliver the loan as Green MBS
business_rules.consequences: If met, loan may be treated as Green MBS; if not, cannot be Green MBS
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae's Green MBS consists of loans secured by properties that have received approved green building certiﬁcations within the last ﬁve years. An eligible green building certiﬁcation requires the home to be at least 10% more eﬃcient than a home built to an individual state's energy code.

examples: Property certified in 2023 and documentation shows ≥10% efficiency vs state code; loan may be delivered as Green MBS.
Property certified in 2018 (more than five years ago); loan cannot be delivered as Green MBS.
Property has an efficiency label but it does not require ≥10% improvement over state code; loan cannot be delivered as Green MBS.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for Green MBS designation

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Green loans must be sold or pooled within 5 years of certification
business_rules.description: For Green whole loans, the loan must be sold within five years of the property's certification year. For Green MBS pools, the pool must have a pool issuance year within five years of the property's certification year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering a loan as Green whole loan sale or Green MBS
business_rules.consequences: If met, Green delivery timing is satisfied; if not, loan/pool not Green eligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For whole loans: The loan must be sold within ﬁve years of the property's certiﬁcation year. For loans in MBS: The pool must have a pool issuance year within ﬁve years of the property's certiﬁcation year. Example : For a loan secured by a property with a green building certiﬁcation received in the year 2020, the loan must be sold to Fannie Mae by the end of 2025.

examples: Certification year 2020 and whole loan sold by 2025-12-31; timing requirement is met.
Certification year 2020 but whole loan sold in 2026; loan cannot be delivered as Green whole loan.
Certification year 2022 but MBS pool issuance year 2028; pool cannot be treated as Green MBS.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for Green MBS designation

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Green MBS must be 100% eligible and include SFC 784 or 881
business_rules.description: To be considered a Green MBS, 100% of the loans in the MBS must be Green MBS-eligible. The lender must deliver one of the specified special feature codes: SFC 784 (ENERGY STAR green building certification) or SFC 881 (Department of Energy Zero Ready Home), in addition to any other SFCs that apply.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when delivering an MBS pool as a Green MBS
business_rules.consequences: If met, pool can be considered Green MBS; if not, pool is not Green MBS
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: To be considered a Green MBS, 100% of the loans in the MBS must be eligible. One of the following special feature codes must be delivered in addition to any other SFCs that may apply to the transaction: SFC 784 - ENERGY STAR green building certiﬁcation, or SFC 881 - Department of Energy Zero Ready Home.

examples: Pool of 50 loans where all 50 meet Green certification rules and each loan includes SFC 784; pool can be Green MBS.
Pool includes 1 non-eligible loan (49/50 eligible); pool cannot be treated as Green MBS even if other loans have SFCs.
All loans eligible but lender omits SFC 784/881 in delivery data; pool is not considered Green MBS until corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery data correction required; Green MBS designation denied

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative path required when P&I increases by more than 20%
business_rules.description: A high LTV refinance option loan must comply with the Alternative Qualification Path requirements if any specified trigger applies to the new loan, including when the P&I payment increases by more than 20% from the current P&I payment, when a borrower is excluded without death and remaining borrower(s) cannot evidence making payments on their own for the prior 12 months, or when the loan is a higher-priced mortgage loan/higher-priced covered transaction under Regulation Z.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to high LTV refinance option loans when listed triggers occur
business_rules.consequences: If triggered, lender must follow Alternative Path; if not followed, loan is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: Exclusion due to death is excluded from the trigger

source_reference.source_text: If any of the following apply to the new loan... Then the loan... the P&I payment increases by more than 20% from the current P&I payment must comply with the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path requirements. a borrower on the loan being reﬁnanced is being excluded from the new loan other than due to death, and the remaining borrower(s) cannot provide evidence of making payments on their own for the prior 12 months the loan is a higher-priced mortgage loan or a higher-priced covered transaction under Regulation Z

examples: Current P&I is $1,200 and new P&I is $1,500 (+25%); loan must be underwritten using Alternative Qualification Path rules.
A co-borrower is removed for divorce (not death) and remaining borrower cannot document making payments alone for prior 12 months; Alternative Path is required.
Refinance is classified as a higher-priced covered transaction under Reg Z; Alternative Path is required even if P&I change is small.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative Qualification Path minimum credit score is 620
business_rules.description: Loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path for the high LTV refinance option must meet a minimum credit score of 620.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to high LTV refinance loans using the Alternative Qualification Path
business_rules.consequences: If score ≥620, requirement is satisfied; if <620, loan is not eligible under the path
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: In addition to all other requirements associated with the high LTV reﬁnance option loans, loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path must also meet the requirements described in the following table. Minimum credit score of 620. Maximum DTI ratio of 45%.

examples: Borrower representative credit score is 640; passes the Alternative Qualification Path credit score requirement.
Borrower representative credit score is 619; fails the Alternative Qualification Path and cannot be originated under it.
Two borrowers with scores 650 and 625; minimum score requirement is met because each required score is ≥620.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative Qualification Path maximum debt-to-income ratio is 45%
business_rules.description: Loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path for the high LTV refinance option must meet a maximum DTI ratio of 45%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to high LTV refinance loans using the Alternative Qualification Path
business_rules.consequences: If DTI ≤45%, requirement is satisfied; if >45%, loan is not eligible under the path
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: loans originated in accordance with the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path must also meet the requirements described in the following table. Minimum credit score of 620. Maximum DTI ratio of 45%. Veriﬁed assets needed to close, when applicable.

examples: DTI calculated at 44.9%; passes the Alternative Qualification Path DTI cap.
DTI calculated at 45.1%; fails the Alternative Qualification Path DTI cap and is not eligible under it.
DTI at 45% exactly; passes because requirement is a maximum of 45%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Alternative Qualification Path loans require manual underwriting in 2025
business_rules.description: For loans subject to the Alternative Qualification Path, the lender is required to manually underwrite the loan (i.e., manual underwriting is required for all loans subject to the path).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when high LTV refinance loan is subject to Alternative Qualification Path
business_rules.consequences: If performed, underwriting method complies; if not, loan is noncompliant/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Veriﬁed assets needed to close, when applicable. The lender is required to manually underwrite all loans subject to the Alternative Qualiﬁcation Path.

examples: P&I increases by 22%; lender completes a full manual underwrite and documents the file accordingly; requirement is met.
Loan is HPML under Reg Z and lender relies solely on DU Approve/Eligible without manual underwriting; requirement is violated.
Borrower excluded (not due to death) and remaining borrower lacks 12-month solo payment evidence; manual underwriting is required under the path.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Purchase cash back limited to refunds and tax credits
business_rules.description: For purchase money transactions, proceeds may not be used to give the borrower cash back except (1) reimbursement for overpayment of fees/charges (including federally required refunds) when the settlement statement clearly indicates the refund and the loan file documents the amount and reason; and (2) a legitimate prorated real estate tax credit where taxes are paid in arrears. If permissible cash back is provided, the lender must confirm minimum borrower contribution requirements are met. A prorated tax credit is not an interested party contribution and cannot be considered when determining sufficient borrower assets. Exception: if the Closing Disclosure shows an escrow account established that includes the seller’s portion of taxes, a prorated tax credit may offset escrow funds needed.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase transactions (purchase money) with borrower receiving cash back
business_rules.consequences: If cash back exceeds allowed purposes, loan is ineligible for purchase/delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Escrow setup on Closing Disclosure may allow tax credit to offset escrow funds

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-01, General Purchase Transaction Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: Proceeds from the transaction may not be used to give the borrower cash back other than the following: • an amount representing reimbursement for the borrower’s overpayment of fees and charges, including refunds that may be required in accordance with certain federal laws or regulations. The settlement statement must clearly indicate the refund, and the loan file must include documentation to support the amount and reason for the refund; and • a legitimate prorated real estate tax credit in locales where real estate taxes are paid in arrears. Note: If the borrower receives cash back for a permissible purpose as listed above, the lender must confirm the minimum borrower contribution requirements associated with the selected mortgage product, if any, have been met. A prorated real estate tax credit is not an interested party contribution, and it cannot be considered when determining if the borrower has sufficient assets for the transaction. Exception: When the Closing Disclosure indicates an escrow account has been established and includes the portion of real estate taxes owed by the property seller for the period they owned the property, a prorated real estate tax credit from the property seller may offset all or a portion of the funds needed for the escrow account.

examples: Borrower receives $450 back because appraisal fee was collected twice; settlement statement shows refund and file documents the reason—allowed.
Borrower receives $5,000 cash back as an inducement not tied to documented overpayment or tax proration—prohibited; loan ineligible.
Closing Disclosure shows escrow setup includes seller’s tax portion; seller tax credit is applied to escrow funds—permitted by exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Clear prohibition with explicit allowed cash-back categories and documentation requirements; no numeric caps stated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: 95.01–97% purchase requires FRM 30 years and DU
business_rules.description: If a purchase transaction has LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV exceeding 95% (95.01% to 97%), the loan must meet additional eligibility requirements: (a) ratio must be 95.01–97%; (b) loan must be a fixed-rate loan with terms up to 30 years; high-balance and ARM loans are not permitted; (c) property must be a one-unit principal residence (manufactured housing not permitted unless it meets MH Advantage requirements); (d) at least one borrower must be a first-time homebuyer (Form 1003 Declarations—ownership interest in last three years answered “No” by at least one borrower); and (e) underwriting method must be DU only. Note: CLTV can be up to 105% if subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Note: does not apply to HomeReady.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase loans where LTV/CLTV/HCLTV is 95.01%–97% inclusive
business_rules.consequences: If any requirement fails, the loan is ineligible for this high-LTV purchase set
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to HomeReady loans; Community Seconds allows CLTV to 105%

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.3-01, Requirements for Purchase Transactions with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratios of 95.01–97%
source_reference.source_text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a purchase transaction, the following requirements apply. Criteria Requirements LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio 95.01 to 97% Note: The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan. Loan Type Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years. Note: High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted. Property and Occupancy One-unit principal residence. Manufactured housing is not permitted, unless the property meets the MH Advantage requirements. Borrower Eligibility At least one borrower must be a first-time homebuyer, as indicated on the Form 1003 in the Declarations section, when at least one borrower responds “No” to the question about having an ownership interest in a property in the last three years. Underwriting Method DU only

examples: Purchase loan at 97% LTV, fixed-rate 30-year, one-unit principal residence, DU Approve/Eligible, with one first-time homebuyer—eligible under this section.
Purchase loan at 96% LTV but ARM product—prohibited; must change product or reduce LTV below threshold.
Purchase loan at 97% LTV with Community Seconds subordinate lien and CLTV 104%—allowed due to Community Seconds exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Highly specific ratio band and product/property/underwriting requirements; includes explicit exceptions/notes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: New construction non-arm’s length bars 2nd/investment
business_rules.description: For newly constructed properties, if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation (any ownership interest, or employment) with the builder, developer, or seller, Fannie Mae will only purchase loans secured by a principal residence. Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans on newly constructed homes secured by a second home or investment property in that scenario.
business_rules.conditions: Purchase of newly constructed property with borrower affiliation to builder/seller
business_rules.consequences: If occupancy is second home/investment, loan is not eligible for purchase
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For the purchase of newly constructed properties, if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation (any ownership interest, or employment) with the builder, developer, or seller of the property, Fannie Mae will only purchase mortgage loans secured by a principal residence. Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans on newly constructed homes secured by a second home or investment property if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation with the builder, developer, or seller of the property.

examples: Borrower is an employee of the builder and buys a newly built home as a principal residence—eligible (subject to other rules).
Borrower is a partial owner of the development entity and intends to occupy as a second home—not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
Borrower has a business affiliation with the seller and the loan is investment occupancy—Fannie Mae will not purchase.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit prohibition tied to occupancy type for a defined non-arm’s length new construction scenario.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster refi allowed only in FEMA IA counties and PR
business_rules.description: Disaster-related limited cash-out refinance flexibilities may be applied only to properties located in areas designated by FEMA as eligible for Individual Assistance (FEMA Disaster Areas) and are applicable only to loans secured by the borrower’s principal residence (not second homes or investment properties). A borrower may obtain a limited cash-out refinance to consolidate non-purchase money subordinate financing used for disaster-related repairs if the subordinate financing (including draws on an existing HELOC) post-dates the disaster. The borrower may pay off the entire HELOC through the limited cash-out refinance, provided a portion of the amount was used for disaster-related expenses to repair damage to the principal residence.
business_rules.conditions: Limited cash-out refi using disaster flexibilities under B5-4.2-02
business_rules.consequences: If location/occupancy/timing not met, loan must follow standard refi rules
business_rules.exceptions: Entire HELOC payoff allowed if some proceeds used for disaster repairs

source_reference.section_id: B5-4.2-02, Location of Property; Occupancy Status; Transaction Types
source_reference.source_text: These flexibilities may be applied to loans on properties located in any counties, cities, or parishes that are designated by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) as eligible for Individual Assistance as a result of a natural disaster (these areas are referred to as “FEMA Disaster Areas”). These guidelines are applicable only to loans secured by the borrower’s principal residence, and may not be used in connection with second homes or investment properties. A borrower may obtain: a limited cash-out refinance to consolidate non-purchase money subordinate financing used for repair of disaster-related property damage to their principal residence. To be eligible, the subordinate financing, including any draws on an existing HELOC, must post-date the disaster. However, the borrower may pay off the entire HELOC through the limited cash-out refinance, provided that a portion of the amount was used for disaster-related expenses to repair property damage to the principal residence.

examples: Property is in a FEMA Individual Assistance county; borrower’s principal residence; HELOC draw for repairs occurred after the disaster—eligible to apply flexibilities.
Borrower seeks to use flexibilities on a second home in a FEMA IA area—not eligible; must underwrite under standard limited cash-out rules.
Borrower pays off entire HELOC in the refi; documentation shows part of HELOC was for disaster repairs—allowed by exception.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit FEMA IA geographic constraint, principal-residence-only requirement, and post-date timing test for subordinate financing/HELOC draws.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for flex; potential repurchase demand if delivered incorrectly

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Disaster repair reimbursement cap is 10% or $15,000
business_rules.description: Under disaster-related limited cash-out refinance flexibilities, cash-out for reimbursement of documented out-of-pocket expenses for completed disaster-related repairs is limited to an amount not to exceed the lesser of 10% of the balance of the new refinance loan or $15,000. The lender must document that subordinate financing/HELOC portion or the entire requested cash-out amount represents funds used for completed disaster-related property repairs (e.g., receipts, work orders, canceled checks). The borrower may not receive reimbursement for sweat equity. All documentation must post-date the disaster and be directly related to completed repairs of disaster-caused damage.
business_rules.conditions: Disaster-related limited cash-out refi requesting repair reimbursement cash-out
business_rules.consequences: If cap exceeded or documentation fails, reduce cash-out or treat as ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B5-4.2-02, Transaction Types; Documentation of Eligible Disaster-Related Expenses and Financing
source_reference.source_text: cash-out for reimbursement of documented out-of-pocket expenses for the completed repair of disaster-related property damage to their principal residence in an amount not to exceed the lesser of 10% of the balance of the new refinance loan or $15,000. The lender must document that the subordinate financing (or a portion of the HELOC) or the entire requested cash-out amount represents funds used for completed disaster-related property repairs. Generally, documentation includes copies of receipts, work orders, canceled checks, etc., related to the cost of materials and labor. The borrower may not receive any reimbursement for amounts representing their sweat equity in connection with the repairs. Note: All documentation must post-date the disaster and be directly related to completed repairs of damage to the property resulting from the disaster.

examples: New refi loan balance is $200,000; max reimbursement is lesser of 10% ($20,000) or $15,000 → $15,000 cap applies.
New refi loan balance is $120,000; 10% is $12,000 → max reimbursement is $12,000 with post-disaster receipts/work orders.
Borrower claims $8,000 value of self-performed labor (sweat equity) with no paid invoices; reimbursement is prohibited and must be excluded.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Contains explicit numeric cap (10% or $15,000) plus explicit documentation/timing and sweat-equity prohibition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Delinquent taxes and liens must be paid off by closing
business_rules.description: Delinquent credit items that could affect Fannie Mae’s lien position or diminish borrower equity—including taxes, judgments, charge-offs of non-mortgage accounts (subject to stated exceptions), tax liens, mechanic’s/materialmen’s liens, and similar liens—must be paid off at or prior to closing.
business_rules.conditions: When delinquent credit (taxes/judgments/liens) exists and could affect lien position/equity
business_rules.consequences: If paid off by closing, loan may proceed; if not paid, loan is not compliant and may be ineligible/subject to repurchase
business_rules.exceptions: Manual underwrite thresholds for non-medical collections/charge-offs (see separate rule)

source_reference.section_id: B3-6-07, Collections, Charge-Offs, Judgments, and Liens
source_reference.source_text: Delinquent credit—including taxes, judgments, charge-oﬀs of non-mortgage accounts (see below for exceptions), tax liens, mechanic's or materialmen’s liens, and liens that have the potential to aﬀect Fannie Mae’s lien position or diminish the borrower’s equity—must be paid oﬀ at or prior to closing.

examples: Borrower has a recorded mechanic’s lien; lender requires payoff and lien release prior to or at closing.
Borrower has delinquent property taxes; taxes are paid off at closing and documented on the closing statement.
Borrower has an outstanding judgment; lender must obtain satisfaction of judgment at or prior to closing to comply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: PACE Loan for Solar Panels Must Be Paid Off at Closing
business_rules.description: If the subject property has solar panels or other energy efficient items financed with a PACE loan, the mortgage loan is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae unless the PACE loan is paid in full prior to or at closing.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property has solar/energy items financed with a PACE loan
business_rules.consequences: If unpaid, loan is ineligible for delivery; if paid off, standard delivery may proceed
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; must be paid in full prior to or at closing

source_reference.source_text: Properties with solar panels and other energy eﬃcient items ﬁnanced with a PACE loan are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if the PACE loan is not paid in full prior to or at closing. For additional information, see B5-3.4-01, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans .

examples: Purchase loan: borrower has a $18,000 PACE balance for solar panels; payoff is not completed at closing → loan is not eligible for delivery.
Purchase loan: PACE payoff is funded and recorded at closing (PACE lien released) → loan may be eligible, subject to standard requirements.
Rate-term refinance: borrower still has an active PACE assessment and will not pay it off → loan is ineligible for delivery.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility for delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Senior UCC Fixture Filing for Solar Panels Must Be Subordinated
business_rules.description: When solar panels are financed and treated as fixtures via a UCC fixture filing recorded in land records, any UCC fixture filing that is senior in priority to the mortgage loan must be subordinated to preserve first-lien position.
business_rules.conditions: Solar panel financing includes a UCC fixture filing recorded in land records
business_rules.consequences: If not subordinated, first-lien position may be impaired and loan may be noncompliant/ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-04 (Solar panels underwriting table: fixture filing)
source_reference.source_text: Note: If a UCC ﬁxture ﬁling* is in the land records as a priority senior to the mortgage loan, it must be subordinated.

examples: Title shows a recorded UCC-1 fixture filing for solar panels dated before the mortgage; lender obtains executed subordination agreement → compliant.
UCC fixture filing remains senior and cannot be subordinated → lender should not deliver the loan to Fannie Mae due to lien priority risk.
Solar financing is paid off and fixture filing is terminated/released before closing → no subordination needed because no senior fixture lien remains.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility for delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Solar Debt Not on Title Must Be in DTI but Excluded from LTV and CLTV
business_rules.description: If solar panels are financed and collateralized by separate non-mortgage debt but do not appear on the title report (i.e., treated as personal property collateral), the lender must include the solar debt obligation in the DTI ratio, instruct the appraiser to provide no contributory value for the panels, and exclude the panels and related debt from the LTV and CLTV calculations.
business_rules.conditions: Solar panels financed/collateralized by non-mortgage debt that does not appear on title
business_rules.consequences: If included improperly, ratios and collateral value are misstated; may trigger QC defects/repurchase risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-3-04 (Solar panels underwriting table: not on title)
source_reference.source_text: Financed and collateralized -- the solar panels are reported to be collateral for separate (non- mortgage) debt used to purchase the panels, but do not appear on the title report• Obtain and review documentation suﬃcient to conﬁrm the terms of the secured loan (such as copies of the credit report, title report, any UCC ﬁnancing statement, related promissory note or related security agreement); • Include the debt obligation in the DTI ratio calculation; • Instruct the appraiser not to provide contributory value of the solar panels towards the appraised value because the panels are collateral for another debt; • Not include the panels in the LTV ratio calculation; and • Not include the debt in the other debt secured by the real estate in the CLTV ratio calculation

examples: Borrower has $150/month solar loan (panels treated as personal property; no title record). Lender counts $150 in DTI; appraiser gives $0 contributory value; LTV uses appraised value excluding solar value → compliant.
Lender omits solar payment from DTI because it is not on title → DTI understated; QC defect likely and delivery risk.
Appraiser adds $12,000 to value for the solar array even though panels are collateral for a separate debt → appraisal value overstated and noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Annual Property Insurance Verification Required for Eligibility
business_rules.description: The servicer must verify at least annually that any property insurance policy for a one- to four-unit property or a unit in a project development meets Fannie Mae's requirements. If a lender is unable to determine whether the applicable insurance policy meets Fannie Mae's requirements, the associated loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Loan requires property insurance for 1–4 unit or project unit collateral
business_rules.consequences: If verified annually and compliant, eligible; if unverifiable/noncompliant, loan is ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The servicer must verify at least annually that any property insurance policy for a one- to four-unit property or a unit in a project development meets Fannie Mae's requirements. Note:  If a lender is unable to determine whether the applicable insurance policy meets Fannie Mae's requirements, the associated loan is not eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae.

examples: Servicer performs annual review confirming coverage amount, deductibles, and paid premiums; policy meets requirements and loan remains eligible.
Lender receives an incomplete insurance binder and cannot determine whether terms meet requirements; loan becomes ineligible for purchase.
Project unit: annual verification confirms master policy covers unit; requirement satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: ARM becomes non-assumable after conversion to fixed
business_rules.description: If an ARM converts to a fixed-rate loan per the borrower’s exercise of a conversion option, the mortgage is no longer assumable. Lenders must disclose to borrowers that any ARM plan that includes an option to convert to a fixed-rate mortgage cannot be assumed once the conversion option is exercised.
business_rules.conditions: ARM includes conversion option and conversion option is exercised
business_rules.consequences: If met, assumption is prohibited; if violated, compliance defect risk
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Once the ARM plan converts to a ﬁxed-rate loan, the mortgage is no longer assumable. Disclosures Regarding Assumption of ARMs Although Fannie Mae ARMs are usually assumable, some plans do restrict assumability. When assumptions are restricted, the lender must advise the borrower of the exact nature of the restriction(s). Note : Lenders must disclose to borrowers that any ARM plan that includes an option to convert to a ﬁxed-rate mortgage cannot be assumed once the conversion option is exercised.

examples: Borrower converts a 7/1 ARM to fixed; a buyer later seeks to assume it—assumption must be denied as non-assumable post-conversion.
Loan docs disclose that conversion triggers non-assumability; borrower acknowledges at closing (compliant).
Servicer permits assumption after conversion; this violates the prohibition and may trigger remedies.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory arbitration loans after 10/31/2004 are ineligible
business_rules.description: A loan originated on or after October 31, 2004 that is subject to mandatory arbitration is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: When loan originated date is on/after 2004-10-31 and has mandatory arbitration
business_rules.consequences: If arbitration clause applies, loan is ineligible for delivery; must be excluded from sale
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: ArbitrationA loan that was originated on or after October 31, 2004, and is subject to mandatory arbitration is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: A loan closed on 2018-05-10 contains a mandatory arbitration rider; it is ineligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
A loan closed on 2003-09-01 contains a mandatory arbitration clause; this specific ineligibility test does not apply based on date (other restrictions may still apply).
A 2026 loan file has an arbitration provision but it is optional (not mandatory); the mandatory-arbitration ineligibility is not triggered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Financed borrower MI ineligible for 2-4 units and cash-out
business_rules.description: Mortgage loans that include financed borrower-purchased mortgage insurance are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if they are secured by a two- to four-unit property, secured by an investment property, or are a cash-out refinance loan.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower-paid MI premium is included in the loan amount (financed MI)
business_rules.consequences: If 2–4 units, investment, or cash-out refinance, loan is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; note clarifies lender-paid MI premiums cannot be financed

source_reference.source_text: The following mortgage loans are not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae if they include ﬁnanced borrower- purchased mortgage insurance: mortgage loans secured by two- to-four-unit properties, mortgage loans secured by investment properties, and cash-out reﬁnance loans. Note : Lender-paid mortgage insurance premiums cannot be ﬁnanced into the loan amount and are therefore not considered ﬁnanced mortgage insurance transactions.

examples: A 3-unit primary residence purchase includes a financed single-premium MI amount in the loan; it is ineligible for delivery.
A 1-unit investment property limited cash-out refinance includes financed MI; it is ineligible due to investment occupancy.
A 1-unit primary residence cash-out refinance includes financed MI; it is ineligible due to cash-out refinance purpose.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Whole loan first payment date within 2 months of disbursement
business_rules.description: For whole loans delivered to Fannie Mae, the first payment date must be no later than two months from the final disbursement date of the loan proceeds. For a single-close construction-to-permanent loan, the two-month period begins at the time of conversion to permanent financing.
business_rules.conditions: When delivering a whole loan to Fannie Mae and setting the first payment date
business_rules.consequences: If first payment date exceeds 2 months, delivery is not compliant with schedule requirement
business_rules.exceptions: Construction-to-permanent: 2 months measured from conversion to permanent financing

source_reference.section_id: C2-2-01, Amortization Schedule Requirements for Whole Loans
source_reference.source_text: For whole loans delivered to Fannie Mae, the ﬁrst payment date must be no later than two months from the ﬁnal disbursement date of the loan proceeds. In the case of a single-close construction-to-permanent loan, the two month period begins at the time of the conversion to permanent ﬁnancing.

examples: Final disbursement occurs on 2026-01-15; first payment date must be no later than 2026-03-15.
Final disbursement occurs on 2026-11-30; first payment date must be no later than 2027-01-30.
Single-close construction-to-permanent converts on 2026-06-10; first payment date must be no later than 2026-08-10 even if earlier construction draws occurred.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Delivery rejection or funding delay

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: MI coverage 12% to 35% for >20-year fixed and ARMs
business_rules.description: For first-lien mortgages on all property types other than standard manufactured homes, fixed-rate loans with terms > 20 years and all ARMs must meet MI coverage requirements by LTV range. Minimum coverage (allowed only with an MI LLPA) is 6% for 80.01–85.00% LTV, 12% for 85.01–90.00% LTV, 16% for 90.01–95.00% LTV, and 18% for 95.01–97.00% LTV. Standard coverage option levels are 12%, 25%, 30%, and 35% respectively. If lender chooses less than standard but no lower than minimum, an MI LLPA is assessed based on LTV and representative credit score (in addition to other LLPAs).
business_rules.conditions: First-lien; fixed-rate term >20 years or any ARM; LTV 80.01–97.00%
business_rules.consequences: If below minimum: ineligible for delivery; if minimum (vs standard): MI LLPA assessed
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (must be ≥ minimum coverage for the LTV range)

source_reference.source_text: For certain transactions, Fannie Mae oﬀers two mortgage insurance coverage level options: standard coverage for the transaction type (noted with ^) and minimum coverage (noted with *) with corresponding LLPAs. Lenders who choose less than standard coverage (but no lower than minimum coverage) will be assessed an LLPA based on the LTV ratio and representative credit score for the mortgage loan... Fixed-rate, term > 20 years All ARMs 6%* + MI LLPA 12%* + MI LLPA 16%* + MI LLPA 18%* + MI LLPA 12%^ 25%^ 30%^ 35%^

examples: Fixed-rate 30-year, 92% LTV: lender may use 16% MI with MI LLPA, or standard 30% MI without the minimum-coverage LLPA.
5/6 ARM, 86% LTV: minimum MI is 12% (with MI LLPA); standard MI is 25%.
30-year fixed, 96.5% LTV: minimum MI is 18% (with MI LLPA); standard MI is 35%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or pricing adjustment (MI LLPA)

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Standard manufactured home MI not allowed above 95.00% LTV
business_rules.description: For first-lien loans secured by standard manufactured homes (not MH Advantage), MI coverage is not available/eligible for the 95.01–97.00% LTV range (shown as 'Not applicable'). For 80.01–85.00%, 85.01–90.00%, and 90.01–95.00% LTV ranges, minimum MI coverage (with MI LLPA) is 6%, 12%, and 16%, and standard coverage is 12%, 25%, and 30% respectively.
business_rules.conditions: First-lien; property is standard manufactured home (not MH Advantage); LTV >80%
business_rules.consequences: If LTV 95.01–97.00%: loan fails MI coverage table and is ineligible for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: N/A (table states 95.01–97.00% is not applicable)

source_reference.source_text: Standard manufactured homes (not MH Advantage) Fixed-rate, ARM, any term 6%* + MI LLPA 12%* + MI LLPA 16%* + MI LLPA Not applicable 12%^ 25%^ 30%^

examples: Standard manufactured home, 94% LTV: minimum MI is 16% (with MI LLPA) or standard 30%.
Standard manufactured home, 96% LTV: 95.01–97.00% is 'Not applicable' so loan cannot meet MI table requirements for delivery.
Standard manufactured home, 83% LTV: minimum MI is 6% (with MI LLPA) or standard 12%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-paid MI prohibited for convertible ARMs except limited MBS case
business_rules.description: Lender-purchased mortgage insurance (LPMI) is accepted for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to fixed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae may consider accepting LPMI for convertible ARMs that are in MBS pools only if the lender uses the 'market rate' post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses lender-purchased MI; evaluate whether ARM is convertible to fixed-rate
business_rules.consequences: If convertible ARM without stated MBS conditions: LPMI not acceptable for delivery
business_rules.exceptions: Convertible ARMs in MBS may be considered if market-rate option used and lender assumes IR risk

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae accepts lender-purchased mortgage insurance coverage for all loan types except adjustable-rate mortgages that can be converted to ﬁxed-rate mortgages. Fannie Mae will consider accepting lender-purchased mortgage insurance for convertible adjustable-rate mortgages that are in MBS pools if the lender uses the “market rate” post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.

examples: 7/1 ARM with a conversion option using LPMI: prohibited for delivery as LPMI is not accepted for convertible ARMs.
Convertible ARM delivered into an MBS pool: may be considered only if lender elects the market-rate post-conversion disposition option and assumes all interest rate risk.
30-year fixed-rate loan using LPMI: acceptable (subject to LPMI lender requirements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Lender-paid MI must be kept until paid in full and delivered with SFC 019
business_rules.description: When providing lender-purchased mortgage insurance (LPMI), the lender must: (i) pay for MI as a corporate obligation with an initial premium and renewal premiums (or acceptable lump-sum life-of-loan plan); (ii) increase servicing compensation by at least the MI renewal premium amount (not required for lump-sum life-of-loan plans); (iii) keep MI coverage in effect until the mortgage is paid in full; and (iv) deliver the loan with Special Feature Code (SFC) 019.
business_rules.conditions: Loan uses lender-purchased MI; applies through delivery and ongoing coverage maintenance
business_rules.consequences: Failure can cause delivery issues, servicing transfer defects, and contractual remedies
business_rules.exceptions: Servicing comp increase not required for lump-sum life-of-loan MI plans

source_reference.source_text: When providing lender-purchased mortgage insurance, the lender must: pay for the mortgage insurance coverage as a corporate obligation with an initial premium and renewal premiums for each subsequent period of coverage... Lump-sum premium plans that provide coverage for the life-of-the-mortgage loan also are acceptable; increase the servicing compensation... by at least the amount of the mortgage insurance renewal premium. (This is not required for lump-sum premium plans...); keep the mortgage insurance coverage in eﬀect until the mortgage is paid in full; deliver the loan with SFC 019;

examples: Monthly-renewal LPMI: lender pays initial premium + monthly renewals, increases servicing comp by at least the renewal premium, and delivers loan with SFC 019.
Single-premium life-of-loan LPMI: lender does not need to increase servicing compensation but still must deliver with SFC 019 and keep coverage in effect until payoff.
Lender delivers an LPMI loan without SFC 019: delivery data defect requiring correction and potential remedy exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: First mortgage term must be 85 to 360 months with 30-year maturity test
business_rules.description: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans with original terms up to 30 years. The term of a first mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the first payment. The minimum original term is 85 months (subject to committing and delivery requirements).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to first-lien mortgage loan terms at origination for Fannie Mae delivery
business_rules.consequences: If term outside limits: loan is not eligible for purchase/securitization
business_rules.exceptions: Single-closing construction-to-permanent: ≤30 years after conversion (construction period disregarded)

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes loans that have original terms up to 30 years. The term of a ﬁrst mortgage may not extend more than 30 years beyond the date that is one month prior to the date of the ﬁrst payment. The minimum original term is 85 months... Exception: The only exception to these requirements is for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which must have a loan term not exceeding 30 years after conversion to permanent ﬁnancing (disregarding the construction period).

examples: 30-year fixed with first payment due 2026-08-01: maturity must be no later than 30 years beyond 2026-07-01 (one month prior).
10-year fixed (120 months): satisfies the 85-month minimum and 30-year maximum.
Single-closing construction-to-permanent: 12-month construction + 30-year permanent term is acceptable because construction period is disregarded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: HOEPA Section 32 Regulation Z loans are not eligible for Fannie Mae delivery
business_rules.description: Any loan subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
business_rules.conditions: Loan is identified as HOEPA/Reg Z Section 32 covered transaction for the subject property state
business_rules.consequences: Loan is ineligible for delivery; must not be sold/securitized to Fannie Mae
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B2-1.5-02, HOEPA and State Higher-Priced Loans
source_reference.source_text: A loan that is subject to the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 (HOEPA), as described in Section 32 of Regulation Z, is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.

examples: Cash-out refinance triggers HOEPA high-cost thresholds under Reg Z Section 32: loan is not eligible for delivery to Fannie Mae.
Purchase loan is not HOEPA-covered: this prohibition does not apply (other eligibility rules still apply).
A lender incorrectly delivers a HOEPA loan: loan fails eligibility and is subject to contractual remedies.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection or repurchase demand

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: Credit card early fees capped at 2% of loan amount
business_rules.description: Credit card financing may be allowed for payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing, but only up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount. To permit this, the lender must either (1) confirm the borrower has sufficient liquid funds (financial reserves) to cover these charges in addition to other closing costs and down payment, or (2) recalculate the credit card payment to account for the new charges and include the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower pays common/customary pre-closing fees by credit card
business_rules.consequences: If >2% or requirements unmet, fees must be re-sourced or loan re-underwritten/denied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Lenders may allow credit card ﬁnancing for the payment of common and customary fees paid outside of closing up to a maximum of 2% of the loan amount if the lender: conﬁrms that the borrower has suﬃcient liquid funds (ﬁnancial reserves) to cover these charges (in addition to funds needed for other closing costs and the down payment that they will be paying); or recalculates the credit card payment, per B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations , to account for the new charges and includes the updated payment in the qualifying ratio calculation.

examples: Loan amount $400,000: max eligible credit-card financed early fees = $8,000; borrower charged $7,500 in origination/lock fees—allowed if reserves cover $7,500 plus other funds-to-close.
Loan amount $250,000: borrower charged $6,000 early fees (2.4%); lender must treat as not permitted and require different payment method or reduce fees.
Borrower charges $3,000 early fees but has limited reserves; lender may still allow if lender recalculates the credit card minimum payment to reflect the new balance and includes it in DTI.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: 30-year fixed-rate purchase mortgage delivered to Fannie Mae
Adjustable-rate mortgage with initial fixed period and subsequent adjustments
Refinance mortgage loan requiring appraisal and eligibility checks

business_rules.rule_name: De minimis correction limited to $500 and barred if it reduces minimum contribution
business_rules.description: Under the remedies framework, a de minimis correction is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or a higher agreed amount) that can resolve an identified significant defect when remitted/refunded/otherwise provided, but it cannot be used if the correction would cause a specific required minimum borrower contribution to not be met or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.
business_rules.conditions: When lender proposes a correction to resolve an identified significant defect
business_rules.consequences: If allowed, defect may be treated as corrected; if barred, other remedy applies
business_rules.exceptions: May exceed $500 only if lender and Fannie Mae agree; barred cases apply

source_reference.section_id: D2-1-04 (08/30/2016), Definitions of Remedy and Correction
source_reference.source_text: One example of a correction is a “de minimis correction,” which is a minor amount not to exceed $500 (or such higher amount as the lender and Fannie Mae may agree) that, when remitted, refunded, or otherwise provided, corrects or otherwise resolves an identiﬁed signiﬁcant defect. However, a “de minimis correction” cannot be made when the correction would result in a speciﬁc required minimum borrower contribution not being met, or to correct a violation of Fannie Mae’s Charter requirements.

examples: Fee cure of $250 is remitted to resolve a minor disclosed-fee defect; qualifies as de minimis correction.
A $400 correction would reduce borrower’s required minimum contribution below the required level; de minimis correction is not permitted.
Lender and Fannie Mae agree in writing to a higher de minimis cap (e.g., $750) for a specific defect type; allowed only per agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Repurchase demand or repurchase alternative if defect remains significant

examples: Fannie Mae post-purchase QC review identifying an appraisal documentation defect
Lender post-closing QC audit sampling loans and documenting findings
QC review resulting in remediation and updated mortgage file records

business_rules.rule_name: Mandatory delivery commitment shortfall limited to $10,000 or 2.5%
business_rules.description: A mandatory delivery commitment generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount by the commitment expiration date. The minimum required delivery amount cannot be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender enters a mandatory delivery commitment for whole loans.
business_rules.consequences: If delivery falls below minimum, lender fails commitment and may need pair-off actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this glossary definition.

source_reference.source_text: mandatory delivery commitment A whole loan commitment that generally requires the lender to deliver eligible mortgages equal to at least the minimum required delivery amount (which is an amount that will not be less than the original commitment amount by more than $10,000 or 2.5% of the original amount) by the expiration date of the commitment.

examples: Original commitment $2,000,000: minimum required delivery is $1,950,000 (2.5% shortfall) not $1,900,000.
Original commitment $300,000: 2.5% is $7,500, so the tighter $10,000 cap does not apply; minimum is $292,500.
Original commitment $350,000: 2.5% is $8,750; minimum is $341,250 (not $340,000).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Pair-off fee or other commitment remedy

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: DU limited waiver invalid if file contains undisclosed contradictory data
business_rules.description: For loans submitted to DU, the lender must review the entire underwriting file for data or information not submitted to DU or inconsistent with what was submitted. If derogatory or contradictory information is found, the lender must take appropriate action (e.g., further investigate to determine whether it would change the DU recommendation, or set aside the DU recommendation if grounds exist for a different decision). If such information exists and is not appropriately addressed, the limited waiver of representations and warranties is no longer valid even for an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to loans submitted to DU for evaluation and potential limited waiver
business_rules.consequences: If acted on, lender can re-evaluate; if not, limited waiver becomes invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: For loans submitted to DU for evaluation, the lender must review the entire underwriting ﬁle to determine whether it includes any data or other information that was either not submitted to DU, or is inconsistent with any data or information that was in fact submitted to DU. If any such information (especially of a derogatory or contradictory nature) is found, the lender must take appropriate action, such as further investigating the information, to see if it would change the DU recommendation, or setting aside the DU recommendation

examples: DU casefile shows no delinquent housing history, but file later reveals recent 60-day mortgage delinquency not entered into DU: lender must re-evaluate; waiver is invalid if not addressed.
Borrower employment in file conflicts with DU-submitted employer and indicates job loss prior to closing: lender must investigate and potentially set aside DU recommendation.
Lender discovers new undisclosed liability post-submission but before closing: must determine whether DU recommendation changes; waiver depends on proper action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief requires acquisition on or after July 1, 2014
business_rules.description: To be eligible for representation and warranty enforcement relief, a loan must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either (1) a whole loan purchased on or after July 1, 2014, or (2) a mortgage loan delivered into MBS with a pool issue date on or after July 1, 2014.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether a loan can obtain enforcement relief framework
business_rules.consequences: If date test met, loan may qualify for relief subject to other criteria
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: Loans must have been acquired by Fannie Mae as either: whole loans purchased on or after July 1, 2014; or mortgage loans delivered into MBS with pool issue dates on or after July 1, 2014.

examples: Whole loan acquired June 15, 2014: fails acquisition-date eligibility and cannot obtain enforcement relief under this framework.
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date July 1, 2014: meets acquisition-date criterion (other criteria still apply).
Loan delivered into MBS with pool issue date August 2020: meets acquisition-date criterion and can proceed to payment history/QC review tests.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: Enforcement relief after 36 payments with max two 30-day lates and no 60+
business_rules.description: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV refinance loans, when enforcement relief is based on acceptable payment history, relief occurs after the borrower pays the first 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent on the 36th monthly payment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to non–high LTV refinance loans seeking relief based on payment history
business_rules.consequences: If thresholds met, lender is relieved from remedies for covered breaches; else not
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans follow separate 12/36-month payment history test

source_reference.source_text: For all loans other than Fannie Mae high LTV reﬁnance loans: To be eligible for relief under the framework, if the relief is based on the borrower’s acceptable payment history, the relief will occur upon payment by the borrower of the ﬁrst 36 monthly payments due following the loan acquisition date, provided that the borrower had no more than two 30-day delinquencies, had no 60-day or greater delinquencies, and is not 30 or more days delinquent with respect to the 36th monthly payment.

examples: Borrower has one 30-day late in month 10 and one 30-day late in month 22, no 60+ delinquencies, and month 36 is current: meets payment history thresholds for relief.
Borrower has three 30-day delinquencies over 36 months: fails relief threshold (more than two 30-day delinquencies).
Borrower is 30 days delinquent on the 36th payment: fails relief requirement even if prior months were acceptable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: PMI lapse requires reinstate or repurchase within 30 days; reimburse in 15 days
business_rules.description: If failure of a Private Mortgage Insurance policy to be in force on or after the Purchase Date is due to Seller negligence, Seller must, no later than 30 days after receiving notification, either take actions necessary to cause the policy to be in force/reinstated or repurchase the related Servicing Rights at the Servicing Rights Repurchase Price. If Seller fails to comply, Servicer must notify Fannie Mae to initiate the repurchase process. If a rescission is notified and the policy cannot be reinstated, Seller must reimburse Servicer within 15 business days of Servicer’s reimbursement request.
business_rules.conditions: PMI policy not in force/rescinded post-Purchase Date due to Seller negligence
business_rules.consequences: If cured timely, servicing continues; if not, repurchase process is initiated
business_rules.exceptions: Servicer not required to advance funds to reinstate PMI

source_reference.source_text: Seller shall, no later than thirty (30) days after Seller’s receipt of notiﬁcation thereof, either (a) take all such actions as are necessary to cause such policy to be in force or to be reinstated by the applicable insurer, or (b) repurchase the related Servicing Rights from Servicer at the Servicing Rights Repurchase Price. If Seller fails to comply with the preceding sentence with respect to a Mortgage Loan, Servicer shall notify Fannie Mae, to initiate the repurchase process with respect to such Mortgage Loan. At no time shall Servicer be made to advance funds to reinstate any Private Mortgage Insurance Policy... Seller shall reimburse Servicer for any amounts due within ﬁfteen (15) Business Days

examples: Seller receives notice on May 1 that PMI lapsed due to Seller error → by May 31 Seller must reinstate PMI or repurchase servicing rights.
Seller does not act within 30 days → Servicer notifies Fannie Mae to initiate repurchase process for the affected mortgage loan.
PMI policy is rescinded and cannot be reinstated; Servicer requests reimbursement on June 10 → Seller must reimburse by 15 business days after June 10.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Initiate repurchase process / servicing rights repurchase

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: SRP refund 100% if paid off in 120 days and $1,500 EPD fee
business_rules.description: Under the Servicing Marketplace Mortgage Loan Servicing Purchase and Sale Agreement: (a) If the mortgage loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller must refund 100% of the price paid by Servicer for the servicing rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae. (b)(iii) If the first, second, or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes 60 or more days delinquent after the Purchase Date (EPD Mortgage Loan), Seller must reimburse the original purchase price paid for the EPD mortgage loan plus an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD mortgage loan, and must wire the amount within seven (7) Business Days from Servicer’s request.
business_rules.conditions: When servicing rights were purchased and payoff/EPD conditions occur after Purchase Date
business_rules.consequences: Seller must refund SRP and/or pay EPD fee within specified timeframes; nonpayment breaches agreement
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt

source_reference.source_text: 7.4 Refund of the SRP and Repurchase of Mortgage Loan . (a) Mortgage Loan Paid in Full . If the Mortgage Loan is paid in full within 120 days of the Purchase Date, Seller shall refund one hundred percent (100%) of the price paid by Servicer for such Servicing Rights, plus any applicable associated interest loss and any applicable fees due to Fannie Mae... (iii) ...the ﬁrst, second or third monthly payment due Servicer becomes sixty (60) or more days delinquent... Seller shall reimburse Servicer for the original purchase price paid... along with an early payment default fee of $1,500 per EPD Mortgage Loan. Seller shall wire to Servicer the applicable amount within seven (7) Business Days from the date of Servicer’s request.

examples: Servicing rights purchased on Jan 1; borrower refinances and pays off Mar 15 (within 120 days) → seller refunds 100% SRP plus applicable interest loss/fees.
First payment due becomes 65 days delinquent after purchase date → seller pays original SRP amount plus $1,500 EPD fee within 7 business days of servicer request.
Third payment becomes 60+ days delinquent on two separate loans → seller owes $1,500 per loan (2 × $1,500) plus reimbursement of both purchase prices.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Contractual reimbursement demand and fees; potential formal dispute/remedies

examples: Seller/Servicer contract with representations and warranties for delivered mortgages
Contractual record retention and examination provisions for mortgage files
Contract remedy section enabling repurchase or make-whole payments

business_rules.rule_name: DU Ineligible if 60+ day mortgage delinquency within 12 months
