source_reference.source_text: Asset veriﬁcation reports provided by third-party vendors may include: a 30- or 60-day report; data covering up to 12 months (or more) of asset account data; a report showing a subset of information typically found on an asset veriﬁcation report (for example, deposit transaction information with no corresponding amount or account balance) intended to be used to verify employment using direct deposit transactions (a "supplemental asset report"). To validate income or employment using an asset veriﬁcation report, the lender must order an asset veriﬁcation report with a minimum of 12 months of data from an authorized vendor.

examples: Lender orders 12-month asset verification report; DU validates employment based on direct deposit history.
Lender orders only a 60-day asset report; DU cannot validate income/employment using the report and lender must meet standard documentation messages.
Vendor provides 15 months of data; lender satisfies the minimum 12-month requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: DU Validation Service Optional and Cannot Be Mandatory
business_rules.description: The DU validation service is optional for conventional loans underwritten through DU. Lenders are not required to participate for DU underwriting, and lenders may not require a borrower to participate in DU validation service.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting conventional loans through DU and considering DU validation service
business_rules.consequences: If violated, borrower participation is improperly conditioned; compliance issue
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The DU validation service is an optional service available only for conventional loans underwritten through DU. Lenders are not required to participate in the DU validation service in order for a loan to be underwritten through DU and may not require a borrower to participate in the DU validation service.

examples: Borrower declines electronic verification; lender proceeds with standard documentation and submits loan to DU without DU validation.
Lender tells borrower the loan will be denied unless borrower participates in DU validation service; this violates the prohibition.
Lender opts in to validate income and employment, but also offers a non-validation path with standard documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: DU Reserves 2 Months Second Home and 6 Months for DTI Over 45%
business_rules.description: For DU loan casefiles, DU determines minimum required reserves as follows: two months’ reserves for a second home transaction; six months’ reserves for (i) a two- to four-unit principal residence transaction, (ii) an investment property transaction, and (iii) a cash-out refinance transaction when DTI ratio is greater than 45%.
business_rules.conditions: When underwriting through DU and DU determines required minimum reserves
business_rules.consequences: Meets: DU reserve condition satisfied; Violates: underwriting conditions unmet; loan not clear to close/deliver
business_rules.exceptions: High LTV refinance loans exempt (separate rule)

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.1-01, Determining Required Minimum Reserves (DU loan casefiles)
source_reference.source_text: DU loan caseﬁles : DU will determine the reserve requirements based on the following: Two months' reserves for a second home transaction. Six months' reserves for the following: a two- to four-unit principal residence transaction, an investment property transaction, and a cash-out reﬁnance transaction with a DTI ratio greater than 45%.

examples: Second home purchase with PITIA $3,000: DU requires 2 months = $6,000 reserves verified from eligible assets.
Investment property refinance with PITIA $2,200: DU requires 6 months = $13,200 reserves; borrower has $9,000—fails reserve condition.
Cash-out refi with DTI 46% and PITIA $2,500: DU requires 6 months = $15,000; if DTI is 44%, this specific 6-month trigger does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery / potential repurchase demand

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: No asset documentation required when refinance funds to verify are $500 or less
business_rules.description: For refinance transactions, DU will not require documentation of assets when the total funds to be verified are $500 or less. Lenders must still determine whether additional documentation is warranted based on the borrower and situation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU requires assets to be verified for a refinance and funds-to-verify ≤ $500.
business_rules.consequences: If ≤ $500, DU does not require asset docs; if > $500, asset verification rules apply.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: This level of documentation may not be adequate for every borrower and every situation. The lender must determine whether additional documentation is warranted. DU will not require documentation of assets for reﬁnance transactions when the total funds to be veriﬁed are $500 or less.

examples: Rate/term refinance with required verified funds of $400: DU does not require asset documentation.
Cash-out refinance with required verified funds of $750: DU requires asset documentation per DU messaging.
Limited cash-out refinance with required verified funds of $500: DU does not require asset documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: Depository asset statements: 60 days purchase or 30 days refinance and dated within 45 days
business_rules.description: When DU requires verification of depository assets, the lender must provide two consecutive monthly bank statements (60 days) for purchase transactions, or one monthly statement (30 days) for limited cash-out and cash-out refinances. Monthly statements must be dated within 45 days of the initial loan application date. Quarterly statements must be dated within 90 days, and the lender must confirm funds were not transferred to another account verified with more current documentation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when DU requires verification of depository assets (checking/savings/MM/CD).
business_rules.consequences: If documentation meets timing/quantity rules, asset verification satisfied; if not, loan fails asset documentation requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) may replace statements.

source_reference.source_text: For depository assets (checking and savings accounts, money market funds, and certiﬁcates of deposit), DU will require the following: two consecutive monthly bank statements (60 days of account activity) for all purchase transactions, or one monthly statement (30 days of account activity) for all limited cash-out and cash-out reﬁnance transactions. Monthly bank statements must be dated within 45 days of the initial loan application date. Quarterly bank statements must be dated within 90 days of the initial loan application date, and the lender must conﬁrm that the funds in the account have not been transferred to another asset account that is veriﬁed with more current documentation. A Veriﬁcation of Deposit (Form 1006) can be obtained in place of bank statements.

examples: Purchase: borrower provides March and April statements (60 days) dated within 45 days of application—acceptable.
Cash-out refinance: borrower provides a single monthly statement showing 30 days of activity dated 70 days before application—unacceptable due to 45-day dating rule.
Limited cash-out refinance: lender obtains Form 1006 instead of bank statements—acceptable substitute.
business_rules.enforcement_action: N/A

examples: DU casefile receiving Approve/Eligible recommendation
DU casefile receiving Refer with Caution recommendation
DU casefile using DU Validation Service for income/asset validation

business_rules.rule_name: Accessory Dwelling Unit Rental Income Capped at 30%
business_rules.description: Rental income from an existing accessory dwelling unit (ADU) on the subject property may be used for qualifying only when all listed conditions are met, including that the qualifying rental income from the ADU is limited to 30% of the borrower’s total qualifying income.
business_rules.conditions: Subject is 1-unit principal residence with 1 ADU; purchase or LCO refi
business_rules.consequences: If met, ADU rental income may be used; if exceeded, cap at 30% or disallow
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond listed ADU conditions

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01, Rental Income (10/08/2025) - Rental Income from an ADU on the Subject Property
source_reference.source_text: Rental Income from an ADU on the Subject Property Rental income from an existing ADU can be used in qualifying with the following conditions: one-unit, principal residence only, rental income from only one ADU allowed, purchase or limited cash-out reﬁnance transactions, and qualifying rental income amount from the ADU is limited to 30% of the total qualifying income. All other documentation and requirements contained within this topic apply.

examples: Purchase 1-unit primary with ADU: total qualifying income $10,000; ADU rent may contribute up to $3,000.
Limited cash-out refi 1-unit primary with 2 ADUs: rental income is not eligible because only one ADU is allowed.
Purchase primary with ADU: if ADU rent would be 40% of total qualifying income, lender must cap at 30% or not use it to qualify.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Principal Residence or Second Home Rental Income Prohibited
business_rules.description: Rental income from the borrower’s principal residence (a one-unit principal residence or the unit occupied in a two- to four-unit property) or a second home cannot be used to qualify the borrower. Fannie Mae allows exceptions only for boarder income and rental income on principal residence properties with accessory units, as referenced.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower seeks to use rent from own principal residence unit or second home
business_rules.consequences: If prohibited use attempted, rental income must be excluded from qualifying income
business_rules.exceptions: Boarder income and ADU/accessory unit rental income exceptions apply

source_reference.source_text: Ineligible Properties Generally, rental income from the borrower’s principal residence (a one-unit principal residence or the unit the borrower occupies in a two- to four-unit property) or a second home cannot be used to qualify the borrower. However, Fannie Mae does allow certain exceptions to this policy for boarder income and rental income on principal residence properties with accessory units. See B3-3.4-04, Boarder Income , for boarder income requirements.

examples: Borrower rents a room in their 1-unit primary residence: rental income cannot be used as ‘rental income’ (may be eligible under boarder income rules).
Borrower owns a second home and wants to count short-term rent to qualify: prohibited; exclude from income.
Borrower has 1-unit primary with ADU: ADU rental income may be considered subject to ADU conditions (including 30% cap).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligible; repurchase demand risk

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Blanket authorization must be signed and reused by copy
business_rules.description: The lender must obtain the borrower’s signature on the Blanket Authorization form to obtain the documentation needed to evaluate the borrower’s creditworthiness. A photocopy of the signed form also serves as authorization, and the information obtained is only to be used in processing the mortgage loan application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to borrower creditworthiness documentation requests during application
business_rules.consequences: Signed authorization supports verifications; missing signature blocks verifications
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender obtains the borrower’s signature on the following Blanket Authorization form to obtain the documentation needed to evaluate the borrower’s creditworthiness. This borrower-signed document gives the lender blanket authorization to request the information needed to document the borrower’s creditworthiness... It is understood that a photocopy of this form also will serve as authorization. The information the lender obtains is only to be used in the processing of my application for a mortgage loan.

examples: Borrower signs the Blanket Authorization at application; lender orders a credit report and VOE using that authorization.
Lender uses a photocopy of the signed authorization to request verification of deposit from a bank; bank accepts the copy as authorization.
If the authorization is unsigned, the lender cannot properly order/verify credit and asset information and must obtain a signature before proceeding.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition/suspense until authorization obtained

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Omitted credit liability with balance > $0 needs support
business_rules.description: If a liability appears on the borrower’s credit report with a balance greater than zero but is omitted from the loan application, the lender must obtain supporting documentation addressing the omission.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when credit report shows a liability balance > $0 omitted from application.
business_rules.consequences: Meets: omission supported; Violated: underwriting defect and delivery/QC risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.source_text: Trended Credit Data Lenders are not required to analyze trended credit data in the credit report. For more information, see B3-2-03, Risk Factors Evaluated by DU . Omitted Accounts Supporting documentation is required when a credit report liability with a balance greater than zero is omitted from the loan application.

examples: Credit report shows an auto loan with $8,500 balance omitted from the 1003; lender obtains documentation showing it was paid off and updates/resubmits as needed.
Borrower omitted a $1 balance store card; because balance is > $0, lender documents why it is excluded/paid and retains support.
Credit report shows a closed account with $0 balance omitted; this rule does not trigger because balance is not greater than zero.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand (if material and not cured)
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit requirement with numeric threshold (balance > 0).

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: HomeReady cash-on-hand must be deposited by application or 30 days pre-close
business_rules.description: For HomeReady purchase money mortgages on one-unit properties, cash-on-hand may be used for down payment, closing costs, and prepaid items, but may not be used to meet any reserve requirement. The lender must verify the borrower customarily uses cash and that the saved funds are consistent with prior payment practices, must verify funds exist in a financial institution or acceptable escrow account, and the funds must be on deposit at the time of application or no less than 30 days prior to closing. The lender must also obtain a written borrower statement disclosing the source of funds and stating the funds have not been borrowed.
business_rules.conditions: HomeReady; purchase; one-unit property; borrower uses cash-on-hand funds
business_rules.consequences: If not verified/timed, cash-on-hand cannot be counted as eligible funds to close
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited passage

source_reference.source_text: Lenders may deliver purchase money mortgages for one-unit properties with cash-on-hand as an acceptable source of funds for the borrower’s down payment, funds for closing costs, and prepaid items. Note : Cash-on-hand may not be used to fund the borrower’s reserve requirement, if applicable. The lender must verify and document the following with respect to the cash-on-hand funds: The borrower customarily uses cash for expenses, and the amount of funds saved is consistent with the borrower’s previous payment practices. The lender must verify that funds for the down payment and closing costs exist in a ﬁnancial institution account or an acceptable escrow account. Funds must be on deposit at the time of application, or no less than 30 days prior to closing. The lender must obtain a written statement from the borrower that discloses the source of funds and states that the funds have not been borrowed.

examples: Funds deposited 10 days before closing: fails the “no less than 30 days prior to closing” timing; must use other eligible assets or delay closing.
Funds deposited before application date and verified in a bank statement: meets the deposit timing requirement (subject to all other verification steps).
Borrower attempts to use cash-on-hand for required reserves: not permitted; reserves must come from other eligible verified assets.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting denial or condition to provide eligible funds

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Seasonal Income Requires Form 1005 or 2 Years W-2s
business_rules.description: When seasonal income is used to qualify, the lender must obtain either (i) a completed Request for Verification of Employment (Form 1005) or (ii) the most recent paystub and two years’ W-2s. In all cases, a verbal verification of employment (VOE) is also required per the verbal VOE policy cross-reference.
business_rules.conditions: Seasonal income is included as qualifying income on the loan file
business_rules.consequences: If met, seasonal income may be evaluated; if not, income cannot be used to qualify
business_rules.exceptions: For seasonal unemployment comp, verify ties to seasonal layoffs and tax returns

source_reference.source_text: Documentation The lender must obtain • a completed Request for Veriﬁcation of Employment (Form 1005); or • the most recent paystub and two years' W-2s. Additionally, a verbal VOE is required. See B3-3.1-04, Verbal Veriﬁcation of Employment, for speciﬁc requirements.

examples: Borrower uses seasonal landscaping income; lender includes Form 1005 and completes verbal VOE—income may be considered.
Borrower provides only one W-2 and no Form 1005; lender cannot support seasonal income—exclude it from qualifying income.
Seasonal unemployment compensation is used; lender documents it on borrower tax returns as tied to seasonal layoffs per referenced policy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Seasonal Income Minimum History Must Be 2 Years
business_rules.description: Seasonal income requires a minimum two-year history to be eligible for use as qualifying income.
business_rules.conditions: Seasonal income is being analyzed as borrower qualifying income
business_rules.consequences: If ≥2-year history exists, proceed; if <2 years, exclude seasonal income from qualification
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for seasonal income history requirement

source_reference.source_text: Income History A minimum two-year history is required. Income Continuance The lender is not required to verify continuance unless they have reason to believe the income may not continue.

examples: Borrower has 3 years of seasonal construction income—meets the minimum history requirement.
Borrower has 14 months of seasonal income—does not meet the 2-year minimum; income must be excluded.
Borrower has 2 years of seasonal income but lender has reason to believe it may stop—continuance must be addressed per guidance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Seasonal Income Average Uses YTD Plus Prior 2 Years
business_rules.description: To determine qualifying seasonal income, the lender must calculate an average income amount using year-to-date (YTD) income when present and the previous two years’ earnings.
business_rules.conditions: Seasonal income is eligible and supported and is used for qualification
business_rules.consequences: If averaged per requirement, resulting amount may be used; if not, income calc is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the averaging method requirement

source_reference.source_text: Determination of Qualifying Income The lender must calculate an average income amount using year-to-date income, when present, and previous two years' earnings.

examples: Borrower YTD seasonal income is $12,000 and prior two years are $30,000 and $28,000; lender averages using YTD when present plus prior two years per policy.
Lender uses only last year’s seasonal income to qualify; calculation is not compliant because it omits YTD and the previous two years’ earnings.
Borrower has YTD available but lender ignores it and uses two-year average only; not compliant because YTD must be used when present.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary Leave Requires Return Date Proof and Verbal VOE
business_rules.description: If the lender is aware the borrower will be on temporary leave at closing and that income is needed to qualify, the lender must obtain: (1) documentation supporting qualifying income by income type including amount/duration of temporary leave income and the regular employment income received prior to leave; (2) written confirmation from the borrower of intent to return to work; (3) employer (or employer designee) documentation confirming expected return-to-work date; and (4) a verbal VOE. If employer confirms borrower is currently on temporary leave, the lender must consider the borrower employed.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower on temporary leave at closing and their income is needed to qualify
business_rules.consequences: If met, borrower may be treated employed and income evaluated; if not, income use is unsupported
business_rules.exceptions: Return-date documentation need not meet allowable age of credit documents policy

source_reference.source_text: If a lender is made aware that a borrower will be on temporary leave at the time of the loan closing and that borrower's income is needed to qualify for the loan, the lender must determine allowable income and conﬁrm employment as described below. The lender must obtain documentation to support the borrower's qualifying income... written conﬁrmation from the borrower of their intent to return to work; documentation produced by the employer... that conﬁrms the borrower's expected return to work date... (This documentation does not have to comply with the Allowable Age of Credit Documents policy...) Additionally, a verbal VOE is required... If the employer conﬁrms the borrower is currently on temporary leave, the lender must consider the borrower employed.

examples: Borrower on parental leave at closing; lender gets employer HR system printout showing return date, borrower intent letter, and verbal VOE—borrower may be treated employed.
Lender relies only on borrower’s statement of return date with no employer documentation—temporary leave income cannot be validated for qualification.
Employer confirms borrower is currently on temporary leave during verbal VOE; lender treats borrower as employed for underwriting purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Temporary Leave Income Uses Lesser Amount Until 1st Payment Date
business_rules.description: If the borrower will return to work as of the first loan payment date, the lender can use the borrower’s regular employment income to qualify. If the borrower will not return to work as of the first loan payment date, the lender must use the lesser of the borrower’s temporary leave income (if any) or regular employment income. If temporary leave income is less than regular income, the lender may supplement with available liquid financial reserves by calculating: supplemental income = available liquid reserves ÷ number of months from first payment date to regular income start date (rounded up). Total qualifying income = supplemental income + temporary leave income, and the total may not exceed the borrower’s regular employment income.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower on temporary leave and return timing relative to first payment date differs
business_rules.consequences: If met, qualifying income is capped and supportable; if violated, income overstated and loan ineligible
business_rules.exceptions: Reserve supplement permitted only when temporary leave income is less than regular income

source_reference.source_text: If the borrower will return to work as of the ﬁrst loan payment date, the lender can consider the borrower's regular employment income in qualifying. If the borrower will not return to work as of the ﬁrst loan payment date, the lender must use the lesser of the borrower's temporary leave income (if any) or regular employment income... The following are instructions on how to calculate the "supplemental income": Supplemental income amount = available liquid reserves divided by the number of months of supplemental income... Number of months... rounded up to the next whole number... Total qualifying income = supplemental income + the temporary leave income... may not exceed the borrower's regular employment income.

examples: Borrower returns to work before first payment date; lender uses regular income of $6,500/month for qualification.
Borrower returns after first payment date; regular income $6,000, temporary leave income $2,000—lender must start with lesser amount ($2,000) and may add reserve-based supplement subject to cap.
First payment date to return date is 3.2 months; lender rounds up to 4 months when computing reserve supplement divisor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Capital gains income requires 2 years tax returns
business_rules.description: When capital gains income is used to qualify, the lender must obtain the borrower’s signed personal federal income tax returns for the most recent two years (including Form 1040 and Schedule D) and evidence of a portfolio of assets that can be sold for future payments. Current receipt of capital gains income is not required for allowable age compliance, but asset ownership documentation must comply with allowable age requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower uses capital gains income to qualify for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If docs complete, capital gains may be used; if not, income cannot qualify
business_rules.exceptions: Current receipt not required; asset ownership docs must meet age rules

source_reference.source_text: However, if the borrower is using income from capital gains to qualify, the income must be veriﬁed in accordance with the following requirements. The lender must obtain: • copies of the borrower’s signed personal federal income tax returns for the most recent two years, including IRS Form 1040, Schedule D, and • evidence that the borrower owns a portfolio of assets that can be sold if additional income is needed to make future mortgage loan payments Note: Due to the nature of this income, the current receipt of the income is not required to comply with the Allowable Age of Credit Documents policy. However, documentation of the asset ownership must be in compliance with the Allowable Age of Credit Documents policy

examples: Borrower claims $3,000/month capital gains: lender gets 2 years 1040 + Schedule D and brokerage statement evidencing asset ownership; income can be considered.
Borrower provides only 1 year tax return: lender cannot use capital gains income for qualifying until 2 years are obtained.
Borrower has acceptable tax returns but outdated asset ownership evidence beyond allowable age: lender must refresh asset documentation before using income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery ineligibility / repurchase exposure

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Capital gains income average uses 1 or 2 years
business_rules.description: Qualifying capital gains income must be averaged based on the historical trend. If stable or increasing, calculate an average using the most recent two years’ personal federal income tax returns; if decreasing, calculate an average using the most recent year’s personal federal income tax returns. Capital losses on Schedule D do not have to be considered when calculating income or liabilities, even if recurring.
business_rules.conditions: Capital gains income is used and tax return trend is evaluated
business_rules.consequences: If averaged per trend, income may qualify; wrong averaging causes QC defects
business_rules.exceptions: Capital losses on Schedule D do not have to be included

source_reference.source_text: Average Income: Depending on the historical trend of the income, the lender must calculate qualifying income as follows: • Stable or Increasing: Calculate an average income amount using the most recent two years personal federal income tax returns. • Decreasing: Calculate an average income amount using the most recent year personal federal income tax returns. Note: Capital losses identiﬁed on IRS Form 1040, Schedule D, do not have to be considered when calculating income or liabilities, even if the losses are recurring.

examples: Schedule D gains: $48,000 (year 1) and $52,000 (year 2) → stable/increasing, average = $50,000/yr ($4,166.67/mo).
Gains drop from $60,000 (year 1) to $30,000 (year 2) → decreasing, use most recent year = $30,000/yr ($2,500/mo).
Borrower has recurring $3,000 capital losses on Schedule D; lender may ignore losses when calculating qualifying income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting finding; potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Qualifying income from assets equals net assets ÷ term
business_rules.description: For employment-related assets used as qualifying income, the lender must compute monthly qualifying income by dividing Net Documented Assets by the loan amortization term (in months). Net Documented Assets equal the sum of eligible assets minus (i) the penalty for complete distribution at time of calculation and (ii) funds used for down payment, closing costs, and required reserves.
business_rules.conditions: Employment-related assets are used to create a monthly income stream
business_rules.consequences: If computed correctly, income may be used; miscalc can cause defects/QC
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender must divide "Net Documented Assets" by the amortization term of the loan (in months). "Net Documented Assets" are equal to the sum of eligible assets minus • the amount of the penalty that would apply if the account was completely distributed at the time of calculation; and • the amount of funds used for down payment, closing costs, and required reserves.

examples: Eligible assets $450,000 net of penalty; funds to close/reserves $100,000; net documented assets $350,000; 360-month loan → $972.22/month.
Net documented assets $120,000; 180-month amortization → $666.67/month qualifying income.
Penalty of $20,000 applies to full distribution and $30,000 needed for closing: eligible assets $200,000 → net = $150,000; 240 months → $625/month.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting finding; potential repurchase demand

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Authorized User Tradelines Require 12 Months Proof to Use
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten loans, tradelines listing the borrower as an authorized user cannot be considered in the underwriting decision unless another borrower in the transaction is the owner of the tradeline or the borrower provides written documentation showing they were the actual and sole payer of the monthly payment for at least 12 months preceding the application date. If such documentation is provided, late payments must be considered and the monthly payment obligation must be included in the DTI ratio. An authorized user tradeline must be considered if the owner is the borrower’s spouse and the spouse is not a borrower. These requirements do not apply to DU casefiles.
business_rules.conditions: Manual underwriting where credit report includes authorized user tradelines
business_rules.consequences: If compliant, tradeline may be used and DTI updated; if not, tradeline excluded
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable to DU; spouse-owned tradeline must be considered even if spouse not borrower

source_reference.section_id: B3-5.3-06, Consideration of Authorized User Accounts
source_reference.source_text:
For manually underwritten loans, credit report tradelines that list a borrower as an authorized user cannot be considered in the underwriting decision, except as outlined below.
An authorized user tradeline may be considered if:
another borrower in the mortgage transaction is the owner of the tradeline; or
the borrower can provide written documentation (e.g., canceled checks, payment receipts, etc.) that they have been the actual and sole payer of the monthly payment on the account for at least 12 months preceding the date of the application.
If written documentation of the borrower’s monthly payments on the authorized user tradeline is provided, then the payment history — particularly any late payments that are indicated — must be considered in the credit analysis and the monthly payment obligation must be included in the debt-to-income ratio.
An authorized user tradeline must be considered if the owner of the tradeline is the borrower's spouse and the spouse is not a borrower in the mortgage transaction.
These requirements do not apply to loan casefiles underwritten through DU.

examples: Manual underwrite: borrower is authorized user on parent’s card; no 12-month proof and parent not a borrower—tradeline cannot be used.
Manual underwrite: borrower provides 12 months of canceled checks showing sole payment—tradeline may be considered, and the monthly payment must be included in DTI.
Manual underwrite: borrower is authorized user on spouse’s card; spouse is not on the loan—tradeline must be considered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible/defect if credit decision improperly considered tradeline

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Homeownership education required before closing for >95% LTV
business_rules.description: At least one borrower on the loan must complete homeownership education prior to loan closing for specific purchase-related scenarios: (1) purchase underwritten in DU where none of the borrowers has at least one credit account or installment account on the credit report (as instructed by DU); (2) manually underwritten purchase where all borrowers do not have a credit score; (3) HomeReady purchase when all occupying borrowers are first-time homebuyers; or (4) purchase transactions with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios greater than 95% when all borrowers are first-time homebuyers. These requirements also apply to construction-to-permanent transactions processed as a purchase.
business_rules.conditions: When processing a purchase (or CTP treated as purchase) meeting listed triggers
business_rules.consequences: If not completed pre-closing, loan is noncompliant with Guide requirements
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; can satisfy via housing counseling in lieu of education

source_reference.source_text: Transactions that Require Homeownership Education For the following transactions, at least one borrower on the loan must complete homeownership education prior to loan closing: if none of the borrowers on a purchase transaction underwritten in DU has at least one credit account or installment account reported on their credit report, regardless of the loan product or whether the borrowers are ﬁrst-time homebuyers (as instructed by DU); if all borrowers on a manually underwritten purchase transaction do not have a credit score, regardless of the loan product or whether the borrowers are ﬁrst-time homebuyers; HomeReady purchase transactions when all occupying borrowers are ﬁrst-time homebuyers; or purchase transactions with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios greater than 95%, when all borrowers are ﬁrst-time homebuyers. Note: The requirements that apply to purchases also apply to construction-to-permanent transactions that are processed as a purchase.

examples: DU purchase: both borrowers have no reported credit accounts; one borrower completes education before closing: compliant.
Manual underwrite purchase: all borrowers are non-scored; no education completed before closing: noncompliant.
First-time HomeReady purchase: all occupying borrowers are first-time; one borrower completes housing counseling instead: compliant (in lieu of education).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase remedial action / repurchase exposure for eligibility breach

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Education provider must meet NIS or HUD standards with certificate
business_rules.description: When homeownership education is required, the lender must ensure the education is administered by a qualified third-party provider that is independent of the lender, and that the provider’s content is aligned with National Industry Standards (NIS) or HUD standards. The lender must confirm the course content alignment and must retain a copy of the certificate of course completion in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: When homeownership education is used to satisfy a required education trigger
business_rules.consequences: If alignment/certificate not retained, education requirement is not evidenced
business_rules.exceptions: Housing counseling may be used in lieu of education

source_reference.source_text: To satisfy the homeownership education requirement, Fannie Mae permits any qualiﬁed third-party provider, independent of the lender, to administer homeownership education. The provider’s content must be aligned with NIS or HUD standards. The education may be delivered in various formats (in-person, Internet, via telephone, or a hybrid format). In lieu of homeownership education, the borrower may receive housing counseling. The lender must conﬁrm the course content is aligned with NIS or HUD standards and must retain a copy of the certiﬁcate of course completion in the loan ﬁle.

examples: Borrower completes an online NIS-aligned course from an independent provider; lender retains completion certificate: compliant.
Lender accepts a course but does not document NIS/HUD alignment or retain certificate: noncompliant evidence of education.
Borrower completes HUD-approved housing counseling instead of education; lender retains counseling completion certificate: compliant via alternative.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Post-purchase documentation defect remedy / repurchase exposure

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Most recent paystub must be within 30 days of application and show YTD
business_rules.description: When paystubs are used to document employment income, the most recent paystub must be dated no earlier than 30 days prior to the initial loan application date and must include all year-to-date earnings. If it lacks sufficient information to calculate income, additional documentation must be obtained.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower employment income is documented using paystubs
business_rules.consequences: If met, paystub is acceptable; if violated, updated/extra documentation is required
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The most recent paystub must be dated no earlier than 30 days prior to the initial loan application date and it must include all year-to-date earnings. Additionally, the most recent paystub must include suﬃcient information to appropriately calculate income; otherwise, additional documentation must be obtained.

examples: Application date 2026-03-15 and paystub date 2026-03-01 (14 days prior) with YTD earnings; acceptable.
Application date 2026-03-15 and paystub date 2026-02-10 (33 days prior); lender must obtain a more recent paystub or other docs.
Paystub within 30 days but missing YTD totals; lender must obtain additional documentation to calculate income.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery or subject to loan repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: VOD Source Verification for Accounts Opened ≤ 90 Days
business_rules.description: If a Verification of Deposit (Form 1006) is used and depository activity is not included, the lender must verify the source of funds for (1) accounts opened within the last 90 days of the application date and (2) account balances that are considerably greater than the average balance reflected on the VOD.
business_rules.conditions: When Form 1006 VOD is used and depository activity is not included
business_rules.consequences: If met: assets acceptable for underwriting; if violated: asset verification is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the VOD requirement sentence

source_reference.section_id: B3-4.2-03 (02/06/2019), Request for Verification of Deposit
source_reference.source_text: When a Veriﬁcation of Deposit (Form 1006) (VOD) is used and depository activity is not included, the lender must verify the source of funds for accounts opened within the last 90 days of the application date, and account balances that are considerably greater than the average balance reﬂected on the VOD.

examples: Borrower opened a new checking account 45 days before application; lender must document where the funds came from.
VOD shows $75,000 balance but average balance is $2,000; lender must verify source for the excess funds.
VOD includes no depository activity; lender obtains bank statements and transfer evidence to confirm funds are not borrowed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition / potential post-purchase defect

business_rules.rule_name: In-file credit report must use 3 repositories or document exception
business_rules.description: An in-file credit report must include all information from three different credit repositories, or two repositories if that is the extent of data available for the borrower or if the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one repository. If only one in-file credit report is available, it is acceptable only if the lender requested information from three repositories.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses an in-file credit report to underwrite a loan application
business_rules.consequences: If met, credit report type is acceptable; if violated, lender must obtain compliant report/verification or file is defective
business_rules.exceptions: Two repositories allowed if data limited or one repository frozen; one repository allowed only if three were requested

source_reference.source_text: The report should include all information from three diﬀerent credit repositories, or two repositories, if: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one credit repository. If only one in-ﬁle credit report is available for a borrower, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three credit repositories.

examples: Borrower has a freeze at one bureau; lender obtains two repositories and documents the freeze as support.
Only one repository returns data; lender shows it requested three repositories, making the single report acceptable.
Lender pulls only one bureau without requesting the other two; report is not acceptable and must be corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: In-file accounts not updated within 90 days must be reverified
business_rules.description: If an in-file credit report lists accounts that were not checked with the creditor within 90 days of the in-file report date, the lender must obtain an updated credit report or a separate written verification for those accounts.
business_rules.conditions: When in-file credit report shows creditor-check age > 90 days for any listed account
business_rules.consequences: If met, lender has current verification; if violated, credit data is stale and underwriting file is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: If the report lists accounts that were not checked with the creditor within 90 days of the date of the in-ﬁle report, the lender must obtain an updated credit report or a separate written veriﬁcation for those accounts.

examples: In-file report dated 01/15 shows a creditor last checked 09/01; lender orders an updated report before closing.
In-file report includes an account last checked 120 days ago; lender obtains written verification from that creditor for the account terms and status.
All accounts show creditor check dates within 60 days; no additional reverification is required by this rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Automated merged credit report cannot use lender affiliate CRA
business_rules.description: An automated merged credit report is not acceptable if it is provided by a credit reporting agency that is affiliated with the lender in any way.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses an automated merged credit report for underwriting
business_rules.consequences: If violated, lender must replace report with non-affiliated provider report or underwriting file is noncompliant
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The report must meet the following requirements: The report must include all information from three diﬀerent credit repositories, or two repositories, if: that is the extent of the data available for the borrower, or the borrower’s credit information is frozen at one credit repository. If information from only one credit repository is available, this is acceptable provided the lender requested information from three diﬀerent credit repositories. The report cannot be provided by a credit reporting agency that is aﬃliated with the lender in any way.

examples: Lender-owned subsidiary produces the merged credit report; lender must obtain a report from a non-affiliated credit reporting agency.
AMC-style vendor is independent and not affiliated; merged report is acceptable if other requirements are met.
Lender uses a third-party CRA that has a shared parent company with the lender; report is prohibited under this rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: RMCR must contact 2 repositories per locality over 2 years
business_rules.description: A residential mortgage credit report (RMCR) must be based on the credit reporting agency contacting at least two national repositories of accumulated credit records for each locality where the borrower lived during the most recent two-year period.
business_rules.conditions: When lender uses a residential mortgage credit report (RMCR) to underwrite
business_rules.consequences: If met, RMCR meets repository-contact standard; if violated, RMCR is noncompliant and must be replaced/corrected
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The report must meet the following requirements: The credit reporting agency must contact at least two national repositories of accumulated credit records for each locality in which the borrower has lived during the most recent two-year period.

examples: Borrower lived in two counties over the last 24 months; RMCR vendor contacts at least two national repositories for each locality.
RMCR vendor contacts only one national repository for a locality; RMCR fails this requirement and must be corrected.
Borrower lived in one locality for the last two years; RMCR must still evidence contact with at least two national repositories for that locality.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan delivery rejection/repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Lease Rent Must Be in U.S. Dollars ($) Only
business_rules.description: When a fully executed current lease agreement is used to document rental income, the rental payment stated on the lease must be denominated in U.S. dollars; rental payment in virtual currency is not permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Lease agreement is used to support rental income for qualifying
business_rules.consequences: If USD shown, lease is acceptable; if virtual currency, lease is unacceptable
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: When the subject property will generate rental income and it is used for qualifying purposes, one of the following Fannie Mae forms must be used to support the income-earning potential of the property: For one-unit properties: Single-Family Comparable Rent Schedule (Form 1007 ) (provided in conjunction with the applicable appraisal report), or For two- to four-unit properties: Small Residential Income Property Appraisal Report  (Form 1025 ). Note : The rental payment on the lease must be reﬂected in U.S. dollars (cannot be in virtual currency).

examples: Purchase: Lease shows rent of $2,400/month; lender may use it (with Form 1007/1025 as applicable).
Refinance: Lease states 0.05 BTC per month; lender must not use this lease for qualifying rental income.
Conversion to investment: Borrower presents USD lease plus Form 1007; acceptable if other requirements met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Subject Rental Income Requires Form 1007 or 1025
business_rules.description: If the borrower has a history of receiving rental income from the subject property and the transaction is a refinance, the lender must obtain the applicable rent schedule (Form 1007 for one-unit or Form 1025 for two- to four-unit), plus either the borrower’s most recent year of signed federal income tax returns (including Schedule 1 and Schedule E) or current lease agreement(s) only when the borrower documents a qualifying exception.
business_rules.conditions: Subject property rental income used; borrower has rental history; refinance
business_rules.consequences: If docs obtained, rental income may be evaluated; if missing, rental income cannot be used
business_rules.exceptions: Lease may substitute for returns only with documented qualifying exception

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01 (Documenting Rental Income from Subject Property table)
source_reference.source_text: Yes Reﬁnance Form 1007 or Form 1025, as applicable, and either • the borrower’s most recent year of signed federal income tax returns, including Schedule 1 and Schedule E, or • copies of the current lease agreement(s) if the borrower can document a qualifying exception (see Reconciling Partial or No Rental History on Tax Returns  below).

examples: Refinance 1-unit: Lender gets Form 1007 and 2024 signed 1040 with Schedule E; rental income can be considered.
Refinance 3-unit: Lender gets Form 1025 and leases only, and documents qualifying exception (e.g., significant interruption); acceptable per guide.
Refinance: Lender provides neither Schedule E nor exception-supported lease; rental income must not be used to qualify.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Non-Subject Rental Income Requires Schedule E or 8825
business_rules.description: When the borrower owns rental property other than the subject property, the lender must document monthly gross and net rental income using the borrower’s most recent signed federal income tax return including Schedule 1 and Schedule E, and/or the most recent signed federal business income tax return for a partnership or S corporation including IRS Form 8825 (when only rental income is reported on the K-1); both are required when rental properties are reported on both personal and business returns.
business_rules.conditions: Borrower owns and rents property other than the subject property
business_rules.consequences: If required returns obtained, rental income may be used; if not, rental income not validated
business_rules.exceptions: Lease may substitute for Schedule E only if qualifying exception documented

source_reference.section_id: B3-3.8-01 (Documenting Rental Income from Property Other Than the Subject Property)
source_reference.source_text: When the borrower owns property – other than the subject property – that is rented, the lender must document the monthly gross (and net) rental income with the borrower’s most recent signed federal income tax return that includes Schedule 1 and Schedule E, the most recent signed federal business income tax return for a partnership or S corporation, that includes IRS Form 8825 (when only rental income is reported on the K-1), or both of the above, when rental properties are reported on both personal and business returns.

examples: Borrower owns a duplex rental (not subject): lender obtains signed 1040 with Schedule E; acceptable documentation basis.
Borrower owns rentals in an S-corp: lender obtains business return including Form 8825; acceptable documentation basis.
Borrower has rentals on both 1040 Schedule E and business return Form 8825: lender must obtain both returns.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for purchase / potential repurchase demand

business_rules.rule_name: Income with expiration must continue 3 years from note date
business_rules.description: When a qualifying income source has a defined expiration date or depends on depletion of an asset account/limited benefit, the lender must document that the income is expected to continue for at least three years from the note date. If an asset account is the sole or majority source of qualifying income, the lender must also assess the borrower’s ability to repay after the income ends or asset is depleted prior to loan maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Qualifying income has defined expiration or asset depletion/limited benefit
business_rules.consequences: If documented ≥3 years, income may be used; if not, income cannot qualify loan
business_rules.exceptions: If no defined expiration and required history is documented, no extra docs needed

source_reference.source_text: If the income source does have a deﬁned expiration date or is dependent on the depletion of an asset account or other limited beneﬁt, the lender must document that the income is expected to continue for at least three years from the note date. Additionally, when an asset account is the sole or majority source of qualifying income, the lender must assess the borrower’s ability to continue repaying the loan once the income source expires or the asset is depleted prior to loan maturity.

examples: Borrower receives contract income ending 24 months after note date; lender cannot use it unless documentation supports continuation ≥3 years from note date.
Borrower qualifies using asset depletion as majority income; lender must underwrite post-depletion repayment capacity before approving.
Borrower has ongoing salary with no stated end date and documented history per income type; lender is not expected to obtain extra continuance documentation absent contrary info.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Credit card financing prohibited for any down payment
business_rules.description: Borrowers may not use credit card financing for the down payment under any circumstances. Credit card financing may only be used for certain early fees (when permitted elsewhere) but never for down payment funds-to-close.
business_rules.conditions: When any portion of down payment is sourced from credit card charges
business_rules.consequences: If used, down payment source is unacceptable; loan must be restructured or denied
business_rules.exceptions: None

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae permits certain costs that must be paid early in the application process, such as lock-in fees, origination fees, commitment fees, credit report fees, and appraisal fees, to be charged to the borrower’s credit card because these fees do not represent extraordinary amounts and the credit card debt is considered in the borrower’s total monthly debt-to-income ratio. Borrowers are not required to pay oﬀ these credit card changes before closing. Under no circumstances may credit card ﬁnancing be used for the down payment.

examples: Purchase loan: borrower charges $8,000 down payment to a credit card cash-advance; lender must disallow and require acceptable verified funds.
Borrower charges $600 appraisal fee to a credit card (allowed early fee) but attempts to treat it as down payment; lender must reclassify and ensure down payment is not credit-funded.
Borrower proposes using a new credit card promotional balance transfer to cover down payment gap; lender must treat as prohibited down payment source.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: Reward points must convert to cash before closing
business_rules.description: Credit card reward points are acceptable for closing costs, down payment, and financial reserves only if converted to cash prior to closing. If converted cash is deposited into the borrower’s depository account, no additional documentation is required unless the deposit is a large deposit; otherwise the lender must evidence availability and cash value prior to conversion and that conversion occurred prior to closing.
business_rules.conditions: When borrower uses credit card reward points as funds to close or reserves
business_rules.consequences: If not converted before closing or not documented, funds are ineligible and must be excluded
business_rules.exceptions: None (large deposit rules may add documentation)

source_reference.source_text: Fannie Mae permits credit card reward points as acceptable funds for use towards closing costs , down payment and ﬁnancial reserves, provided the reward points are converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan. The following requirements apply: If the credit card reward points are converted to cash and deposited into the borrower’s depository account (for example, checking or savings), no additional documentation is required unless the deposit is considered a large deposit. In this event, the lender must follow the requirements in Evaluating Large Deposits in B3-4.2-02, Depository Accounts . If the credit card reward points are converted to cash, but not deposited into a borrower’s depository account, the lender must provide evidence the reward points were available to the borrower prior to the conversion, including veriﬁcation of the cash value (for example, credit card reward statement prior to conversion); and converted to cash prior to the closing of the loan.

examples: Borrower converts 120,000 points to $1,200 and deposits to checking 40 days before closing; lender accepts without extra documentation unless deposit meets 'large deposit' definition.
Borrower converts points to a prepaid card used at closing; lender must retain reward statement showing cash value and proof conversion occurred before closing.
Borrower plans to convert points at closing table; lender must require conversion prior to closing or exclude funds from assets/funds-to-close.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible for delivery; potential repurchase demand if delivered

examples: Loan application submitted for purchase of a primary residence
Loan application submitted for refinance requiring updated appraisal
Loan application underwritten through Desktop Underwriter (DU)

business_rules.rule_name: ULDD data errors in 5 or more loans trigger life-of-loan remedy
business_rules.description: Even if a loan obtained enforcement relief, the lender remains responsible for life-of-loan data accuracy. A remedy may be enforced when delivery data (ULDD) inaccuracies (i) affect five or more loans, (ii) involve the same delivery data element(s), (iii) differ from the information documented in the lender’s mortgage loan files, and (iv) are “significant” such that the loan would have been ineligible for delivery under the contract terms in effect at delivery, or would have been eligible but under different terms (which may result in repricing instead of repurchase).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to delivered loans with a pattern of ULDD inaccuracies across ≥5 loans.
business_rules.consequences: Significant inaccuracies can result in repurchase demand or loan repricing.
business_rules.exceptions: No pattern (fewer than 5 loans) fails this test; fraud has separate standard.

source_reference.source_text:
Even if a mortgage loan has met the requirements for enforcement relief set forth in A2-3.2-02, Enforcement
Relief for Breaches of Certain Representations and Warranties Related to Underwriting and Eligibility , the lender
remains responsible throughout the life of the loan for representations and warranties related to data accuracy
as set forth below. In connection with mortgage loans delivered to Fannie Mae, there must not be delivery data
(Uniform Loan Delivery Dataset) inaccuracies pertaining to the borrower, the property, or the project, if and to
the extent
the data inaccuracies aﬀect ﬁve or more loans and involve the same delivery data element(s);
such delivery data diﬀer from the information documented in the lender's mortgage loan ﬁles; and
the data inaccuracies are “signiﬁcant,” in that, using the information of the loan ﬁle to qualify the
borrower, property, and project,
the loan would not have been eligible for delivery under the terms of the lender’s contracts with
Fannie Mae in eﬀect at the time of delivery of the loan; or
the loan would have been eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, but under diﬀerent terms.

examples: 5 loans delivered with incorrect occupancy type in ULDD vs file; corrected data makes 2 loans ineligible: Fannie may enforce repurchase for those loans.
8 loans delivered with overstated credit scores in ULDD vs file; corrected data changes terms but remains eligible: Fannie may re-price instead of repurchase.
4 loans have the same ULDD data element error: does not meet the ≥5-loan threshold for this pattern-based test.
business_rules.extraction_notes: Explicit multi-part threshold test; consequence references repurchase/repricing framework.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan repurchase demand

examples: Representation that underwriting complied with eligibility and documentation requirements
Warranty that loan data delivered matches the mortgage file
Limited waiver applied for DU-underwritten loans under specified conditions

business_rules.rule_name: Manual files must retain Form 1008; DU files retain final report
business_rules.description: For manually underwritten mortgage loans, Form 1008 (or a similar document) must be retained in the mortgage file. For loans underwritten with DU, the final DU Underwriting Analysis Report must be retained in the mortgage file. For DU loans, retaining Form 1008 is optional (may, but not required).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting method is manual or Desktop Underwriter (DU)
business_rules.consequences: Retention supports audit/QC; missing required item causes file deficiency
business_rules.exceptions: For DU loans, Form 1008 retention is optional (not required)

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-02, Uniform Underwriting and Transmittal Summary and DU Underwriting Analysis Report
source_reference.source_text: The Uniform Underwriting and Transmittal Summary (Form 1008) summarizes key data from the loan application package... Form 1008 (or a similar document) must be retained in the mortgage file for manually underwritten mortgage loans. Lenders may, but are not required to, retain Form 1008 for loans underwritten with DU. For loans underwritten with DU, the final DU Underwriting Analysis Report must be retained in the mortgage file.

examples: Manual underwrite: lender places a completed Form 1008 in the closed loan file to meet the retention requirement.
DU underwrite: lender retains the final DU Underwriting Analysis Report in the file; Form 1008 is absent and still compliant.
DU underwrite: if the final DU report is missing from the file, QC flags a documentation defect requiring remediation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC finding; remediation or repurchase risk if defects are material

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Verifications must be requested from institution and returned direct
business_rules.description: The lender must attach a copy of the borrower-signed Blanket Authorization form to each Form 1005 or Form 1006 sent to a verifying institution. The information must be requested directly from the institution. The completed verification form(s) must be signed and dated and must be sent directly from the verifying institution.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using Form 1005/1006 to verify employment/income/assets
business_rules.consequences: Direct-source verifications are acceptable; indirect/undated forms are invalid
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.source_text: The lender attaches a copy of the Blanket Authorization form to each Form 1005 or Form 1006 sent to a verifying institution. The information must be requested directly from the institution. The completed form(s) must be signed and dated, and must be sent directly from the verifying institution.

examples: VOE: lender emails Form 1005 to employer HR with the blanket authorization attached; HR returns the signed/dated VOE directly to the lender.
Asset verification: bank returns a completed VOD directly to the lender; the lender does not accept a borrower-forwarded copy.
If the verification is missing a signature/date or was routed through the borrower, the lender must re-verify using direct-source methods.
business_rules.enforcement_action: QC finding; underwriting condition to re-verify documentation

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Credit documents must be no more than 4 months old on note date
business_rules.description: For all mortgage loans (existing and new construction), credit documents (including credit reports and employment, income, and asset documentation) must be no more than four months old on the note date. If consecutive documents exist in the file, the most recent document is used to determine compliance with the age requirement (e.g., most recent bank statement date controls when two consecutive monthly statements are used).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all credit reports and income/asset/employment docs for the loan
business_rules.consequences: If within 4 months, age requirement met; if older, documents are stale
business_rules.exceptions: Disaster-impact exceptions referenced elsewhere (B2-3-05)

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents
source_reference.source_text: Credit documents include credit reports and employment, income, and asset documentation. For all mortgage loans (existing and new construction), the credit documents must be no more than four months old on the note date. When consecutive credit documents are in the loan file, the most recent document is used to determine whether it meets the age requirement. For example, when two consecutive monthly bank statements are used to verify a depository asset, the date of the most recent statement must be no more than four months old on the note date.

examples: Note date 2026-06-30: most recent paystub dated 2026-04-15 (76 days old) is acceptable under the 4-month rule.
Two bank statements dated 2026-02-28 and 2026-03-31: lender uses 2026-03-31 to test the 4-month requirement.
Note date 2026-06-30 with latest bank statement dated 2026-01-31: statement is older than 4 months and triggers an update requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential delivery/QC defect if uncorrected

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Credit documents older than 4 months must be updated
business_rules.description: If credit documents are older than allowed under the four-month age requirement, the lender must update the credit documents before proceeding.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when any required credit document exceeds 4 months on note date
business_rules.consequences: Updated docs restore eligibility; failure leaves file noncompliant/stale
business_rules.exceptions: Disaster-impact exceptions referenced elsewhere (B2-3-05)

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents
source_reference.source_text: If the credit documents are older than allowed, the lender must update them. For age requirements related to appraisals, see B4-1.2-04, Appraisal Age and Use Requirements. Also see B2-3-05, Properties Affected by a Disaster for exceptions to the allowable age of credit documents for loans impacted by a natural disaster.

examples: Credit report is 140 days old at note date; lender re-pulls a new credit report prior to closing.
Most recent bank statement is 5 months old; lender obtains a more recent statement or alternative current verification.
Loan impacted by a declared disaster: lender applies the separate disaster exception guidance instead of standard updating.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential post-purchase defect if not updated

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Tax return required Oct 15 to Apr 14; Form 4868 not permitted
business_rules.description: For loans with an application date and disbursement date falling within the October 15 (current year minus 1) through April 14 (current year) window, the most recent year’s tax return is required and the use of a tax extension (IRS Form 4868) is not permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to income types requiring federal tax returns; date window as stated
business_rules.consequences: If return obtained, documentation satisfied; if not, loan file is deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for this date window

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Federal Income Tax Returns
source_reference.source_text: October 15, [current year minus 1] to April 14, current year... The most recent year’s tax return is required. The use of a Tax Extension (IRS Form 4868) is not permitted.

examples: Application 2025-12-01 and disbursement 2026-03-15: lender must obtain the most recent year’s tax return; borrower cannot rely on Form 4868.
Application 2025-10-20 and disbursement 2026-04-10: most recent return is required; file is incomplete if only prior-year returns are provided.
Borrower provides Form 4868 instead of the most recent return in this window: lender must obtain the required return to proceed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential post-purchase documentation defect

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: If tax return not obtained Apr 15 to Jun 30, signed 4506-C required
business_rules.description: For application dates April 15 through June 30 of the current year, the most recent year’s tax return is recommended but previous year(s) are acceptable. If the most recent year’s tax return is not obtained, the loan file must include a completed and signed IRS Form 4506-C for transcripts of tax returns provided by the borrower to the lender.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when tax returns are required and application date is Apr 15–Jun 30
business_rules.consequences: With 4506-C, file meets alternative documentation; without it, file deficient
business_rules.exceptions: None stated

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Federal Income Tax Returns
source_reference.source_text: April 15, current year to June 30, current year The most recent year’s tax return is recommended; however, the previous year(s) is also acceptable. In the event the most recent year's tax return is not obtained, the loan file must include a completed and signed IRS Form 4506-C for transcripts of tax returns provided by the borrower to the lender.

examples: Application 2026-05-20: lender uses 2024 and 2023 returns; since 2025 is not obtained, lender includes a signed 4506-C in the file.
Application 2026-06-15: borrower provides 2025 return; 4506-C is not required by this specific fallback condition.
Application 2026-04-20: lender fails to obtain 2025 return and omits 4506-C; loan file is incomplete and must be corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Underwriting condition; potential post-purchase documentation defect

examples: Mortgage file retained with appraisal, DU findings, and closing package
Mortgage file requested for examination during Fannie Mae review
Mortgage file missing required exhibit leading to defect findings

business_rules.rule_name: Jul 1 to Oct 14 missing return requires 4868 proof and IRS no-transcript
business_rules.description: For application dates July 1 through October 14 of the current year, the most recent year’s tax return is recommended but previous year(s) are acceptable. If the most recent year’s tax return is not obtained, the lender must: (1) obtain from the borrower a copy of IRS Form 4868, proof of e-filing Form 4868, or confirmation of electronic estimated tax payment(s); (2) review and compare the total tax liability reported/paid against the most recent year obtained to assess income stability; and (3) obtain an IRS response from filing IRS Form 4506-C confirming no transcripts are available for the applicable tax year (or, at lender discretion, borrower-provided evidence obtained directly from the IRS website).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when app date is Jul 1–Oct 14 and most recent return not obtained
business_rules.consequences: If all steps completed, alternative documentation satisfied; else file deficient
business_rules.exceptions: Alternative IRS no-transcript evidence allowed if obtained directly from IRS site

source_reference.section_id: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Federal Income Tax Returns
source_reference.source_text: July 1, current year to October 14, current year... In the event the most recent year's tax return is not obtained, the lender must perform all of the following: Obtain one of the following documents... copy of IRS Form 4868... proof of the e-filing of Form 4868, or confirmation of electronic payment(s)... Review the total tax liability... and compare it with the borrower’s tax liability from the most recent year obtained...
